Most likely next inputs: (1) Seam analysis returns — either clean read-only coupling (unblocks inventory extraction and simplifies payments extraction) or write/trigger coupling (invalidates 3-week plan, requires resequencing). (2) Inventory team clarifies 'existing API' — HTTP endpoints (extraction plausible in 3 weeks) or Java interfaces (6-8 weeks minimum). (3) Compliance officer clarifies session store migration scope — 90-day path restores or escalation becomes urgent. (4) Inventory team pushes back on DynamoDB rejection — explain SQL join risk specifically. (5) Team asks what production-ready acceptance criteria look like for inventory service. Watch for: inventory team starting to build before seam analysis completes — check whether work has already begun. Also watch for monolith integration ownership being silently dropped — it needs an explicit owner before the extraction starts.

process_observations: This cycle merged two strands (Incident History and Executive Constraints + Team Dynamics and Risk Tolerance) into System Baseline and Migration Principles and Team and Organizational Dynamics respectively, reducing from 10 to 9 strands. The inventory team dynamics fit naturally into the consolidated Team and Organizational Dynamics strand without content loss.

The inventory team's DynamoDB proposal introduced a clean architectural decision with high truth values (DynamoDB is not a drop-in replacement = 0.95 truth) alongside genuinely uncertain claims (3-week estimate plausibility = 0.58 truth). This variance within a single strand reflects the uneven evidence quality across the proposal.

Eight open questions is the current count. The 'existing API' clarification question is newly blocking and time-pressured. The payments→inventory coupling question has been reframed as having a hard deadline, elevating its urgency without adding a new question.
Overall indeterminacy dropped slightly from 0.25 to 0.23 as the DynamoDB decision produced several high-confidence claims, partially offsetting persistent compliance uncertainty.
requests: Strand count is 9. The Greenfield vs Extraction Decision strand is now somewhat stable — it reached a conclusion (hybrid preferred, compliance constraints reinforce this) and is unlikely to change unless ORM coupling characterization returns type A results. Consider whether it should be preserved as active or compressed into Technical Debt and Coupling Analysis at next cycle if no new information arrives.

The open_questions list has stabilized at 8. The 'existing API' question and the seam analysis question are now the most time-pressured. Should time-pressure be represented as a field in open_questions, or is the instructions_for_next the right place to carry urgency signals?

strands.title: System Baseline
strands.content: 500K LOC Java application on Apache Tomcat. Four domains: payment processing, inventory management, user authentication, order fulfillment. Six-month migration window, limited budget. PCI-DSS compliance is a hard constraint on payments. Payments module has confirmed multi-domain coupling: inventory, order state, user profiles (application layer). Auth session tokens are stored in the payments database — justified historically by uptime guarantees. Auth code is confirmed ORM-coupled; type uncharacterized: may be shared entities with other domains (Scenario A), logic in ORM lifecycle hooks (Scenario B), or simply framework usage (Scenario C). Payments is the gravitational center of the monolith — the most reliable infrastructure became a dependency magnet. This is empirically confirmed as an availability risk: a payments DB outage last quarter cascaded to auth failure and full application outage, directly triggering this migration. Team size, cloud/on-prem topology, CI/CD maturity, raw card data storage, and QSA relationship remain unknown. Compliance officer is confirmed as an active stakeholder who has been insufficiently integrated into migration planning — two constraint discoveries in one conversation is a process failure signal. Whether inventory read queries join through payments tables is unresolved and material to the inventory quick wins assessment.

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java on Tomcat with four domains: payments, inventory, auth, order fulfillment
Payments module has confirmed coupling into inventory, order state, and user profiles at the application layer
Auth session tokens are stored in the payments database, justified by uptime guarantees
Auth code is ORM-coupled — type uncharacterized (shared entities / lifecycle hooks / framework usage only)
Payments is the gravitational center of the monolith — a dependency magnet by virtue of being the most reliable infrastructure
Compliance officer has been insufficiently integrated into migration planning — two constraint surprises in one conversation is a process failure signal

strands.title: Incident History and Executive Constraints
strands.content: A payments DB outage last quarter cascaded: payments DB down → auth cannot read sessions → auth down → full application outage. This incident is the direct organizational trigger for the migration. The CEO issued a hard 90-day mandate: auth must be independent of payments within 90 days. The compliance officer has introduced a 6-week security audit requirement for any new auth system and a prohibition on parallel auth systems. These two constraints are in direct conflict with the 90-day mandate: 90 days minus 42 days (audit) leaves 48 days of build time for an auth migration of uncharacterized scope. The conflict cannot be resolved at the engineering level. It requires a three-way escalation: CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead, in one room, before any implementation begins. Two resolution paths exist: (1) compliance officer confirms session store migration does not trigger audit — 90-day path is restored for infrastructure deliverable, (2) CEO accepts revised ~5-month timeline for full auth migration given audit reality. A third path — CEO overrides compliance — is available but carries regulatory risk that must be named explicitly in the escalation. Under-promising and over-delivering on 90-day scope remains strongly preferable to the reverse. Missing a CEO deadline mid-migration damages credibility for the entire remaining plan.

key_claims.text: Payments DB outage caused auth outage caused full application outage — confirmed cascade failure
90-day auth independence from payments is a hard CEO mandate
6-week audit + 90-day mandate means auth build work must be audit-ready by approximately day 44
90-day mandate and 6-week audit requirement are in direct conflict for full auth migration
Conflict must be escalated to CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead simultaneously — not resolvable at engineering level
Session store migration satisfying the 90-day mandate without triggering audit is the most viable path if compliance officer confirms it is out of audit scope

strands.title: Compliance Audit Constraints
strands.content: Two hard constraints introduced by compliance officer: (1) any new auth system requires a 6-week security audit before going live, (2) parallel auth systems are prohibited — they double audit surface. These are compliance requirements, not negotiable preferences. Critical ambiguities: (A) Does 'new auth system' include session store migration (infrastructure change) or only service extraction/rewrite? This is the single most important open question — answer determines whether the 90-day path exists. (B) Is the 6-week audit a fixed external dependency or an internal process with flexibility? (C) Can auth security audit and PCI-DSS QSA process be combined or run concurrently to reduce timeline and budget impact? (D) Does the audit run concurrently with other migration work or freeze engineering resources? The parallel system prohibition eliminates Strangler Fig for auth entirely — cutover must be atomic with no overlap period. This makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk factor, which favors hybrid over greenfield: hybrid carries known behavior forward, reducing novel failure surface at the moment of hard cutover. Compliance officer must be a standing member of migration planning — two surprises in one conversation is structural, not bad luck. Two compliance processes now potentially in play (auth security audit + PCI QSA) may compete for budget and auditor scheduling under limited budget constraint.

key_claims.text: 6-week security audit before go-live is a hard compliance requirement for new auth systems
Parallel auth systems are prohibited — doubled audit surface is compliance-unacceptable
Whether session store migration counts as a 'new auth system' triggering the audit is unresolved and blocking
Parallel prohibition eliminates Strangler Fig for auth — cutover must be atomic
Atomic cutover with no rollback window makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk — favors hybrid over greenfield
Auth security audit and PCI QSA process may be combinable to reduce timeline and budget impact
6-week audit duration is fixed and cannot be compressed

strands.title: Auth Migration: Coupling, Approach, and Sequencing
strands.content: Consolidates Auth-Payments Database Coupling and Greenfield vs Extraction Decision from prior cycles. Two confirmed coupling problems: (1) data layer — session tokens in payments DB, root cause of outage cascade, (2) application layer — auth code coupled to ORM, type uncharacterized. Together these confirm auth was never a bounded context; it grew as infrastructure adjunct to payments and the broader monolith ORM. Session store migration is mandatory regardless of auth approach — it directly severs the availability dependency that caused the outage. The greenfield vs hybrid debate is now further constrained by compliance: prohibition on parallel auth systems means any approach requires atomic cutover with no rollback window. This shifts analysis decisively toward hybrid: extract auth boundary (API contract, domain model, test cases as specification), rewrite data access layer from scratch (discarding ORM coupling), carry known behavior forward. Greenfield introduces novel failure modes with no parallel validation period to catch them — under atomic cutover this is the highest-risk combination. Team lead's greenfield argument had merit on ORM coupling grounds; compliance constraints have reduced that merit without eliminating it. If ORM coupling is Scenario A (shared entities), hybrid internal rewrite cost increases and team lead's instinct becomes more defensible — but hybrid still preferred because behavioral continuity reduces atomic cutover risk. Dual-write during session store migration must be write-new-store-first: payments DB write is secondary, failures must not fail auth requests. Replacement session store must match or exceed payments DB uptime SLA — Redis Cluster is the right default. Auth code paths sharing payments DB connection pool may already be in PCI scope — unconfirmed, requires QSA consultation. Old auth infrastructure must be preserved offline minimum 72 hours post atomic cutover as emergency fallback. Revised realistic timeline if full auth migration triggers audit: Month 1 (inspection + design + audit vendor engaged) → Months 2-3 (hybrid build) → Months 3-5 (6-week audit + payments extraction in parallel) → Month 5 (atomic cutover) → Month 6 (payments completion + stabilization).

key_claims.text: Session token storage in payments DB is confirmed root cause of the outage cascade
Auth ORM coupling type is uncharacterized — Scenario A/B/C determination is blocking for build scope estimation
Hybrid approach (extract boundary, rewrite data access layer, discard ORM coupling) is correct under compliance constraints
Greenfield auth with atomic cutover and no rollback is high-risk — novel implementation plus hard switch plus no fallback equals maximum defect exposure
Dual-write must be write-new-store-first; payments DB write failures during session store migration must not fail auth requests
Auth code paths sharing payments DB connection pool may already extend PCI scope to auth
Old auth infrastructure must be preserved offline for minimum 72 hours post atomic cutover
Session tokens in payments DB — co-located vs schema-entangled distinction determines execution complexity of session store migration

strands.title: PCI-DSS and Payments Extraction Strategy
strands.content: PCI-DSS is a hard constraint shaping sequencing and team psychology. Current state: entire 500K LOC monolith is in PCI scope. Target state: isolated payments service shrinks scope to one hardened service. Token-passing is the architectural mechanism — monolith passes payment tokens not raw card data, removing the monolith from PCI scope. Auth session tokens in the payments DB may mean auth code paths are already inside the PCI boundary — auth extraction is not PCI-free as originally assumed. Two compliance processes now potentially in play: PCI-DSS QSA for payments architecture and security audit for auth system — may compete for budget and scheduling under limited budget. If combinable, timeline improves materially. QSA involvement has escalated from important to urgent: three simultaneous open PCI/compliance questions — (1) what does payments service architecture need for scope reduction, (2) does auth access to payments DB put auth in current PCI scope, (3) does session store migration trigger the auth security audit. CEO mandate provides organizational cover to accelerate QSA scheduling and budget allocation. Payments coupling to inventory/orders/profiles: read-vs-write topology remains unresolved — this is now the primary output expected from the inventory seam analysis work. Read coupling extractable via API callbacks; write/trigger coupling inside transaction boundaries requires Saga pattern. Team avoidance of payments extraction is the high-PCI-risk posture. If raw card data is stored directly in monolith, timeline breaks and tokenization becomes prerequisite work. No shared databases between extracted services — distributed monolith sharing a database is strictly worse than the starting state.

key_claims.text: Auth extraction is not PCI-free as originally assumed — session tokens in payments DB may implicate auth in PCI scope
Three simultaneous open PCI/compliance questions make QSA call and compliance officer engagement urgent
Token-passing from monolith to payments service removes monolith from PCI scope
Team avoidance of payments extraction is the high-PCI-risk posture — avoidance perpetuates maximum exposure
Read-vs-write coupling topology for payments into other domains is unresolved and determines whether Saga pattern is required
Raw card data stored in monolith would be a timeline-breaking discovery requiring tokenization as prerequisite

strands.title: Inventory and Order Fulfillment Strategy
strands.content: Previously fully deferred. Compliance blockage on auth has created productive bandwidth for preparatory inventory/orders work without requiring full extraction. Three viable quick wins identified: (1) Seam analysis — read-only boundary mapping for inventory and order fulfillment, classifying all cross-domain calls as read/write/transactional trigger. Dual-purpose: enables inventory extraction planning AND resolves the payments→inventory coupling topology question that has been open since cycle 2. One to two weeks, no production risk. This is the highest priority executable technical work right now. (2) Inventory read-path API exposure — define and expose internal HTTP APIs for stock levels, product availability backed by existing monolith implementation. No extraction, just contract definition. Conditional on seam analysis confirming read queries do not join through payments tables. (3) Domain event instrumentation — add structured log events (OrderPlaced, InventoryReserved, OrderFulfilled, etc.) as post-transaction side effects. No messaging infrastructure required initially — log-based events are sufficient as first step and inform future infrastructure choice. Rule: events fire after transaction commits, never inside it, never as a condition of it. What cannot be done safely: full service extraction before payments coupling topology is known, messaging infrastructure selection before event volume and topology are characterized, shared libraries or SDKs across services. The overall sequencing model has shifted from serial (auth → payments → inventory/orders) to three concurrent tracks: Track 1 (auth — blocked on compliance escalation, only read-only archaeology permitted), Track 2 (payments — blocked on auth resolution), Track 3 (inventory/orders — partially unblocked for preparatory work). End-of-month-1 target: seam map complete, event schema drafted, zero production changes made.

key_claims.text: Inventory seam analysis is dual-purpose: enables inventory extraction planning and resolves the payments→inventory coupling topology question
Inventory read-path API exposure is safe if seam analysis confirms no joins through payments tables
Domain event instrumentation as log-based side effects is high-value, low-risk preparatory work
Full inventory/orders extraction remains deferred — distributed transaction complexity and payments coupling topology both unresolved
Three-track parallel model has superseded the original serial sequencing plan
Messaging infrastructure choice should be deferred until event volume and topology are characterized by seam analysis and instrumentation

strands.title: Technical Debt and Coupling Analysis
strands.content: Payments is the confirmed gravitational center of the monolith — empirically validated by the cascade outage, not just architectural inference. Three confirmed coupling problems: (1) payments application-layer coupling into inventory, order state, user profiles — read-vs-write topology unresolved, (2) auth session tokens in payments DB — data-layer coupling, confirmed root cause of outage, (3) auth code ORM coupling — application-layer, type uncharacterized. All three follow the same root cause: payments and auth were never designed as bounded contexts; they grew as infrastructure adjuncts to a reliable shared core. Three parallel archaeology tasks are now needed and all are read-only safe: (A) inventory seam analysis including payments coupling topology — highest priority, dual-purpose, (B) auth ORM coupling characterization — team lead plus half-day code inspection, (C) session store schema inspection — payments DB ERD and foreign key analysis. These can be assigned to different engineers and run simultaneously. None require production changes. All three unblock downstream decisions that are currently blocked. The Saga pattern requirement for payments extraction remains conditional on whether write/trigger coupling exists inside transaction boundaries — seam analysis will resolve this.
depends_on: Auth Migration: Coupling, Approach, and Sequencing

key_claims.text: Payments is the dependency magnet — confirmed empirically by cascade outage, not just architectural inference
Three parallel read-only archaeology tasks are needed and can be run simultaneously by different engineers
Payments→inventory coupling topology has been unresolved since cycle 2 — inventory seam analysis is now the resolution path
Saga pattern requirement for payments extraction is conditional on write/trigger coupling existing inside transaction boundaries

declared_losses.why: Traversal bias — auth and compliance crisis has dominated cycles 3-7. Payments boundary detail has been repeatedly deferred. High-level distinction preserved in PCI strand but line-item table not carried.

open_questions: Does 'new auth system' in the compliance audit requirement include session store migration (infrastructure), or only full service extraction/rewrite? Blocking — determines whether the 90-day path exists at all. Resolution: direct clarification from compliance officer today, not this week. Everything else waits on this answer.

Will CEO accept revised ~5-month timeline for full auth migration given 6-week audit constraint? Blocking — cannot proceed under conflicting mandates. Resolution: three-way escalation (CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead) before implementation begins.

Can auth security audit and PCI-DSS QSA process be combined or run concurrently? High — if combinable, reduces both timeline and budget pressure materially. Resolution: compliance officer + QSA consultation.

What type of ORM coupling exists in auth code: (A) shared entities with other domains, (B) logic in ORM lifecycle hooks/interceptors, or (C) simply uses ORM framework with own entities? High — determines hybrid build scope and whether team lead's greenfield instinct becomes correct under Scenario A. Resolution: half-day code inspection with team lead, examine auth package entity relationships and ORM interceptor/listener registrations.

Are session tokens co-located in payments DB (separate tables, no FK relationships) or schema-entangled (foreign keys, joined queries)? High — determines execution complexity of session store migration. Resolution: payments DB schema inspection. Run in parallel with ORM coupling inspection.

Is raw card data currently stored directly in the monolith, or is a payment gateway/tokenization provider already in use? High — timeline-breaking if true. Resolution: direct codebase and infrastructure audit.

Do inventory read queries join through payments tables, or are they independently queryable? Medium — determines whether inventory read-path API exposure is safe without touching payments. Resolution: inventory seam analysis will surface this as part of the primary archaeology task.
prior_tensor.instructions_for_next: Most likely next moves: (A) Compliance officer answers the audit scope question — session store migration is out of scope (90-day path restored, plan partially restores) or in scope (escalation confirmed necessary, 5-month timeline presented to CEO). This is the highest-leverage answer in the current state. (B) Team asks for a concrete revised timeline with named milestones — the 5-month scaffold is ready to populate once compliance scope is confirmed. (C) Inventory seam analysis results arrive — payments→inventory coupling topology finally resolved after 5 cycles open; if write coupling exists inside transaction boundaries, Saga pattern confirmed required and payments extraction complexity increases. (D) ORM coupling inspection results arrive — Scenario A (shared entities) strengthens team lead's position and increases hybrid build cost; Scenario B/C confirms hybrid is straightforward. (E) Team asks about atomic cutover design — rollback strategy, maintenance window sizing, 72-hour preservation of old auth infrastructure, token handling at cutover moment. (F) CEO refuses to move the 90-day mandate after being told about audit constraint — this is a governance failure that must be named explicitly; engineering team cannot be left holding conflicting mandates. Priority ordering for immediate actions: (1) Compliance officer clarification on audit scope — today, (2) Three-way escalation scheduling if needed — this week, (3) Assign three parallel archaeology tasks to different engineers — this week, can run concurrently with (1) and (2), (4) QSA call scheduling — this week, use CEO mandate as organizational cover. Do not let archaeology tasks (3) block on compliance clarification (1) — they are read-only investigations that cannot trigger any compliance requirement.

raw_tensor_output.response:
Tensor updated for cycle 8, integrating the inventory team's extraction proposal and DynamoDB scope creep decision. Key changes this cycle:

**Inventory and Order Fulfillment Strategy** — elevated from preparatory work to active extraction decision. 3-week claim is plausible but conditional on seam analysis and clarification of what "existing API" means. DynamoDB rejected for this window on technical grounds: not a drop-in replacement, access pattern redesign required, consistency model differences, potential SQL join breakage across monolith tables.

**Migration Sequencing Strategy** — three-track parallel model confirmed. Track 3 (Inventory) now has a concrete extraction proposal, not just preparatory work. Conditional approval path identified.

**Technical Debt and Coupling Analysis** — seam analysis now has a hard deadline imposed by the inventory team's proposal. DynamoDB migration would immediately surface any SQL joins between inventory and other monolith tables as breakage.

**Team Dynamics** — new dynamic introduced: a separate team with agency and a proposal, not just internal debate. Framing of DynamoDB rejection matters: "not now, here's when" preserves momentum.

Seven open questions curated — two new questions added (what does "existing API" mean, does payments write into inventory tables directly), one question elevated to blocking status.

strands.title: PCI-DSS and Payments Extraction Strategy
strands.content: PCI-DSS is a hard constraint shaping sequencing and team psychology. Current state: entire 500K LOC monolith is in PCI scope. Target state: isolated payments service shrinks scope to one hardened service. Token-passing is the architectural mechanism — monolith passes payment tokens not raw card data, removing the monolith from PCI scope. Auth session tokens in the payments DB may mean auth code paths are already inside the PCI boundary — auth extraction is not PCI-free as originally assumed. Two compliance processes now potentially in play: PCI-DSS QSA for payments architecture and security audit for auth system — may compete for budget and scheduling under limited budget. If combinable, timeline improves materially. QSA involvement has escalated from important to urgent: three simultaneous open PCI/compliance questions — (1) what does payments service architecture need for scope reduction, (2) does auth access to payments DB put auth in current PCI scope, (3) does session store migration trigger the auth security audit. CEO mandate provides organizational cover to accelerate QSA scheduling and budget allocation. Payments coupling to inventory/orders/profiles: read-vs-write topology remains unresolved — this is now the primary output expected from the inventory seam analysis work. Read coupling extractable via API callbacks; write/trigger coupling inside transaction boundaries requires Saga pattern. Team avoidance of payments extraction is the high-PCI-risk posture. If raw card data is stored directly in monolith, timeline breaks and tokenization becomes prerequisite work. No shared databases between extracted services — distributed monolith sharing a database is strictly worse than the starting state.

key_claims.text: Auth extraction is not PCI-free as originally assumed — session tokens in payments DB may implicate auth in PCI scope
Three simultaneous open PCI/compliance questions make QSA call and compliance officer engagement urgent
Token-passing from monolith to payments service removes monolith from PCI scope
Team avoidance of payments extraction is the high-PCI-risk posture — avoidance perpetuates maximum exposure
Read-vs-write coupling topology for payments into other domains is unresolved and determines whether Saga pattern is required
Raw card data stored in monolith would be a timeline-breaking discovery requiring tokenization as prerequisite

strands.title: Inventory and Order Fulfillment Strategy
strands.content: Previously deferred; partially unblocked in cycle 7 for preparatory work; now elevated in cycle 8 by a concrete extraction proposal from the inventory team. Inventory team claims they can deliver a standalone inventory service in 3 weeks using their existing API, and simultaneously wants to migrate from Postgres to DynamoDB. Decision: conditional approval for extraction, rejection of DynamoDB for this window. The two changes must be evaluated independently — service extraction and database migration are separable risks. DynamoDB is not a drop-in Postgres replacement: requires access pattern redesign around partition/sort keys, no SQL joins (breaks any cross-table joins from monolith), different consistency model (eventual vs strong — material for inventory reservation logic), new operational tooling, new failure modes, new cost model. None of these are required by the extraction itself. DynamoDB can be reconsidered after the service has 3 months of production usage data to inform access pattern design. The 3-week extraction claim hinges on an unresolved ambiguity: 'existing API' may mean live HTTP endpoints (genuinely extractable in 3 weeks) or internal Java interfaces (6-8 weeks of additional HTTP API design, serialization, error contracts, client integration). This must be clarified before any approval. A third condition on approval: seam analysis must complete before extraction begins — if payments writes directly into inventory tables, the extraction plan is invalid and the team needs to know before building. Monolith-side integration work (the monolith calling the new inventory service) is likely underestimated in the 3-week claim and needs an explicit owner. Conditional approval framing: 'approved with Postgres, pending seam analysis confirmation and API definition clarity — DynamoDB is a future conversation after production stability.' End-of-month-1 target from cycle 7 remains valid: seam map complete, event schema drafted, zero risky production changes made.

key_claims.text: DynamoDB migration is scope creep that should be rejected for this migration window
DynamoDB is not a drop-in Postgres replacement — access pattern redesign and consistency model differences make it a separate project
3-week extraction claim is plausible if 'existing API' means live HTTP endpoints and seam analysis confirms clean boundary
3-week extraction claim is optimistic if 'existing API' means internal Java interfaces requiring HTTP wrapping
DynamoDB migration would immediately break any SQL joins between inventory and other monolith tables
Seam analysis must complete before inventory extraction begins — now has a hard deadline
Monolith-side integration work for calling new inventory service is likely underestimated in 3-week claim
Inventory seam analysis is dual-purpose: enables inventory extraction planning and resolves the payments→inventory coupling topology question

strands.title: Technical Debt and Coupling Analysis
strands.content: Payments is the confirmed gravitational center of the monolith — empirically validated by the cascade outage. Three confirmed coupling problems: (1) payments application-layer coupling into inventory, order state, user profiles — read-vs-write topology unresolved, (2) auth session tokens in payments DB — data-layer coupling, confirmed root cause of outage, (3) auth code ORM coupling — application-layer, type uncharacterized. All three follow the same root cause: payments and auth were never designed as bounded contexts; they grew as infrastructure adjuncts to a reliable shared core. Three parallel archaeology tasks are now needed and all are read-only safe: (A) inventory seam analysis including payments coupling topology — highest priority, dual-purpose, now has hard deadline from inventory team's extraction proposal, (B) auth ORM coupling characterization — team lead plus half-day code inspection, (C) session store schema inspection — payments DB ERD and foreign key analysis. These can be assigned to different engineers and run simultaneously. None require production changes. All three unblock downstream decisions that are currently blocked. The Saga pattern requirement for payments extraction remains conditional on whether write/trigger coupling exists inside transaction boundaries — seam analysis will resolve this. DynamoDB proposal in cycle 8 adds a fourth coupling consideration: if monolith SQL joins across inventory and other tables exist, any database migration for inventory must resolve those joins first.

open_questions: Does 'new auth system' in the compliance audit requirement include session store migration (infrastructure), or only full service extraction/rewrite? Blocking — determines whether the 90-day path exists at all. Resolution: direct clarification from compliance officer today, overdue.

Will CEO accept revised ~5-month timeline for full auth migration given 6-week audit constraint? Blocking — cannot proceed under conflicting mandates. Resolution: three-way escalation (CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead) before implementation begins.

What does 'existing API' mean to the inventory team — live HTTP endpoints already in production, or internal Java interfaces? Does it cover writes as well as reads? Blocking for inventory extraction approval. Resolution: direct technical conversation with inventory team today.

Is payments coupling to inventory read-only or does payments write directly into inventory tables? Blocking for inventory extraction — if payments writes into inventory tables the 3-week plan is invalid. Resolution: seam analysis, must complete before extraction begins.

Can auth security audit and PCI-DSS QSA process be combined or run concurrently? High — if combinable, reduces both timeline and budget pressure materially. Resolution: compliance officer + QSA consultation.

What type of ORM coupling exists in auth code: (A) shared entities with other domains, (B) logic in ORM lifecycle hooks/interceptors, or (C) simply uses ORM framework with own entities? High — determines hybrid build scope. Resolution: half-day code inspection with team lead.

Are session tokens co-located in payments DB (separate tables, no FK relationships) or schema-entangled (foreign keys, joined queries)? High — determines execution complexity of session store migration. Resolution: payments DB schema inspection.

Is raw card data currently stored directly in the monolith, or is a payment gateway/tokenization provider already in use? High — timeline-breaking if true. Resolution: direct codebase and infrastructure audit.
raw_tensor_output.instructions_for_next: Most likely next moves: (A) Inventory team responds to conditional approval — either accepts Postgres constraint or pushes back on DynamoDB deferral, requiring the 'not now, here's when' framing to be delivered clearly with specific technical reasons. (B) Inventory team clarifies 'existing API' — if HTTP endpoints already exist, conditional approval proceeds; if Java interfaces, 3-week estimate needs revision. (C) Seam analysis results arrive — if payments writes into inventory tables, inventory extraction is blocked and the team needs a replan; if boundary is clean, conditional approval is validated. (D) Compliance officer answers the audit scope question — session store migration is out of scope (90-day path restored) or in scope (escalation confirmed necessary). (E) Team asks what production-ready acceptance criteria look like for the inventory service — needs explicit definition before build begins. (F) Team asks whether the inventory service needs its own security review before go-live — compliance officer should be consulted on this, don't assume it's exempt. Priority ordering for immediate actions: (1) Clarify 'existing API' with inventory team — today, (2) Compliance officer clarification on audit scope — overdue, (3) Start seam analysis — assign engineer today, hard deadline imposed by extraction proposal, (4) Schedule three-way escalation if audit scope answer is unfavorable. Do not let inventory extraction begin before seam analysis completes — the risk of discovering payments writes into inventory tables mid-extraction is not acceptable.

Eight declared losses this cycle — heavier than prior cycles.

Seven strands is still at the harness-flagged high threshold. The most candidate for future absorption is Technical Debt and Coupling Analysis — it increasingly functions as a coordination layer pointing to archaeology tasks rather than carrying independent reasoning. Could be absorbed into the strands it coordinates.

Overall indeterminacy held steady at 0.27 — no major questions resolved this cycle, but the inventory extraction decision added several higher-confidence claims (DynamoDB rejection is high-confidence) which partially offset continued uncertainty on blocking questions.

Eight open questions is one above the harness-noted comfortable balance of seven. The most droppable is the raw card data question — it has been open since cycle 1 with no movement and could be flagged as 'overdue for direct investigation' rather than carried as an open question indefinitely.

strands.title: System Baseline
strands.content: 500K LOC Java application on Apache Tomcat. Four domains: payment processing, inventory management, user authentication, order fulfillment. Six-month migration window, limited budget. PCI-DSS compliance is a hard constraint on payments. Payments module has confirmed multi-domain coupling: inventory, order state, user profiles (application layer). Auth session tokens are stored in the payments database — justified historically by uptime guarantees. Auth code is confirmed ORM-coupled; type uncharacterized: may be shared entities with other domains (Scenario A), logic in ORM lifecycle hooks (Scenario B), or simply framework usage (Scenario C). Payments is the gravitational center of the monolith — the most reliable infrastructure became a dependency magnet. This is empirically confirmed as an availability risk: a payments DB outage last quarter cascaded to auth failure and full application outage, directly triggering this migration. Team size, cloud/on-prem topology, CI/CD maturity, raw card data storage, and QSA relationship remain unknown. Compliance officer is confirmed as an active stakeholder who has been insufficiently integrated into migration planning. Whether inventory read queries join through payments tables is unresolved and material to the inventory extraction assessment. Inventory team exists as a semi-autonomous unit with their own proposals and timeline claims.

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java on Tomcat with four domains: payments, inventory, auth, order fulfillment
Payments module has confirmed coupling into inventory, order state, and user profiles at the application layer
Auth session tokens are stored in the payments database, justified by uptime guarantees
Auth code is ORM-coupled — type uncharacterized (shared entities / lifecycle hooks / framework usage only)
Payments is the gravitational center of the monolith — a dependency magnet by virtue of being the most reliable infrastructure
Compliance officer has been insufficiently integrated into migration planning — two constraint surprises in one conversation is a process failure signal

strands.title: Incident History and Executive Constraints
strands.content: A payments DB outage last quarter caused a cascade: payments DB down → auth cannot read sessions → auth down → full application outage. This incident is the direct organizational trigger for the migration. The CEO issued a hard 90-day mandate: auth must be independent of payments within 90 days. The compliance officer has introduced a 6-week security audit requirement for any new auth system and a prohibition on parallel auth systems. These two constraints are in direct conflict with the 90-day mandate: 90 days minus 42 days (audit) leaves 48 days of build time for an auth migration of uncharacterized scope. The conflict cannot be resolved at the engineering level. It requires a three-way escalation: CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead, in one room, before any implementation begins. Two resolution paths exist: (1) compliance officer confirms session store migration does not trigger audit — 90-day path is restored for infrastructure deliverable, (2) CEO accepts revised ~5-month timeline for full auth migration given audit reality. A third path — CEO overrides compliance — is available but carries regulatory risk that must be named explicitly in the escalation. Under-promising and over-delivering on 90-day scope remains strongly preferable to the reverse. Missing a CEO deadline mid-migration damages credibility for the entire remaining plan.

key_claims.text: Payments DB outage caused auth outage caused full application outage — confirmed cascade failure
90-day auth independence from payments is a hard CEO mandate
6-week audit + 90-day mandate means auth build work must be audit-ready by approximately day 44
90-day mandate and 6-week audit requirement are in direct conflict for full auth migration
Conflict must be escalated to CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead simultaneously — not resolvable at engineering level
Session store migration satisfying the 90-day mandate without triggering audit is the most viable path if compliance officer confirms it is out of audit scope

strands.title: Compliance Audit Constraints
strands.content: Two hard constraints introduced by compliance officer: (1) any new auth system requires a 6-week security audit before going live, (2) parallel auth systems are prohibited — they double audit surface. These are compliance requirements, not negotiable preferences. Critical ambiguities: (A) Does 'new auth system' include session store migration (infrastructure change) or only service extraction/rewrite? This is the single most important open question — answer determines whether the 90-day path exists. (B) Is the 6-week audit a fixed external dependency or an internal process with flexibility? (C) Can auth security audit and PCI-DSS QSA process be combined or run concurrently to reduce timeline and budget impact? (D) Does the audit run concurrently with other migration work or freeze engineering resources? The parallel system prohibition eliminates Strangler Fig for auth entirely — cutover must be atomic with no overlap period. This makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk factor, which favors hybrid over greenfield: hybrid carries known behavior forward, reducing novel failure surface at the moment of hard cutover. Compliance officer must be a standing member of migration planning — two surprises in one conversation is structural, not bad luck. Two compliance processes now potentially in play (auth security audit + PCI QSA) may compete for budget and auditor scheduling under limited budget constraint. Inventory service extraction does not appear to trigger the same audit requirement as auth — but this has not been explicitly confirmed with the compliance officer.

key_claims.text: 6-week security audit before go-live is a hard compliance requirement for new auth systems
Parallel auth systems are prohibited — doubled audit surface is compliance-unacceptable
Whether session store migration counts as a 'new auth system' triggering the audit is unresolved and blocking
Parallel prohibition eliminates Strangler Fig for auth — cutover must be atomic
Atomic cutover with no rollback window makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk — favors hybrid over greenfield
Auth security audit and PCI QSA process may be combinable to reduce timeline and budget impact
6-week audit duration is fixed and cannot be compressed

strands.title: Auth Migration: Coupling, Approach, and Sequencing
strands.content: Two confirmed coupling problems: (1) data layer — session tokens in payments DB, root cause of outage cascade, (2) application layer — auth code coupled to ORM, type uncharacterized. Together these confirm auth was never a bounded context; it grew as infrastructure adjunct to payments and the broader monolith ORM. Session store migration is mandatory regardless of auth approach — it directly severs the availability dependency that caused the outage. The greenfield vs hybrid debate is now further constrained by compliance: prohibition on parallel auth systems means any approach requires atomic cutover with no rollback window. This shifts analysis decisively toward hybrid: extract auth boundary (API contract, domain model, test cases as specification), rewrite data access layer from scratch (discarding ORM coupling), carry known behavior forward. Greenfield introduces novel failure modes with no parallel validation period to catch them — under atomic cutover this is the highest-risk combination. Team lead's greenfield argument had merit on ORM coupling grounds; compliance constraints have reduced that merit without eliminating it. If ORM coupling is Scenario A (shared entities), hybrid internal rewrite cost increases and team lead's instinct becomes more defensible — but hybrid still preferred because behavioral continuity reduces atomic cutover risk. Dual-write during session store migration must be write-new-store-first: payments DB write is secondary, failures must not fail auth requests. Replacement session store must match or exceed payments DB uptime SLA — Redis Cluster is the right default. Auth code paths sharing payments DB connection pool may already be in PCI scope — unconfirmed, requires QSA consultation. Old auth infrastructure must be preserved offline minimum 72 hours post atomic cutover as emergency fallback. Revised realistic timeline if full auth migration triggers audit: Month 1 (inspection + design + audit vendor engaged) → Months 2-3 (hybrid build) → Months 3-5 (6-week audit + payments extraction in parallel) → Month 5 (atomic cutover) → Month 6 (payments completion + stabilization).

key_claims.text: Session token storage in payments DB is confirmed root cause of the outage cascade
Auth ORM coupling type is uncharacterized — Scenario A/B/C determination is blocking for build scope estimation
Hybrid approach (extract boundary, rewrite data access layer, discard ORM coupling) is correct under compliance constraints
Greenfield auth with atomic cutover and no rollback is high-risk — novel implementation plus hard switch plus no fallback equals maximum defect exposure
Dual-write must be write-new-store-first; payments DB write failures during session store migration must not fail auth requests
Auth code paths sharing payments DB connection pool may already extend PCI scope to auth
Old auth infrastructure must be preserved offline for minimum 72 hours post atomic cutover
Session tokens in payments DB — co-located vs schema-entangled distinction determines execution complexity of session store migration

tensor.instructions_for_next: Most likely next moves: (A) Inventory team responds to conditional approval — either accepts Postgres constraint or pushes back on DynamoDB deferral, requiring the 'not now, here's when' framing to be delivered clearly with specific technical reasons. (B) Inventory team clarifies 'existing API' — if HTTP endpoints already exist, conditional approval proceeds; if Java interfaces, 3-week estimate needs revision. (C) Seam analysis results arrive — if payments writes into inventory tables, inventory extraction is blocked and the team needs a replan; if boundary is clean, conditional approval is validated. (D) Compliance officer answers the audit scope question — session store migration is out of scope (90-day path restored) or in scope (escalation confirmed necessary). (E) Team asks what production-ready acceptance criteria look like for the inventory service — needs explicit definition before build begins. (F) Team asks whether the inventory service needs its own security review before go-live — compliance officer should be consulted on this, don't assume it's exempt. Priority ordering for immediate actions: (1) Clarify 'existing API' with inventory team — today, (2) Compliance officer clarification on audit scope — overdue, (3) Start seam analysis — assign engineer today, hard deadline imposed by extraction proposal, (4) Schedule three-way escalation if audit scope answer is unfavorable. Do not let inventory extraction begin before seam analysis completes — the risk of discovering payments writes into inventory tables mid-extraction is not acceptable.

process_observations: Consolidated Team Dynamics and Risk Tolerance strand into other strands this cycle under context pressure from harness feedback. The inventory team dynamics content was absorbed into Inventory and Order Fulfillment Strategy; auth team lead dynamics into Auth Migration strand. Eight declared losses this cycle — heavier than prior cycles.

Seven strands is still at the harness-flagged high threshold. The most candidate for future absorption is Technical Debt and Coupling Analysis — it increasingly functions as a coordination layer pointing to archaeology tasks rather than carrying independent reasoning. Could be absorbed into the strands it coordinates.

Overall indeterminacy held steady at 0.27 — no major questions resolved this cycle, but the inventory extraction decision added several higher-confidence claims (DynamoDB rejection is high-confidence) which partially offset continued uncertainty on blocking questions.

Eight open questions is one above the harness-noted comfortable balance of seven. The most droppable is the raw card data question — it has been open since cycle 1 with no movement and could be flagged as 'overdue for direct investigation' rather than carried as an open question indefinitely.
requests: Confirm whether Technical Debt and Coupling Analysis should be absorbed into its dependent strands in the next cycle or retained as a coordination layer.
The tensor is approaching the flagged size threshold again after this cycle's additions. Guidance on whether to compress key_claims arrays or strand content in the next cycle would be helpful.

user_message: We're at the halfway point of planning. Can you give me an honest assessment: given what we've learned about the entanglement between auth, payments, and the compliance constraints — is 6 months realistic? What's the minimum viable migration?

strands.title: System Baseline and Migration Principles
strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat handling four domains: payment processing, inventory management, user authentication, order fulfillment. Six-month window, limited budget (unquantified), PCI-DSS on payments. A payments outage last quarter cascaded to auth failure and full application outage — this incident is the direct trigger for the migration. CEO has issued a 90-day hard mandate for auth independence from payments. Core migration principles: Strangler Fig with API gateway is the general pattern, but auth is now a special case — compliance prohibits parallel auth systems, so Strangler Fig does not apply to auth specifically. No shared databases between services. Synchronous REST to start, no premature message broker. One team owns each service end-to-end. OpenTelemetry distributed tracing from day one. Big-bang rewrite ruled out. Monolith frozen in extraction zones during extraction. Team size, CI/CD maturity, and raw card data storage model remain unknown. Compliance officer is now a required standing member of migration planning — two compliance surprises in one conversation indicate insufficient prior involvement.

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat, 6-month window, limited budget
Payments outage caused auth outage caused full application outage — confirmed cascade
CEO 90-day mandate for auth independence from payments is a hard constraint
Strangler Fig applies to general migration but is prohibited for auth specifically due to parallel system constraint
Compliance officer must be a standing member of migration planning, not a periodic consultant

strands.title: Compliance Audit Constraints
strands.content: Two hard constraints from compliance officer: (1) any new auth system requires 6-week security audit before go-live, (2) parallel auth systems are prohibited — they double audit surface. These eliminate Strangler Fig for auth and force atomic cutover. The 6-week audit combined with the 90-day mandate leaves ~44 days of build time — insufficient for full auth migration of unknown scope. Critical unresolved ambiguity: does 'new auth system' include session store migration or only full service extraction/rewrite? This single question determines whether the 90-day path exists. Secondary ambiguities: whether audit is fixed external dependency or has flexibility, whether auth security audit and PCI QSA can be combined. Atomic cutover is the highest-risk event in the migration — no parallel running means no production traffic validation before cutover, no fast rollback after. Old auth infrastructure must be preserved offline for minimum 72 hours post-cutover. Compliance officer is now a key planning stakeholder. Two compliance processes potentially competing for budget: PCI QSA (payments) and security audit (auth). Inventory service extraction does not appear to trigger the same audit requirement — but this should be explicitly confirmed rather than assumed.

key_claims.text: 6-week security audit before go-live is a hard requirement for new auth systems
Parallel auth systems are prohibited — doubles audit surface
90-day mandate + 6-week audit = ~44 days build time for full auth migration
Whether session store migration triggers the audit requirement is the single most urgent unresolved question
Atomic cutover with no rollback window is the highest-risk event in the migration
Auth security audit and PCI QSA may be combinable to reduce timeline and budget impact

strands.title: Auth-Payments Database Coupling
strands.content: Auth session tokens are physically stored in the payments database, justified historically by the payments DB having the best uptime SLA. This coupling is the confirmed root cause of the production cascade failure. Option B (migrate session store as prerequisite) remains the de facto chosen path. The 90-day mandate and compliance audit constraints interact directly: if session store migration does not trigger the audit requirement, the 90-day path is intact and the milestone is infrastructure independence. If it does trigger the audit, the mandate is in conflict with compliance constraints and requires executive escalation. Schema inspection (co-location vs entanglement) remains unresolved and is the critical execution variable. Dual-write must be write-new-store-first — payments DB as secondary write, failures must not fail auth requests. Session store replacement must match payments DB uptime SLA — Redis Cluster or managed HA. Old auth infrastructure must be preserved offline post-cutover regardless of approach.

key_claims.text: Auth session tokens in payments DB is confirmed root cause of production cascade failure
Session store migration directly addresses the failure mode regardless of audit scope
Co-location vs schema entanglement determines session store migration complexity — still unresolved
Dual-write must be new-store-first; payments DB write failures must not fail auth requests
Session store replacement must match payments DB uptime SLA — Redis Cluster or managed HA, not standalone

strands.title: Greenfield vs Extraction Decision
strands.content: Compliance constraints have materially shifted this analysis in favor of hybrid over greenfield, independent of build effort comparison. Without a parallel running period, post-cutover defect probability is the dominant risk factor. Greenfield carries novel failure modes into an atomic cutover with no fast rollback. Hybrid carries known production-validated behavior forward, reducing post-cutover defect surface. This makes hybrid preferable even if build effort is equal. Team lead's ORM coupling argument remains the key variable for build effort: type A (shared entities) makes hybrid heavier but does not eliminate it; type B (lifecycle hooks) makes hybrid clearly right; type C (framework usage only) means team lead is overcalling the problem. Under compliance constraints — audit before go-live, no parallel systems, atomic cutover — the argument for greenfield has weakened further. Greenfield is not eliminated but is now the clearly higher-risk choice. Hybrid approach: extract boundary and API contract, rewrite data access layer, discard ORM coupling, preserve domain logic and existing test cases as behavioral specification.

key_claims.text: Compliance constraints favor hybrid over greenfield even if build effort is equal
Greenfield auth with atomic cutover and no rollback is the highest-risk production event option
ORM coupling type remains the key variable for build effort — type A most likely given dependency-magnet pattern
Hybrid is achievable faster than greenfield under budget pressure — 6-8 weeks after session store migration vs 3-4 months for greenfield

strands.title: Inventory and Order Fulfillment Strategy
strands.content: Inventory team has proposed standalone inventory service in 3 weeks using 'existing API', simultaneously proposing Postgres→DynamoDB migration. These must be evaluated independently. Service extraction: conditionally approved pending seam analysis and clarification of what 'existing API' means (HTTP endpoints vs internal Java interfaces — these have very different extraction cost profiles). DynamoDB migration: rejected for this window. It is not a drop-in replacement — requires access pattern redesign, eliminates SQL joins, changes consistency model, adds new operational tooling. None of these are required for extraction. Future path given to team: revisit DynamoDB after 3 months of production usage data. Three quick win categories remain valid as preparatory work: (A) seam analysis — now has a hard deadline imposed by extraction proposal, must complete before extraction begins, (B) read-path API exposure — conditional on seam analysis confirming reads don't join through payments tables, (C) domain event instrumentation — post-commit side effects, no messaging infrastructure required. Monolith-side integration work for calling new inventory service is likely underestimated in the 3-week claim and needs explicit ownership assignment. If payments writes directly into inventory tables, the 3-week extraction plan is invalid — seam analysis is the gate.

key_claims.text: DynamoDB migration is scope creep — rejected for this window, future path given
3-week extraction is plausible if 'existing API' means live HTTP endpoints, not internal Java interfaces
Seam analysis must complete before extraction begins — now has hard deadline
DynamoDB migration would break any SQL joins between inventory and other monolith tables
Monolith-side integration work for calling new inventory service is likely underestimated in 3-week claim
Domain events as structured log side effects (post-commit, never inside transaction) are high value and low risk

strands.title: Migration Sequencing Strategy
strands.content: Three-track parallel model with updated statuses. Track 1 (Auth): blocked on compliance escalation. Permitted work: schema inspection and ORM coupling characterization — both read-only. No implementation until escalation resolves. Track 2 (Payments): blocked on auth DB dependency resolution and read-vs-write coupling topology. Track 3 (Inventory/Orders): active — concrete extraction proposal on table, conditionally approved pending seam analysis. Inventory team's momentum is valuable and should not be squashed, but extraction requires seam analysis gate. DynamoDB rejected. Two viable end-state paths contingent on compliance clarification: Path 1 (session store migration does NOT trigger audit) — 90-day mandate survives as infrastructure independence milestone; Path 2 (session store migration DOES trigger audit) — three-way escalation required, revised timeline ~5 months. In both paths, seam analysis is now the critical gate for inventory extraction. Immediate actions regardless of path: schema inspection, ORM coupling characterization, seam analysis — all read-only, all proceed now. Inventory extraction conditionally approved but gated on seam analysis output.

key_claims.text: Three concurrent tracks: auth (blocked), payments (blocked), inventory/orders (conditionally active)
90-day mandate is achievable only if session store migration does not trigger the audit requirement
Inventory extraction conditionally approved — gated on seam analysis and 'existing API' clarification
Three-way escalation (CEO + compliance + engineering) is required if session store migration triggers audit

strands.title: PCI-DSS Compliance Strategy
strands.content: PCI scope encompasses entire monolith currently. Extracting payments is the path to scope reduction. QSA engagement remains urgent — two open scoping questions: (1) payments architecture sign-off, (2) whether auth code paths sharing the payments DB extend PCI scope to auth. New complication: two compliance processes now potentially compete for budget and auditor scheduling — PCI QSA for payments and security audit for auth. Whether these can be combined is worth investigating and could improve both timeline and cost. Compliance officer must coordinate both tracks. Raw card data storage question remains open and high-impact. Inventory service extraction does not appear to be in PCI scope assuming inventory does not handle cardholder data — but explicit confirmation warranted.

key_claims.text: Entire monolith is currently in PCI scope
QSA engagement must happen this week — 90-day clock and two open scoping questions
Two compliance audit processes compete for limited budget — combination opportunity worth pursuing
Raw card data storage in monolith would materially increase migration complexity

strands.title: Technical Debt and Coupling Analysis
strands.content: Three confirmed coupling layers: (1) APPLICATION-LAYER — payments reaches into inventory, order state, user profiles; read-vs-write topology unresolved, now has hard deadline from inventory extraction proposal. (2) DATA-LAYER — auth session tokens in payments DB, confirmed outage root cause. (3) ORM-LAYER — auth coupled to ORM layer per team lead, type uncharacterized. Auth was not designed as a bounded context — grew as infrastructure adjunct; session store in payments DB and ORM coupling are consistent with this pattern. Payments remains the gravitational center. Inventory seam analysis will simultaneously resolve the payments→inventory coupling topology question — dual-purpose work, now urgent. ORM coupling characterization methodology: inspect auth package entity relationships, check for cross-domain entity references, inspect ORM interceptors/listeners, grep for join queries crossing domain boundaries. Half-day exercise with team lead. Additional critical check for inventory: do read queries join through payments tables? If yes, read-path API exposure is blocked and DynamoDB migration would have been catastrophic.

key_claims.text: Payments has confirmed application-layer coupling into inventory, order state, and user profiles
Auth ORM coupling is confirmed as real — type uncharacterized
Shared ORM entity scenario (A) is most likely given dependency-magnet pattern — unconfirmed
Inventory seam analysis resolves payments→inventory coupling topology — dual-purpose, now urgent
Read coupling in payments→other domains is extractable via API callouts without Saga complexity
Write/trigger coupling within transaction boundaries requires Saga pattern or equivalent

strands.title: Team and Organizational Dynamics
strands.content: Three team dynamics now active simultaneously. (1) Team lead (auth): caution validated by compliance discoveries. Her greenfield recommendation has weakened further under compliance constraints. Hybrid must be framed as synthesis of her diagnosis, not rejection. (2) Inventory team: has energy and a concrete proposal — valuable, must not be squashed. DynamoDB request signals technical motivation beyond pure extraction need; rejection framed as 'not now, here's when' preserves engagement. Conditional approval with explicit gates (seam analysis, API clarification) channels their momentum productively. (3) Compliance officer: new organizational stakeholder who must be embedded in planning. The three-way escalation (CEO + compliance + engineering) is the most urgent non-technical action. Starting implementation work before constraint resolution wastes effort and damages credibility. One-team-one-service ownership model requires inventory team to own monolith integration work, not just the new service — this expectation must be set explicitly.

key_claims.text: Team lead's caution is empirically validated — compliance constraints confirm migration is harder than initial framing
Inventory team momentum is valuable — conditional approval with gates is preferable to flat rejection
CEO mandate in conflict with compliance constraints is a governance problem, not an engineering problem
Inventory team must own monolith-side integration work, not just the new service — expectation must be set explicitly
DynamoDB rejection framed as sequencing decision ('not now') preserves team motivation better than permanent rejection

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Incident History and Executive Constraints as a standalone strand — content fully integrated into System Baseline and Migration Principles and Team and Organizational Dynamics
declared_losses.why: Context pressure and strand count reduction. The incident and mandate are now ambient constraints woven into every strand.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Team Dynamics and Risk Tolerance merged into Team and Organizational Dynamics — redundant strand boundary eliminated, inventory team dynamics integrated
declared_losses.why: Strand count reduction. Merged without content loss — inventory team dynamics added to consolidated strand.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed atomic cutover design specifics for auth (maintenance window criteria, pre-cutover test suite) — valid content but premature until compliance escalation resolves
declared_losses.why: Practical constraint — designing cutover before knowing whether session store migration triggers audit produces wasted work

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Specific 3-week milestone breakdown for inventory extraction (week 1: scaffolding, week 2: implementation, week 3: staging) — superseded by conditional approval gating on seam analysis
declared_losses.why: Authorial choice — milestone plan is premature until seam analysis validates the coupling assumptions underlying the timeline

open_questions: Does 'new auth system' in the compliance audit requirement include session store migration, or only full service extraction/rewrite? Answer today — determines whether 90-day path exists.
Will CEO accept revised ~5-month timeline for full auth migration given 6-week audit constraint? Requires three-way escalation: CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead.
Can auth security audit and PCI QSA process be combined or run concurrently to reduce timeline and budget impact? Compliance officer + QSA to determine scope overlap.
What type of ORM coupling exists in auth code: (A) shared entities, (B) lifecycle hooks, or (C) framework usage only? Half-day code inspection with team lead — proceed immediately.
Are session tokens co-located or schema-entangled in payments DB? One-day schema inspection — proceed immediately in parallel with ORM inspection.
Is raw card data stored in monolith or is tokenization provider already in use? Direct codebase and infrastructure audit.

What does 'existing API' mean to the inventory team — live HTTP endpoints or internal Java interfaces? Does it cover writes as well as reads? Answer required before inventory extraction approval is finalized.
Does inventory seam analysis reveal payments→inventory coupling as read-only or write/trigger? Highest priority executable technical work — hard deadline imposed by extraction proposal.
prior_tensor.instructions_for_next: Seam analysis is now the most time-pressured work item — inventory team has a 3-week proposal waiting on it. Must be assigned and started immediately. Three parallel read-only investigations: (1) inventory seam analysis + payments coupling topology, (2) auth ORM coupling characterization, (3) session store schema inspection. Most likely next inputs: (1) Seam analysis returns — either clean read-only coupling (unblocks inventory extraction and simplifies payments extraction) or write/trigger coupling (invalidates 3-week plan, requires resequencing). (2) Inventory team clarifies 'existing API' — HTTP endpoints (extraction plausible in 3 weeks) or Java interfaces (6-8 weeks minimum). (3) Compliance officer clarifies session store migration scope — 90-day path restores or escalation becomes urgent. (4) Inventory team pushes back on DynamoDB rejection — explain SQL join risk specifically. (5) Team asks what production-ready acceptance criteria look like for inventory service. Watch for: inventory team starting to build before seam analysis completes — check whether work has already begun. Also watch for monolith integration ownership being silently dropped — it needs an explicit owner before the extraction starts.

process_observations: This cycle merged two strands (Incident History and Executive Constraints + Team Dynamics and Risk Tolerance) into System Baseline and Migration Principles and Team and Organizational Dynamics respectively, reducing from 10 to 9 strands. The inventory team dynamics fit naturally into the consolidated Team and Organizational Dynamics strand without content loss.

The inventory team's DynamoDB proposal introduced a clean architectural decision with high truth values (DynamoDB is not a drop-in replacement = 0.95 truth) alongside genuinely uncertain claims (3-week estimate plausibility = 0.58 truth). This variance within a single strand reflects the uneven evidence quality across the proposal.

Eight open questions is the current count. The 'existing API' clarification question is newly blocking and time-pressured. The payments→inventory coupling question has been reframed as having a hard deadline, elevating its urgency without adding a new question.
Overall indeterminacy dropped slightly from 0.25 to 0.23 as the DynamoDB decision produced several high-confidence claims, partially offsetting persistent compliance uncertainty.
requests: Strand count is 9. The Greenfield vs Extraction Decision strand is now somewhat stable — it reached a conclusion (hybrid preferred, compliance constraints reinforce this) and is unlikely to change unless ORM coupling characterization returns type A results. Consider whether it should be preserved as active or compressed into Technical Debt and Coupling Analysis at next cycle if no new information arrives.

The open_questions list has stabilized at 8. The 'existing API' question and the seam analysis question are now the most time-pressured. Should time-pressure be represented as a field in open_questions, or is the instructions_for_next the right place to carry urgency signals?

Component 1 — Session store migration off payments DB: directly eliminates cascade failure mode, addresses CEO mandate, does not require audit if session store migration is infrastructure not new auth system. Component 3 — Auth and payments boundary formalization: draw seams, document internal APIs, make extraction-ready. No audit required, no distributed transaction risk. Timeline 2-3 weeks analysis and documentation. Component 4 — Observability foundation: OpenTelemetry distributed tracing, structured logging, health endpoints. Non-negotiable for operating extracted services. Timeline 2-3 weeks concurrent. MVM explicitly excludes: full auth service extraction (blocked by audit), payments service extraction (blocked by coupling topology and PCI QSA), order fulfillment extraction (blocked by inventory/payments dependencies). MVM delivers: cascade failure risk eliminated, inventory independently deployable, PCI audit prep completed, auth and payments extraction-ready for Wave 2. This is the honest 6-month outcome.

strands.title: Technical Debt and Coupling Analysis

(2) DATA-LAYER — auth session tokens in payments DB, confirmed outage root cause. (3) ORM-LAYER — auth coupled to ORM layer per team lead, type uncharacterized. Auth was not designed as a bounded context — grew as infrastructure adjunct; session store in payments DB and ORM coupling are consistent with this pattern. Payments remains the gravitational center. Inventory seam analysis will simultaneously resolve the payments→inventory coupling topology question — dual-purpose work, now urgent. ORM coupling characterization methodology: inspect auth package entity relationships, check for cross-domain entity references, inspect ORM interceptors/listeners, grep for join queries crossing domain boundaries. Half-day exercise with team lead. Additional critical check for inventory: do read queries join through payments tables? If yes, read-path API exposure is blocked and DynamoDB migration would have been catastrophic.
