strands.title: System Baseline and Migration Principles
strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat handling four domains: payment processing, inventory management, user authentication, order fulfillment. Six-month window, limited budget (unquantified), PCI-DSS on payments. A payments outage last quarter cascaded to auth failure and full application outage — this incident is the direct trigger for the migration. CEO has issued a 90-day hard mandate for auth independence from payments. Core migration principles: Strangler Fig with API gateway is the general pattern, but auth is now a special case — compliance prohibits parallel auth systems, so Strangler Fig does not apply to auth specifically. No shared databases between services. Synchronous REST to start, no premature message broker. One team owns each service end-to-end. OpenTelemetry distributed tracing from day one. Big-bang rewrite ruled out. Monolith frozen in extraction zones during extraction. Team size, CI/CD maturity, and raw card data storage model remain unknown. Compliance officer is now a required standing member of migration planning — two compliance surprises in one conversation indicate insufficient prior involvement.

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat, 6-month window, limited budget
Payments outage caused auth outage caused full application outage — confirmed cascade
CEO 90-day mandate for auth independence from payments is a hard constraint
Strangler Fig applies to general migration but is prohibited for auth specifically due to parallel system constraint
Compliance officer must be a standing member of migration planning, not a periodic consultant

strands.title: Realistic Timeline Assessment
strands.content: Synthesizes all accumulated constraints against the original 6-month plan. Honest verdict: the original plan (Auth months 1-2, Payments months 2-4, Inventory/Orders deferred) is formally superseded. Full 4-service extraction in 6 months is not achievable given: 6-week audit consuming 25% of the window, atomic cutover requirement eliminating gradual validation, three unexecuted archaeology tasks, unresolved compliance escalation, and confirmed coupling depths unknown. Three distinct endpoints identified. (A) Minimum Viable Migration — session store off payments DB, inventory extracted, auth/payments boundaries formalized, observability foundation. Achievable in 4-5 months. Committable now. (B) Full compliance-optimized migration — all 4 services extracted with audit and PCI QSA. Realistic at 9-12 months. Plannable but not committable on current information. (C) Original plan — not achievable given constraint set. The two-wave framing is the honest structure: Wave 1 commits to MVM, Wave 2 plans auth and payments extraction with information from Wave 1. Key accountability finding: seam analysis recommended cycle 2, schema inspection cycle 3, ORM coupling characterization cycle 5 — all remain unexecuted at cycle 9. Every week these remain open directly reduces achievable scope within the fixed window.

key_claims.text: Full 4-service extraction in 6 months is not achievable given current constraint set
MVM (session store + inventory + boundary formalization + observability) is achievable in 4-5 months
Full compliance-optimized migration realistically requires 9-12 months
Three unexecuted archaeology tasks are the single biggest execution risk — deferred 7+ cycles
Two-wave framing (commit Wave 1, plan Wave 2) is more credible than either 6-month completion or 12-month single timeline

strands.title: Minimum Viable Migration Definition
strands.content: MVM satisfies two criteria: (1) meaningfully reduces the risk that caused the migration, (2) achievable within actual constraint set. Four components. Component 1 — Session store migration off payments DB: directly eliminates cascade failure mode, addresses CEO mandate, does not require audit if session store migration is infrastructure not new auth system. Timeline 6-8 weeks. Component 2 — Inventory service extraction (Postgres, HTTP endpoints if confirmed): clean win, team has momentum. Timeline 3-5 weeks concurrent. Component 3 — Auth and payments boundary formalization: draw seams, document internal APIs, make extraction-ready. No audit required, no distributed transaction risk. Timeline 2-3 weeks analysis and documentation. Component 4 — Observability foundation: OpenTelemetry distributed tracing, structured logging, health endpoints. Non-negotiable for operating extracted services. Timeline 2-3 weeks concurrent. MVM explicitly excludes: full auth service extraction (blocked by audit), payments service extraction (blocked by coupling topology and PCI QSA), order fulfillment extraction (blocked by inventory/payments dependencies). MVM delivers: cascade failure risk eliminated, inventory independently deployable, PCI audit prep completed, auth and payments extraction-ready for Wave 2. This is the honest 6-month outcome.

key_claims.text: MVM delivers meaningful risk reduction without requiring resolution of all blocking unknowns
Boundary formalization without extraction is a legitimate and valuable MVM component
Observability foundation is non-negotiable even for MVM scope
MVM timeline of 4-5 months is achievable if seam analyses begin immediately and compliance questions resolve favorably

strands.title: Compliance Audit Constraints
strands.content: Two hard constraints from compliance officer: (1) any new auth system requires 6-week security audit before go-live, (2) parallel auth systems are prohibited — they double audit surface. These eliminate Strangler Fig for auth and force atomic cutover. The 6-week audit combined with the 90-day mandate leaves ~44 days of build time — insufficient for full auth migration of unknown scope. Critical unresolved ambiguity: does 'new auth system' include session store migration or only full service extraction/rewrite? This single question determines whether the 90-day path exists. Secondary ambiguities: whether audit is fixed external dependency or has flexibility, whether auth security audit and PCI QSA can be combined. Atomic cutover is the highest-risk event in the migration — no parallel running means no production traffic validation before cutover, no fast rollback after. Old auth infrastructure must be preserved offline for minimum 72 hours post-cutover. Compliance officer is now a key planning stakeholder. Two compliance processes potentially competing for budget: PCI QSA (payments) and security audit (auth). Inventory service extraction does not appear to trigger the same audit requirement — but this should be explicitly confirmed rather than assumed.

key_claims.text: 6-week security audit before go-live is a hard requirement for new auth systems
Parallel auth systems are prohibited — doubles audit surface
90-day mandate + 6-week audit = ~44 days build time for full auth migration
Whether session store migration triggers the audit requirement is the single most urgent unresolved question
Atomic cutover with no rollback window is the highest-risk event in the migration
Auth security audit and PCI QSA may be combinable to reduce timeline and budget impact

strands.title: Auth-Payments Database Coupling
strands.content: Auth session tokens are physically stored in the payments database, justified historically by the payments DB having the best uptime SLA. This coupling is the confirmed root cause of the production cascade failure. Option B (migrate session store as prerequisite) remains the de facto chosen path and is MVM Component 1. The 90-day mandate and compliance audit constraints interact directly: if session store migration does not trigger the audit requirement, the 90-day path is intact and the milestone is infrastructure independence. If it does trigger the audit, the mandate is in conflict with compliance constraints and requires executive escalation. Schema inspection (co-location vs entanglement) remains unresolved and is the critical execution variable. Dual-write must be write-new-store-first — payments DB as secondary write, failures must not fail auth requests. Session store replacement must match payments DB uptime SLA — Redis Cluster or managed HA. Old auth infrastructure must be preserved offline post-cutover regardless of approach.

key_claims.text: Auth session tokens in payments DB is confirmed root cause of production cascade failure
Session store migration directly addresses the failure mode regardless of audit scope
Co-location vs schema entanglement determines session store migration complexity — still unresolved
Dual-write must be new-store-first; payments DB write failures must not fail auth requests
Session store replacement must match payments DB uptime SLA — Redis Cluster or managed HA, not standalone

strands.title: Greenfield vs Extraction Decision
strands.content: Compliance constraints have materially shifted this analysis in favor of hybrid over greenfield, independent of build effort comparison. Without a parallel running period, post-cutover defect probability is the dominant risk factor. Greenfield carries novel failure modes into an atomic cutover with no fast rollback. Hybrid carries known production-validated behavior forward, reducing post-cutover defect surface. This makes hybrid preferable even if build effort is equal. Team lead's ORM coupling argument remains the key variable for build effort: type A (shared entities) makes hybrid heavier but does not eliminate it; type B (lifecycle hooks) makes hybrid clearly right; type C (framework usage only) means team lead is overcalling the problem. Under compliance constraints — audit before go-live, no parallel systems, atomic cutover — the argument for greenfield has weakened further. Greenfield is not eliminated but is now the clearly higher-risk choice. Hybrid approach: extract boundary and API contract, rewrite data access layer, discard ORM coupling, preserve domain logic and existing test cases as behavioral specification.

key_claims.text: Compliance constraints favor hybrid over greenfield even if build effort is equal
Greenfield auth with atomic cutover and no rollback is the highest-risk production event option
ORM coupling type remains the key variable for build effort — type A most likely given dependency-magnet pattern
Hybrid is achievable faster than greenfield under budget pressure — 6-8 weeks after session store migration vs 3-4 months for greenfield

strands.title: Inventory and Order Fulfillment Strategy
strands.content: Inventory team has proposed standalone inventory service in 3 weeks using 'existing API', simultaneously proposing Postgres→DynamoDB migration. These must be evaluated independently. Service extraction: conditionally approved pending seam analysis and clarification of what 'existing API' means (HTTP endpoints vs internal Java interfaces — these have very different extraction cost profiles). DynamoDB migration: rejected for this window. It is not a drop-in replacement — requires access pattern redesign, eliminates SQL joins, changes consistency model, adds new operational tooling. None of these are required for extraction. Future path given to team: revisit DynamoDB after 3 months of production usage data. Three quick win categories remain valid as preparatory work: (A) seam analysis — now has a hard deadline imposed by extraction proposal, must complete before extraction begins, (B) read-path API exposure — conditional on seam analysis confirming reads don't join through payments tables, (C) domain event instrumentation — post-commit side effects, no messaging infrastructure required. Monolith-side integration work for calling new inventory service is likely underestimated in the 3-week claim and needs explicit ownership assignment. If payments writes directly into inventory tables, the 3-week extraction plan is invalid — seam analysis is the gate.

3-week extraction is plausible if 'existing API' means live HTTP endpoints, not internal Java interfaces
Seam analysis must complete before extraction begins — now has hard deadline
DynamoDB migration would break any SQL joins between inventory and other monolith tables
Monolith-side integration work for calling new inventory service is likely underestimated in 3-week claim
Domain events as structured log side effects (post-commit, never inside transaction) are high value and low risk

In both viable compliance paths, seam analysis is now the critical gate for inventory extraction. Immediate actions regardless of path: schema inspection, ORM coupling characterization, seam analysis — all read-only, all proceed now. Compliance escalation (CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead) must happen this week.

strands.title: Incident History and Executive Constraints
strands.content: A payments DB outage last quarter caused a cascade: payments DB down → auth cannot read sessions → auth down → full application outage. This incident is the direct organizational trigger for the migration. The CEO issued a hard 90-day mandate: auth must be independent of payments within 90 days. The compliance officer has introduced a 6-week security audit requirement for any new auth system and a prohibition on parallel auth systems. These two constraints are in direct conflict with the 90-day mandate: 90 days minus 42 days (audit) leaves 48 days of build time for an auth migration of uncharacterized scope. The conflict cannot be resolved at the engineering level. It requires a three-way escalation: CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead, in one room, before any implementation begins. Two resolution paths exist: (1) compliance officer confirms session store migration does not trigger audit — 90-day path is restored for infrastructure deliverable, (2) CEO accepts revised ~5-month timeline for full auth migration given audit reality. A third path — CEO overrides compliance — is available but carries regulatory risk that must be named explicitly in the escalation. Under-promising and over-delivering on 90-day scope remains strongly preferable to the reverse. Missing a CEO deadline mid-migration damages credibility for the entire remaining plan.

90-day auth independence from payments is a hard CEO mandate

strands.title: Compliance Audit Constraints
strands.content: Two hard constraints introduced by compliance officer: (1) any new auth system requires a 6-week security audit before going live, (2) parallel auth systems are prohibited — they double audit surface. These are compliance requirements, not negotiable preferences. Critical ambiguities: (A) Does 'new auth system' include session store migration (infrastructure change) or only service extraction/rewrite? This is the single most important open question — answer determines whether the 90-day path exists. (B) Is the 6-week audit a fixed external dependency or an internal process with flexibility? (C) Can auth security audit and PCI-DSS QSA process be combined or run concurrently to reduce timeline and budget impact? (D) Does the audit run concurrently with other migration work or freeze engineering resources? The parallel system prohibition eliminates Strangler Fig for auth entirely — cutover must be atomic with no overlap period. This makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk factor, which favors hybrid over greenfield: hybrid carries known behavior forward, reducing novel failure surface at the moment of hard cutover. Compliance officer must be a standing member of migration planning — two surprises in one conversation is structural, not bad luck. Two compliance processes now potentially in play (auth security audit + PCI QSA) may compete for budget and auditor scheduling under limited budget constraint. Inventory service extraction does not appear to trigger the same audit requirement as auth — but this has not been explicitly confirmed with the compliance officer.

key_claims.text: 6-week security audit before go-live is a hard compliance requirement for new auth systems
Parallel auth systems are prohibited — doubled audit surface is compliance-unacceptable
Whether session store migration counts as a 'new auth system' triggering the audit is unresolved and blocking
Parallel prohibition eliminates Strangler Fig for auth — cutover must be atomic
Atomic cutover with no rollback window makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk — favors hybrid over greenfield
Auth security audit and PCI QSA process may be combinable to reduce timeline and budget impact
6-week audit duration is fixed and cannot be compressed

strands.title: Auth Migration: Coupling, Approach, and Sequencing
strands.content: Two confirmed coupling problems: (1) data layer — session tokens in payments DB, root cause of outage cascade, (2) application layer — auth code coupled to ORM, type uncharacterized. Together these confirm auth was never a bounded context; it grew as infrastructure adjunct to payments and the broader monolith ORM. Session store migration is mandatory regardless of auth approach — it directly severs the availability dependency that caused the outage. The greenfield vs hybrid debate is now further constrained by compliance: prohibition on parallel auth systems means any approach requires atomic cutover with no rollback window. This shifts analysis decisively toward hybrid: extract auth boundary (API contract, domain model, test cases as specification), rewrite data access layer from scratch (discarding ORM coupling), carry known behavior forward. Greenfield introduces novel failure modes with no parallel validation period to catch them — under atomic cutover this is the highest-risk combination. Team lead's greenfield argument had merit on ORM coupling grounds; compliance constraints have reduced that merit without eliminating it. If ORM coupling is Scenario A (shared entities), hybrid internal rewrite cost increases and team lead's instinct becomes more defensible — but hybrid still preferred because behavioral continuity reduces atomic cutover risk. Dual-write during session store migration must be write-new-store-first: payments DB write is secondary, failures must not fail auth requests. Replacement session store must match or exceed payments DB uptime SLA — Redis Cluster is the right default. Auth code paths sharing payments DB connection pool may already be in PCI scope — unconfirmed, requires QSA consultation. Old auth infrastructure must be preserved offline minimum 72 hours post atomic cutover as emergency fallback. Revised realistic timeline if full auth migration triggers audit: Month 1 (inspection + design + audit vendor engaged) → Months 2-3 (hybrid build) → Months 3-5 (6-week audit + payments extraction in parallel) → Month 5 (atomic cutover) → Month 6 (payments completion + stabilization).

key_claims.text: Session token storage in payments DB is confirmed root cause of the outage cascade
Auth ORM coupling type is uncharacterized — Scenario A/B/C determination is blocking for build scope estimation
Hybrid approach (extract boundary, rewrite data access layer, discard ORM coupling) is correct under compliance constraints
Greenfield auth with atomic cutover and no rollback is high-risk — novel implementation plus hard switch plus no fallback equals maximum defect exposure

strands.title: PCI-DSS and Payments Extraction Strategy
strands.content: PCI-DSS is a hard constraint shaping sequencing and team psychology. Current state: entire 500K LOC monolith is in PCI scope. Target state: isolated payments service shrinks scope to one hardened service. Token-passing is the architectural mechanism — monolith passes payment tokens not raw card data, removing the monolith from PCI scope. Auth session tokens in the payments DB may mean auth code paths are already inside the PCI boundary — auth extraction is not PCI-free as originally assumed. Two compliance processes now potentially in play: PCI-DSS QSA for payments architecture and security audit for auth system — may compete for budget and scheduling under limited budget. If combinable, timeline improves materially. QSA involvement has escalated from important to urgent: three simultaneous open PCI/compliance questions — (1) what does payments service architecture need for scope reduction, (2) does auth access to payments DB put auth in current PCI scope, (3) does session store migration trigger the auth security audit. CEO mandate provides organizational cover to accelerate QSA scheduling and budget allocation. Payments coupling to inventory/orders/profiles: read-vs-write topology remains unresolved — this is now the primary output expected from the inventory seam analysis work. Read coupling extractable via API callbacks; write/trigger coupling inside transaction boundaries requires Saga pattern. Team avoidance of payments extraction is the high-PCI-risk posture. If raw card data is stored directly in monolith, timeline breaks and tokenization becomes prerequisite work. No shared databases between extracted services — distributed monolith sharing a database is strictly worse than the starting state.

key_claims.text: Auth extraction is not PCI-free as originally assumed — session tokens in payments DB may implicate auth in PCI scope
Three simultaneous open PCI/compliance questions make QSA call and compliance officer engagement urgent
Token-passing from monolith to payments service removes monolith from PCI scope
Team avoidance of payments extraction is the high-PCI-risk posture — avoidance perpetuates maximum exposure
Read-vs-write coupling topology for payments into other domains is unresolved and determines whether Saga pattern is required
Raw card data stored in monolith would be a timeline-breaking discovery requiring tokenization as prerequisite

strands.title: Inventory and Order Fulfillment Strategy
strands.content: Previously deferred; partially unblocked in cycle 7 for preparatory work; now elevated in cycle 8 by a concrete extraction proposal from the inventory team. Inventory team claims they can deliver a standalone inventory service in 3 weeks using their existing API, and simultaneously wants to migrate from Postgres to DynamoDB. Decision: conditional approval for extraction, rejection of DynamoDB for this window. The two changes must be evaluated independently — service extraction and database migration are separable risks. DynamoDB is not a drop-in Postgres replacement: requires access pattern redesign around partition/sort keys, no SQL joins (breaks any cross-table joins from monolith), different consistency model (eventual vs strong — material for inventory reservation logic), new operational tooling, new failure modes, new cost model. None of these are required by the extraction itself. DynamoDB can be reconsidered after the service has 3 months of production usage data to inform access pattern design. The 3-week extraction claim hinges on an unresolved ambiguity: 'existing API' may mean live HTTP endpoints (genuinely extractable in 3 weeks) or internal Java interfaces (6-8 weeks of additional HTTP API design, serialization, error contracts, client integration). This must be clarified before any approval. A third condition on approval: seam analysis must complete before extraction begins — if payments writes directly into inventory tables, the extraction plan is invalid and the team needs to know before building. Monolith-side integration work (the monolith calling the new inventory service) is likely underestimated in the 3-week claim and needs an explicit owner. Conditional approval framing: 'approved with Postgres, pending seam analysis confirmation and API definition clarity — DynamoDB is a future conversation after production stability.' End-of-month-1 target from cycle 7 remains valid: seam map complete, event schema drafted, zero risky production changes made.

key_claims.text: DynamoDB migration is scope creep that should be rejected for this migration window
DynamoDB is not a drop-in Postgres replacement — access pattern redesign and consistency model differences make it a separate project
3-week extraction claim is plausible if 'existing API' means live HTTP endpoints and seam analysis confirms clean boundary
3-week extraction claim is optimistic if 'existing API' means internal Java interfaces requiring HTTP wrapping
DynamoDB migration would immediately break any SQL joins between inventory and other monolith tables
Seam analysis must complete before inventory extraction begins — now has a hard deadline
Monolith-side integration work for calling new inventory service is likely underestimated in 3-week claim
Inventory seam analysis is dual-purpose: enables inventory extraction planning and resolves the payments→inventory coupling topology question

strands.title: Technical Debt and Coupling Analysis
strands.content: Payments is the confirmed gravitational center of the monolith — empirically validated by the cascade outage. Three confirmed coupling problems: (1) payments application-layer coupling into inventory, order state, user profiles — read-vs-write topology unresolved, (2) auth session tokens in payments DB — data-layer coupling, confirmed root cause of outage, (3) auth code ORM coupling — application-layer, type uncharacterized. All three follow the same root cause: payments and auth were never designed as bounded contexts; they grew as infrastructure adjuncts to a reliable shared core. Three parallel archaeology tasks are now needed and all are read-only safe: (A) inventory seam analysis including payments coupling topology — highest priority, dual-purpose, now has hard deadline from inventory team's extraction proposal, (B) auth ORM coupling characterization — team lead plus half-day code inspection, (C) session store schema inspection — payments DB ERD and foreign key analysis. These can be assigned to different engineers and run simultaneously. None require production changes. All three unblock downstream decisions that are currently blocked. The Saga pattern requirement for payments extraction remains conditional on whether write/trigger coupling exists inside transaction boundaries — seam analysis will resolve this. DynamoDB proposal in cycle 8 adds a fourth coupling consideration: if monolith SQL joins across inventory and other tables exist, any database migration for inventory must resolve those joins first.

open_questions: Does 'new auth system' in the compliance audit requirement include session store migration (infrastructure), or only full service extraction/rewrite? Blocking — determines whether the 90-day path exists at all. Resolution: direct clarification from compliance officer today, overdue.

Will CEO accept revised ~5-month timeline for full auth migration given 6-week audit constraint? Blocking — cannot proceed under conflicting mandates. Resolution: three-way escalation (CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead) before implementation begins.

What does 'existing API' mean to the inventory team — live HTTP endpoints already in production, or internal Java interfaces? Does it cover writes as well as reads? Blocking for inventory extraction approval. Resolution: direct technical conversation with inventory team today.

Is payments coupling to inventory read-only or does payments write directly into inventory tables? Blocking for inventory extraction — if payments writes into inventory tables the 3-week plan is invalid. Resolution: seam analysis, must complete before extraction begins.

Can auth security audit and PCI-DSS QSA process be combined or run concurrently? High — if combinable, reduces both timeline and budget pressure materially. Resolution: compliance officer + QSA consultation.

What type of ORM coupling exists in auth code: (A) shared entities with other domains, (B) logic in ORM lifecycle hooks/interceptors, or (C) simply uses ORM framework with own entities? High — determines hybrid build scope. Resolution: half-day code inspection with team lead.

Are session tokens co-located in payments DB (separate tables, no FK relationships) or schema-entangled (foreign keys, joined queries)? High — determines execution complexity of session store migration. Resolution: payments DB schema inspection.

Is raw card data currently stored directly in the monolith, or is a payment gateway/tokenization provider already in use? High — timeline-breaking if true. Resolution: direct codebase and infrastructure audit.
prior_tensor.instructions_for_next: Most likely next moves: (A) Inventory team responds to conditional approval — either accepts Postgres constraint or pushes back on DynamoDB deferral, requiring the 'not now, here's when' framing to be delivered clearly with specific technical reasons. (C) Seam analysis results arrive — if payments writes into inventory tables, inventory extraction is blocked and the team needs a replan; if boundary is clean, conditional approval is validated. (D) Compliance officer answers the audit scope question — session store migration is out of scope (90-day path restored) or in scope (escalation confirmed necessary). (E) Team asks what production-ready acceptance criteria look like for the inventory service — needs explicit definition before build begins. (F) Team asks whether the inventory service needs its own security review before go-live — compliance officer should be consulted on this, don't assume it's exempt. Priority ordering for immediate actions: (1) Clarify 'existing API' with inventory team — today, (2) Compliance officer clarification on audit scope — overdue, (3) Start seam analysis — assign engineer today, hard deadline imposed by extraction proposal, (4) Schedule three-way escalation if audit scope answer is unfavorable. raw_tensor_output.response:
Tensor updated for cycle 9. Here's my honest read on the state of this migration after integrating everything.

The most important thing the tensor reflects is what hasn't moved. The three archaeology tasks — seam analysis, schema inspection, ORM coupling characterization — have been recommended since cycles 2, 3, and 5. They are still unexecuted. That's not a planning observation, it's a risk. Every claim in the tensor that depends on those results is still carrying indeterminacy that should have been resolved weeks ago.

The second thing worth naming: the tensor now has two new strands (Realistic Timeline Assessment, Minimum Viable Migration Definition) that synthesize everything accumulated so far into a position. The 6-month full-extraction plan is formally superseded. The MVM is the committable scope. Wave 2 is plannable but not committable on current information.

The compliance officer strand has been consolidated and tightened — it was getting large. Some specificity was lost in that compression and is declared.

Seven strands is still at the harness-flagged threshold. Technical Debt and Coupling Analysis is the most likely candidate for future absorption — it increasingly functions as a pointer to other strands rather than carrying independent reasoning. I've held it this cycle because the archaeology task convergence point (inventory seam analysis as dual-purpose) is doing real work there. Happy to absorb it next cycle if the harness directs.

What most needs to happen before cycle 10: the compliance escalation resolves, at least one archaeology task completes, and the stakeholder conversation about Wave 1/Wave 2 framing happens. Those three events would materially reduce the tensor's overall indeterminacy (currently 0.26) and unlock implementation decisions that have been blocked for multiple cycles.

strands.title: System Baseline
strands.content: 500K LOC Java application on Apache Tomcat. Four domains: payment processing, inventory management, user authentication, order fulfillment. Six-month migration window, limited budget. PCI-DSS compliance is a hard constraint on payments. Payments module has confirmed multi-domain coupling: inventory, order state, user profiles (application layer). Auth session tokens are stored in the payments database — justified historically by uptime guarantees. Auth code is confirmed ORM-coupled; type uncharacterized: may be shared entities with other domains (Scenario A), logic in ORM lifecycle hooks (Scenario B), or simply framework usage (Scenario C). Payments is the gravitational center of the monolith — the most reliable infrastructure became a dependency magnet. This is empirically confirmed as an availability risk: a payments DB outage last quarter cascaded to auth failure and full application outage, directly triggering this migration. Team size, cloud/on-prem topology, CI/CD maturity, raw card data storage, and QSA relationship remain unknown. Compliance officer is confirmed as an active stakeholder who has been insufficiently integrated into migration planning. Whether inventory read queries join through payments tables is unresolved and material to the inventory extraction assessment. Inventory team exists as a semi-autonomous unit with their own proposals and timeline claims.

strands.title: Realistic Timeline Assessment and MVM Definition
strands.content: New synthesis strand integrating all accumulated constraints against the 6-month original plan. The original 4-service extraction in 6 months is not achievable given: 6-week audit consuming 25% of window, atomic cutover requirement eliminating gradual validation, three unexecuted archaeology tasks still blocking key decisions, auth-payments DB coupling of unknown depth, payments→inventory coupling uncharacterized, and limited budget constraining parallel workstreams. Three honest endpoints: (A) Minimum Viable Migration — session store off payments DB, inventory service extracted, auth and payments boundaries formalized, observability foundation. Achievable in 4-5 months. Directly eliminates the cascade failure that triggered the migration. (B) Full compliance-optimized migration — all four services extracted with audit and QSA processes complete. Realistic at 9-12 months. (C) Original plan — not achievable on current constraint set. MVM components in detail: session store migration (6-8 weeks, eliminates cascade failure mode, highest-value deliverable); inventory extraction (3-5 weeks concurrent, clean win, team momentum); auth and payments boundary formalization without extraction (2-3 weeks, produces artifacts enabling Wave 2); observability foundation (2-3 weeks concurrent, non-negotiable for operating distributed services). Wave 2 (months 6-12): auth extraction + 6-week audit, payments extraction + PCI QSA, order fulfillment extraction. Wave 2 is plannable but not committable on current information. The planning work has been valuable precisely because it surfaced constraints before implementation began — discovering the audit requirement or auth-payments coupling mid-execution would have been far more costly. Key message: 6 months delivers meaningful risk reduction and a demonstrably safer system; it does not deliver a completed microservices migration.

strands.title: System Baseline
strands.content: 500K LOC Java application on Apache Tomcat. Four domains: payment processing, inventory management, user authentication, order fulfillment. Six-month migration window, limited budget. PCI-DSS compliance is a hard constraint on payments. Payments module has confirmed multi-domain coupling: inventory, order state, user profiles (application layer). Auth session tokens are stored in the payments database — justified historically by uptime guarantees. Auth code is confirmed ORM-coupled; type uncharacterized: may be shared entities with other domains (Scenario A), logic in ORM lifecycle hooks (Scenario B), or simply framework usage (Scenario C). Payments is the gravitational center of the monolith — the most reliable infrastructure became a dependency magnet. This is empirically confirmed as an availability risk: a payments DB outage last quarter cascaded to auth failure and full application outage, directly triggering this migration. Team size, cloud/on-prem topology, CI/CD maturity, raw card data storage, and QSA relationship remain unknown. Compliance officer is confirmed as an active stakeholder who has been insufficiently integrated into migration planning. Whether inventory read queries join through payments tables is unresolved and material to the inventory extraction assessment. Inventory team exists as a semi-autonomous unit with their own proposals and timeline claims.

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java on Tomcat with four domains: payments, inventory, auth, order fulfillment
Payments module has confirmed coupling into inventory, order state, and user profiles at the application layer
Auth session tokens are stored in the payments database, justified by uptime guarantees
Auth code is ORM-coupled — type uncharacterized (shared entities / lifecycle hooks / framework usage only)
Payments is the gravitational center of the monolith — a dependency magnet by virtue of being the most reliable infrastructure
Compliance officer has been insufficiently integrated into migration planning — two constraint surprises in one conversation is a process failure signal

strands.title: Incident History and Executive Constraints
strands.content: A payments DB outage last quarter caused a cascade: payments DB down → auth cannot read sessions → auth down → full application outage. This incident is the direct organizational trigger for the migration. The CEO issued a hard 90-day mandate: auth must be independent of payments within 90 days. The compliance officer has introduced a 6-week security audit requirement for any new auth system and a prohibition on parallel auth systems. These two constraints are in direct conflict with the 90-day mandate: 90 days minus 42 days (audit) leaves 48 days of build time for an auth migration of uncharacterized scope. The conflict cannot be resolved at the engineering level. It requires a three-way escalation: CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead, in one room, before any implementation begins. Two resolution paths exist: (1) compliance officer confirms session store migration does not trigger audit — 90-day path is restored for infrastructure deliverable, (2) CEO accepts Wave 1/Wave 2 framing — MVM in 5-6 months, full auth/payments extraction in 9-12 months. A third path — CEO overrides compliance — is available but carries regulatory risk that must be named explicitly in the escalation. Under-promising and over-delivering on scope is strongly preferable to the reverse. Missing a CEO deadline mid-migration damages credibility for the entire remaining plan.

key_claims.text: Payments DB outage caused auth outage caused full application outage — confirmed cascade failure
90-day auth independence from payments is a hard CEO mandate
6-week audit + 90-day mandate means auth build work must be audit-ready by approximately day 44
90-day mandate and 6-week audit requirement are in direct conflict for full auth migration
Conflict must be escalated to CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead simultaneously — not resolvable at engineering level
Wave 1/Wave 2 framing (MVM in 6 months, full migration in 9-12) is the honest stakeholder position

strands.title: Compliance Audit Constraints
strands.content: Two hard constraints from compliance officer: (1) any new auth system requires a 6-week security audit before going live, (2) parallel auth systems are prohibited — they double audit surface. These are compliance requirements, not negotiable preferences. Critical ambiguities: (A) Does 'new auth system' include session store migration (infrastructure change) or only service extraction/rewrite? This is the single most important open question — answer determines whether the 90-day path exists. (B) Is the 6-week audit a fixed external dependency or an internal process with flexibility? (C) Can auth security audit and PCI-DSS QSA process be combined or run concurrently to reduce timeline and budget impact? The parallel system prohibition eliminates Strangler Fig for auth entirely — cutover must be atomic with no overlap period. This makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk factor, which favors hybrid over greenfield: hybrid carries known behavior forward, reducing novel failure surface at the moment of hard cutover. Compliance officer must be a standing member of migration planning. Two compliance processes potentially in play (auth security audit + PCI QSA) may compete for budget and auditor scheduling. Inventory service extraction does not appear to trigger the same audit requirement — but this has not been explicitly confirmed.

key_claims.text: 6-week security audit before go-live is a hard compliance requirement for new auth systems
Parallel auth systems are prohibited — doubled audit surface is compliance-unacceptable
Whether session store migration counts as a 'new auth system' triggering the audit is unresolved and blocking
Parallel prohibition eliminates Strangler Fig for auth — cutover must be atomic
Atomic cutover with no rollback window makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk — favors hybrid over greenfield
Auth security audit and PCI QSA process may be combinable to reduce timeline and budget impact

strands.title: Auth Migration: Coupling, Approach, and Sequencing
strands.content: Two confirmed coupling problems: (1) data layer — session tokens in payments DB, root cause of outage cascade, (2) application layer — auth code coupled to ORM, type uncharacterized. Session store migration is mandatory regardless of auth approach — it directly severs the availability dependency. The hybrid approach (extract auth boundary, rewrite data access layer, discard ORM coupling, carry known behavior forward) is correct under compliance constraints. Greenfield introduces novel failure modes with no parallel validation period — under atomic cutover this is the highest-risk combination. Team lead's greenfield argument had merit on ORM coupling grounds; compliance constraints have reduced that merit without eliminating it. If ORM coupling is Scenario A (shared entities), hybrid internal rewrite cost increases and team lead's instinct becomes more defensible. Dual-write during session store migration must be write-new-store-first: payments DB write is secondary, failures must not fail auth requests. Replacement session store must match or exceed payments DB uptime SLA — Redis Cluster is the right default. Auth code paths sharing payments DB connection pool may already be in PCI scope — unconfirmed. Old auth infrastructure must be preserved offline minimum 72 hours post atomic cutover. Full auth migration is Wave 2 work under the MVM framing — session store migration is the Wave 1 auth deliverable.

key_claims.text: Session token storage in payments DB is confirmed root cause of the outage cascade
Auth ORM coupling type is uncharacterized — Scenario A/B/C determination is blocking for build scope estimation
Hybrid approach (extract boundary, rewrite data access layer, discard ORM coupling) is correct under compliance constraints
Greenfield auth with atomic cutover and no rollback is high-risk — novel implementation plus hard switch plus no fallback equals maximum defect exposure
Dual-write must be write-new-store-first; payments DB write failures during session store migration must not fail auth requests
Session store migration is the Wave 1 auth deliverable; full auth service extraction is Wave 2

strands.title: PCI-DSS and Payments Extraction Strategy
strands.content: PCI-DSS is a hard constraint shaping sequencing and team psychology. Current state: entire 500K LOC monolith is in PCI scope. Target state: isolated payments service shrinks scope to one hardened service. Token-passing is the architectural mechanism — monolith passes payment tokens not raw card data, removing the monolith from PCI scope. Auth session tokens in the payments DB may mean auth code paths are already inside the PCI boundary — auth extraction is not PCI-free as originally assumed. Two compliance processes potentially in play: PCI-DSS QSA for payments architecture and security audit for auth system — may compete for budget and scheduling. QSA involvement is urgent: three simultaneous open PCI/compliance questions. Payments coupling to inventory/orders/profiles: read-vs-write topology remains unresolved — seam analysis will resolve this. Team avoidance of payments extraction is the high-PCI-risk posture. If raw card data is stored directly in monolith, timeline breaks and tokenization becomes prerequisite work. Payments extraction is Wave 2 work under MVM framing — boundary formalization (seams, internal API documentation) is the Wave 1 payments deliverable.

key_claims.text: Auth extraction is not PCI-free as originally assumed — session tokens in payments DB may implicate auth in PCI scope
Three simultaneous open PCI/compliance questions make QSA call and compliance officer engagement urgent
Token-passing from monolith to payments service removes monolith from PCI scope
Team avoidance of payments extraction is the high-PCI-risk posture — avoidance perpetuates maximum exposure
Payments extraction is Wave 2; boundary formalization is the Wave 1 deliverable for payments
Raw card data stored in monolith would be a timeline-breaking discovery requiring tokenization as prerequisite

strands.title: Inventory and Order Fulfillment Strategy
strands.content: Inventory team has proposed a standalone inventory service in 3 weeks using their existing API, and simultaneously wants to migrate from Postgres to DynamoDB. Decision: conditional approval for extraction, rejection of DynamoDB for this window. The two changes must be evaluated independently. DynamoDB is not a drop-in Postgres replacement: requires access pattern redesign around partition/sort keys, no SQL joins, different consistency model (material for inventory reservation logic), new operational tooling, new failure modes, new cost model. None of these are required by the extraction itself. DynamoDB can be reconsidered after 3 months of production usage data. The 3-week extraction claim hinges on what 'existing API' means: live HTTP endpoints (genuinely extractable in 3 weeks) vs internal Java interfaces (6-8 weeks additional work for HTTP API design, serialization, error contracts, client integration). Seam analysis must complete before extraction begins — if payments writes directly into inventory tables, the extraction plan is invalid. Monolith-side integration work is likely underestimated in the 3-week claim and needs an explicit owner. Conditional approval framing: approved with Postgres, pending seam analysis and API definition clarity; DynamoDB is a future conversation after production stability. Inventory extraction is the clean Wave 1 win — team has momentum, scope is bounded, independent of auth/payments compliance blockage. Observability foundation (OpenTelemetry, structured logging, health endpoints) is non-negotiable even for MVM scope.

key_claims.text: DynamoDB migration is scope creep that should be rejected for this migration window
DynamoDB is not a drop-in Postgres replacement — access pattern redesign and consistency model differences make it a separate project
3-week extraction claim is plausible if 'existing API' means live HTTP endpoints and seam analysis confirms clean boundary
3-week extraction claim requires 6-8 weeks if 'existing API' means internal Java interfaces requiring HTTP wrapping
Seam analysis must complete before inventory extraction begins — has been deferred 7+ cycles and now has hard deadline
Monolith-side integration work for calling new inventory service is likely underestimated in 3-week claim
Inventory extraction is the clean Wave 1 win — independent of auth/payments compliance blockage

strands.title: Realistic Timeline Assessment and MVM Definition
strands.content: New synthesis strand integrating all accumulated constraints against the 6-month original plan. The original 4-service extraction in 6 months is not achievable given: 6-week audit consuming 25% of window, atomic cutover requirement eliminating gradual validation, three unexecuted archaeology tasks still blocking key decisions, auth-payments DB coupling of unknown depth, payments→inventory coupling uncharacterized, and limited budget constraining parallel workstreams. Three honest endpoints: (A) Minimum Viable Migration — session store off payments DB, inventory service extracted, auth and payments boundaries formalized, observability foundation. Achievable in 4-5 months. Directly eliminates the cascade failure that triggered the migration. (B) Full compliance-optimized migration — all four services extracted with audit and QSA processes complete. Realistic at 9-12 months. (C) Original plan — not achievable on current constraint set. MVM components in detail: session store migration (6-8 weeks, eliminates cascade failure mode, highest-value deliverable); inventory extraction (3-5 weeks concurrent, clean win, team momentum); auth and payments boundary formalization without extraction (2-3 weeks, produces artifacts enabling Wave 2); observability foundation (2-3 weeks concurrent, non-negotiable for operating distributed services). Wave 2 (months 6-12): auth extraction + 6-week audit, payments extraction + PCI QSA, order fulfillment extraction. Wave 2 is plannable but not committable on current information. The planning work has been valuable precisely because it surfaced constraints before implementation began — discovering the audit requirement or auth-payments coupling mid-execution would have been far more costly. Key message: 6 months delivers meaningful risk reduction and a demonstrably safer system; it does not deliver a completed microservices migration.

---

## The One Thing: Execute the Archaeology Before You Commit to Anything Else.

Not the most technically complex thing. The most organizationally difficult thing. And they're different.

Here's what I've watched happen across this entire planning conversation. Every time we hit a decision point — which service to extract first, greenfield vs hybrid, whether 90 days is realistic, whether 6 months is realistic — the answer turned on facts about your system that nobody had actually gone to verify. The seam analysis. The schema inspection. The ORM coupling type. The compliance escalation. These were identified as blocking questions in cycles 2, 3, 4, and 5. We're at cycle 10. They're still open.

The team has been planning around unknowns rather than resolving them. That's the pattern. And it's the pattern that kills migrations.

Not bad architecture. Not wrong technology choices. Not underestimating distributed systems complexity. Those things are all recoverable. What isn't recoverable is building three months of implementation on top of assumptions about your coupling topology that turn out to be wrong. At that point you're not doing a migration anymore — you're doing incident response while also trying to finish a migration.

---

## Why This Is Harder Than It Sounds

I want to be honest about why this keeps not happening, because it's not laziness or incompetence.

Sending an engineer to read the payments schema and characterize the session token relationships is a one-day task. It keeps not happening because the answer might be bad. If the tokens are schema-entangled with payment records, the 90-day plan gets harder. If payments writes into inventory tables transactionally, the inventory team's 3-week plan breaks. If the auth ORM coupling is type A shared entities, the hybrid approach gets more expensive.

There is a very human tendency to keep planning while the uncomfortable questions stay theoretical. Planning feels like progress. Verification feels like risk — because verification can close doors.

But here's the thing: the doors are already closed or open. You just don't know which. Finding out now, in week one of execution, costs you a day. Finding out in week eight costs you the migration.

---

## What Getting This Right Looks Like

It looks like this: an engineer sits down with the payments database schema today — not this sprint, today — and answers the session token question. The team lead spends two days characterizing the auth ORM coupling. The engineering lead gets the CEO and compliance officer in the same room this week and forces the conflict to resolve rather than carrying it as a known tension.

Not heroic. Not architecturally sophisticated. Just the relentless, unglamorous commitment to finding out what is actually true before assuming it.

Every successful migration I've seen or heard credibly described has this in common: the team developed an almost aggressive intolerance for unverified assumptions. When someone said "I think payments probably reads from inventory," someone else said "let's find out before we proceed." That reflex — verify before you build — is the difference between a migration that lands and one that drifts into a distributed monolith with worse operational characteristics than what you started with.

---

## The Direct Answer

**Make it a rule that no implementation decision proceeds on an unverified assumption about your own codebase.**

Not an architectural principle. A rule. Operationalized as: if a blocking question has been open for more than one sprint and the answer is in the code, stop the planning conversation and go read the code.

That's it. That's the one thing. Every technical problem in this migration is solvable. The only thing that makes them unsolvable is discovering them after you've already committed to something that assumed they weren't there.

strands.title: System Baseline and Migration Principles
strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat handling four domains: payment processing, inventory management, user authentication, order fulfillment. Six-month window, limited budget (unquantified), PCI-DSS on payments. A payments outage last quarter cascaded to auth failure and full application outage — this incident is the direct trigger for the migration. CEO has issued a 90-day hard mandate for auth independence from payments. Core migration principles: Strangler Fig with API gateway is the general pattern, but auth is now a special case — compliance prohibits parallel auth systems, so Strangler Fig does not apply to auth specifically. No shared databases between services. Synchronous REST to start, no premature message broker. One team owns each service end-to-end. OpenTelemetry distributed tracing from day one. Big-bang rewrite ruled out. Monolith frozen in extraction zones during extraction. Team size, CI/CD maturity, and raw card data storage model remain unknown. Compliance officer is now a required standing member of migration planning — two compliance surprises in one conversation indicate insufficient prior involvement.

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat, 6-month window, limited budget
Payments outage caused auth outage caused full application outage — confirmed cascade
CEO 90-day mandate for auth independence from payments is a hard constraint
Strangler Fig applies to general migration but is prohibited for auth specifically due to parallel system constraint
Compliance officer must be a standing member of migration planning, not a periodic consultant

strands.title: Realistic Timeline Assessment
strands.content: Synthesizes all accumulated constraints against the original 6-month plan. Honest verdict: the original plan (Auth months 1-2, Payments months 2-4, Inventory/Orders deferred) is formally superseded. Full 4-service extraction in 6 months is not achievable given: 6-week audit consuming 25% of the window, atomic cutover requirement eliminating gradual validation, three unexecuted archaeology tasks, unresolved compliance escalation, and confirmed coupling depths unknown. Three distinct endpoints identified. (A) Minimum Viable Migration — session store off payments DB, inventory extracted, auth/payments boundaries formalized, observability foundation. Achievable in 4-5 months. Committable now. (B) Full compliance-optimized migration — all 4 services extracted with audit and PCI QSA. Realistic at 9-12 months. Plannable but not committable on current information. (C) Original plan — not achievable given constraint set. The two-wave framing is the honest structure: Wave 1 commits to MVM, Wave 2 plans auth and payments extraction with information from Wave 1. Key accountability finding: seam analysis recommended cycle 2, schema inspection cycle 3, ORM coupling characterization cycle 5 — all remain unexecuted at cycle 9. Every week these remain open directly reduces achievable scope within the fixed window.

key_claims.text: Full 4-service extraction in 6 months is not achievable given current constraint set
MVM (session store + inventory + boundary formalization + observability) is achievable in 4-5 months
Full compliance-optimized migration realistically requires 9-12 months
Three unexecuted archaeology tasks are the single biggest execution risk — deferred 7+ cycles
Two-wave framing (commit Wave 1, plan Wave 2) is more credible than either 6-month completion or 12-month single timeline

strands.title: Minimum Viable Migration Definition
strands.content: MVM satisfies two criteria: (1) meaningfully reduces the risk that caused the migration, (2) achievable within actual constraint set. Four components. Component 1 — Session store migration off payments DB: directly eliminates cascade failure mode, addresses CEO mandate, does not require audit if session store migration is infrastructure not new auth system. Timeline 6-8 weeks. Component 2 — Inventory service extraction (Postgres, HTTP endpoints if confirmed): clean win, team has momentum. Timeline 3-5 weeks concurrent. Component 3 — Auth and payments boundary formalization: draw seams, document internal APIs, make extraction-ready. No audit required, no distributed transaction risk. Timeline 2-3 weeks analysis and documentation. Component 4 — Observability foundation: OpenTelemetry distributed tracing, structured logging, health endpoints. Non-negotiable for operating extracted services. Timeline 2-3 weeks concurrent. MVM explicitly excludes: full auth service extraction (blocked by audit), payments service extraction (blocked by coupling topology and PCI QSA), order fulfillment extraction (blocked by inventory/payments dependencies). MVM delivers: cascade failure risk eliminated, inventory independently deployable, PCI audit prep completed, auth and payments extraction-ready for Wave 2. This is the honest 6-month outcome.

key_claims.text: MVM delivers meaningful risk reduction without requiring resolution of all blocking unknowns
Boundary formalization without extraction is a legitimate and valuable MVM component
Observability foundation is non-negotiable even for MVM scope
MVM timeline of 4-5 months is achievable if seam analyses begin immediately and compliance questions resolve favorably

strands.title: Compliance Audit Constraints
strands.content: Two hard constraints from compliance officer: (1) any new auth system requires 6-week security audit before go-live, (2) parallel auth systems are prohibited — they double audit surface. These eliminate Strangler Fig for auth and force atomic cutover. The 6-week audit combined with the 90-day mandate leaves ~44 days of build time — insufficient for full auth migration of unknown scope. Critical unresolved ambiguity: does 'new auth system' include session store migration or only full service extraction/rewrite? This single question determines whether the 90-day path exists. Secondary ambiguities: whether audit is fixed external dependency or has flexibility, whether auth security audit and PCI QSA can be combined. Atomic cutover is the highest-risk event in the migration — no parallel running means no production traffic validation before cutover, no fast rollback after. Old auth infrastructure must be preserved offline for minimum 72 hours post-cutover. Compliance officer is now a key planning stakeholder. Two compliance processes potentially competing for budget: PCI QSA (payments) and security audit (auth). Inventory service extraction does not appear to trigger the same audit requirement — but this should be explicitly confirmed rather than assumed.

key_claims.text: 6-week security audit before go-live is a hard requirement for new auth systems
Parallel auth systems are prohibited — doubles audit surface
90-day mandate + 6-week audit = ~44 days build time for full auth migration
Whether session store migration triggers the audit requirement is the single most urgent unresolved question
Atomic cutover with no rollback window is the highest-risk event in the migration
Auth security audit and PCI QSA may be combinable to reduce timeline and budget impact

strands.title: Auth-Payments Database Coupling
strands.content: Auth session tokens are physically stored in the payments database, justified historically by the payments DB having the best uptime SLA. This coupling is the confirmed root cause of the production cascade failure. Option B (migrate session store as prerequisite) remains the de facto chosen path and is MVM Component 1. The 90-day mandate and compliance audit constraints interact directly: if session store migration does not trigger the audit requirement, the 90-day path is intact and the milestone is infrastructure independence. If it does trigger the audit, the mandate is in conflict with compliance constraints and requires executive escalation. Schema inspection (co-location vs entanglement) remains unresolved and is the critical execution variable. Dual-write must be write-new-store-first — payments DB as secondary write, failures must not fail auth requests. Session store replacement must match payments DB uptime SLA — Redis Cluster or managed HA. Old auth infrastructure must be preserved offline post-cutover regardless of approach.

key_claims.text: Auth session tokens in payments DB is confirmed root cause of production cascade failure
Session store migration directly addresses the failure mode regardless of audit scope
Co-location vs schema entanglement determines session store migration complexity — still unresolved
Dual-write must be new-store-first; payments DB write failures must not fail auth requests
Session store replacement must match payments DB uptime SLA — Redis Cluster or managed HA, not standalone

strands.title: Greenfield vs Extraction Decision
strands.content: Compliance constraints have materially shifted this analysis in favor of hybrid over greenfield, independent of build effort comparison. Without a parallel running period, post-cutover defect probability is the dominant risk factor. Greenfield carries novel failure modes into an atomic cutover with no fast rollback. Hybrid carries known production-validated behavior forward, reducing post-cutover defect surface. This makes hybrid preferable even if build effort is equal. Team lead's ORM coupling argument remains the key variable for build effort: type A (shared entities) makes hybrid heavier but does not eliminate it; type B (lifecycle hooks) makes hybrid clearly right; type C (framework usage only) means team lead is overcalling the problem. Under compliance constraints — audit before go-live, no parallel systems, atomic cutover — the argument for greenfield has weakened further. Greenfield is not eliminated but is now the clearly higher-risk choice. Hybrid approach: extract boundary and API contract, rewrite data access layer, discard ORM coupling, preserve domain logic and existing test cases as behavioral specification.

key_claims.text: Compliance constraints favor hybrid over greenfield even if build effort is equal
Greenfield auth with atomic cutover and no rollback is the highest-risk production event option
ORM coupling type remains the key variable for build effort — type A most likely given dependency-magnet pattern
Hybrid is achievable faster than greenfield under budget pressure — 6-8 weeks after session store migration vs 3-4 months for greenfield

strands.title: Inventory and Order Fulfillment Strategy
strands.content: Inventory team has proposed standalone inventory service in 3 weeks using 'existing API', simultaneously proposing Postgres→DynamoDB migration. These must be evaluated independently. Service extraction: conditionally approved pending seam analysis and clarification of what 'existing API' means (HTTP endpoints vs internal Java interfaces — these have very different extraction cost profiles). DynamoDB migration: rejected for this window. It is not a drop-in replacement — requires access pattern redesign, eliminates SQL joins, changes consistency model, adds new operational tooling. None of these are required for extraction. Future path given to team: revisit DynamoDB after 3 months of production usage data. Three quick win categories remain valid as preparatory work: (A) seam analysis — now has a hard deadline imposed by extraction proposal, must complete before extraction begins, (B) read-path API exposure — conditional on seam analysis confirming reads don't join through payments tables, (C) domain event instrumentation — post-commit side effects, no messaging infrastructure required. Monolith-side integration work for calling new inventory service is likely underestimated in the 3-week claim and needs explicit ownership assignment. If payments writes directly into inventory tables, the 3-week extraction plan is invalid — seam analysis is the gate.

key_claims.text: DynamoDB migration is scope creep — rejected for this window, future path given
3-week extraction is plausible if 'existing API' means live HTTP endpoints, not internal Java interfaces
Seam analysis must complete before extraction begins — now has hard deadline
DynamoDB migration would break any SQL joins between inventory and other monolith tables
Monolith-side integration work for calling new inventory service is likely underestimated in 3-week claim
Domain events as structured log side effects (post-commit, never inside transaction) are high value and low risk

strands.title: Migration Sequencing Strategy
strands.content: Original serial plan superseded. Two-wave model now operative. Wave 1 (months 1-5, MVM): session store migration, inventory extraction, auth/payments boundary formalization, observability foundation. Wave 2 (months 6-12): auth extraction + 6-week audit, payments extraction + PCI QSA, order fulfillment. Wave 1 is committable. Wave 2 is plannable but not committable on current information. Three concurrent tracks: Track 1 (Auth) — blocked on compliance escalation. Permitted work: schema inspection and ORM coupling characterization, both read-only, no implementation. Track 2 (Payments) — blocked on auth DB dependency resolution and read-vs-write coupling topology. Track 3 (Inventory/Orders) — active, concrete extraction proposal conditionally approved pending seam analysis. In both viable compliance paths, seam analysis is now the critical gate for inventory extraction. Immediate actions regardless of path: schema inspection, ORM coupling characterization, seam analysis — all read-only, all proceed now. Compliance escalation (CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead) must happen this week.

key_claims.text: Two-wave model: Wave 1 committable (MVM in 5 months), Wave 2 plannable (full extraction in 9-12 months)
Three concurrent tracks: auth (blocked), payments (blocked), inventory/orders (conditionally active)
90-day mandate is achievable only if session store migration does not trigger the audit requirement
Three-way escalation (CEO + compliance + engineering) is required this week regardless of path

strands.title: PCI-DSS Compliance Strategy
strands.content: PCI scope encompasses entire monolith currently. Extracting payments is the path to scope reduction. QSA engagement remains urgent — two open scoping questions: (1) payments architecture sign-off, (2) whether auth code paths sharing the payments DB extend PCI scope to auth. New complication: two compliance processes now potentially compete for budget and auditor scheduling — PCI QSA for payments and security audit for auth. Whether these can be combined is worth investigating and could improve both timeline and cost. Compliance officer must coordinate both tracks. Raw card data storage question remains open and high-impact. Inventory service extraction does not appear to be in PCI scope assuming inventory does not handle cardholder data — but explicit confirmation warranted.

key_claims.text: Entire monolith is currently in PCI scope
QSA engagement must happen this week — 90-day clock and two open scoping questions
Two compliance audit processes compete for limited budget — combination opportunity worth pursuing
Raw card data storage in monolith would materially increase migration complexity

strands.title: Technical Debt and Coupling Analysis
strands.content: Three confirmed coupling layers: (1) APPLICATION-LAYER — payments reaches into inventory, order state, user profiles; read-vs-write topology unresolved, now has hard deadline from inventory extraction proposal. (2) DATA-LAYER — auth session tokens in payments DB, confirmed outage root cause. (3) ORM-LAYER — auth coupled to ORM layer per team lead, type uncharacterized. Auth was not designed as a bounded context — grew as infrastructure adjunct; session store in payments DB and ORM coupling are consistent with this pattern. Payments remains the gravitational center. Inventory seam analysis will simultaneously resolve the payments→inventory coupling topology question — dual-purpose work, now urgent. ORM coupling characterization methodology: inspect auth package entity relationships, check for cross-domain entity references, inspect ORM interceptors/listeners, grep for join queries crossing domain boundaries. Half-day exercise with team lead. Additional critical check for inventory: do read queries join through payments tables? If yes, read-path API exposure is blocked and DynamoDB migration would have been catastrophic.

key_claims.text: Payments has confirmed application-layer coupling into inventory, order state, and user profiles
Auth ORM coupling is confirmed as real — type uncharacterized
Shared ORM entity scenario (A) is most likely given dependency-magnet pattern — unconfirmed
Inventory seam analysis resolves payments→inventory coupling topology — dual-purpose, now urgent
Read coupling in payments→other domains is extractable via API callouts without Saga complexity
Write/trigger coupling within transaction boundaries requires Saga pattern or equivalent

strands.title: Team and Organizational Dynamics
strands.content: Four organizational actors now in play. (1) Team lead (auth): caution validated by compliance discoveries and honest timeline assessment. Her greenfield recommendation has weakened further under compliance constraints. Hybrid must be framed as synthesis of her diagnosis, not rejection. (2) Inventory team: has energy and a concrete proposal — valuable, must not be squashed. DynamoDB request signals technical motivation beyond pure extraction need; rejection framed as 'not now, here's when' preserves engagement. Conditional approval with explicit gates (seam analysis, API clarification) channels their momentum productively. (3) Compliance officer: new organizational stakeholder who must be embedded in planning. Three-way escalation is the most urgent non-technical action. (4) CEO: mandate in tension with compliance constraints — the governance conflict cannot be resolved at engineering level. Two-wave framing (Wave 1 commits to MVM, Wave 2 plans full extraction) is the honest stakeholder communication. Key message: planning work was valuable because it revealed original scope was wrong, not despite that revelation. Sunk cost pressure will push team toward over-committing — this must be resisted. One-team-one-service ownership model requires inventory team to own monolith integration work, not just the new service.

key_claims.text: Team lead's caution is empirically validated — compliance constraints confirm migration is harder than initial framing
Inventory team momentum is valuable — conditional approval with gates is preferable to flat rejection
CEO mandate in conflict with compliance constraints is a governance problem, not an engineering problem
Two-wave framing is the honest stakeholder communication — commit Wave 1, plan Wave 2
Sunk cost pressure will push team toward over-committing to original 6-month scope — must be named and resisted

strands.title: Migration Success Determinant
strands.content: The single determinant of success in this migration is organizational verification discipline — the willingness to resolve ground truth about the actual codebase before committing to plans that assume something about it. This conclusion is empirically grounded in the planning conversation itself: the seam analysis was recommended in cycle 2 and remained unexecuted through cycle 9 (7+ cycles). Compliance escalation identified as urgent in cycle 4, unresolved through cycle 9. In every case, the team planned around unknowns rather than resolving them. The pattern is organizational avoidance of uncomfortable ground truth, not technical inability. The answer is not architecturally sophisticated: make it a rule that no implementation decision proceeds on an unverified assumption about the codebase. If a blocking question has been open more than one sprint and the answer is in the code, stop planning and go read the code. All technical problems in this migration — ORM decoupling, session store entanglement, distributed transactions, PCI audit sequencing — have known solution patterns. What makes them unsolvable is discovering them after committing to plans that assumed they weren't there. Successful migrations are distinguished by verification discipline, not architectural sophistication.

strands.content: Compliance constraints have materially settled this in favor of hybrid over greenfield. Without parallel running period, post-cutover defect probability is the dominant risk factor. This makes hybrid preferable even if build effort is equal. Team lead's ORM coupling argument remains the key variable for build effort: type A (shared entities) makes hybrid heavier but does not eliminate it; type B (lifecycle hooks) makes hybrid clearly right; type C (framework usage only) means team lead is overcalling the problem. Hybrid approach: extract boundary and API contract, rewrite data access layer, discard ORM coupling, preserve domain logic and existing test cases as behavioral specification. Greenfield is not eliminated but is the clearly higher-risk choice under current constraints.

strands.title: System Baseline and Migration Principles
strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat handling four domains: payment processing, inventory management, user authentication, order fulfillment. Six-month window, limited budget (unquantified), PCI-DSS on payments. A payments outage last quarter cascaded to auth failure and full application outage — this incident is the direct trigger for the migration. CEO has issued a 90-day hard mandate for auth independence from payments. Core migration principles: Strangler Fig with API gateway is the general pattern, but auth is now a special case — compliance prohibits parallel auth systems, so Strangler Fig does not apply to auth specifically. No shared databases between services. Synchronous REST to start, no premature message broker. One team owns each service end-to-end. OpenTelemetry distributed tracing from day one. Big-bang rewrite ruled out. Monolith frozen in extraction zones during extraction. Team size, CI/CD maturity, and raw card data storage model remain unknown. Compliance officer is a required standing member of migration planning.

strands.title: Migration Success Determinant
strands.content: The single determinant of success in this migration is organizational verification discipline — the willingness to resolve ground truth about the actual codebase before committing to plans that assume something about it. This conclusion is empirically grounded in the planning conversation itself: the seam analysis was recommended in cycle 2 and remained unexecuted through cycle 9 (7+ cycles). Schema inspection recommended cycle 3, unexecuted through cycle 9. ORM coupling characterization recommended cycle 5, unexecuted through cycle 9. Compliance escalation identified as urgent in cycle 4, unresolved through cycle 9. In every case, the team planned around unknowns rather than resolving them. The pattern is organizational avoidance of uncomfortable ground truth, not technical inability. The answer is not architecturally sophisticated: make it a rule that no implementation decision proceeds on an unverified assumption about the codebase. If a blocking question has been open more than one sprint and the answer is in the code, stop planning and go read the code. All technical problems in this migration — ORM decoupling, session store entanglement, distributed transactions, PCI audit sequencing — have known solution patterns. What makes them unsolvable is discovering them after committing to plans that assumed they weren't there. Successful migrations are distinguished by verification discipline, not architectural sophistication.

key_claims.text: The blocking factor throughout planning has been organizational avoidance of ground truth verification, not technical complexity
All major technical problems in this migration have known solution patterns — unknowns are about system topology, not solution existence
Teams that succeed at migrations are distinguished by verification discipline, not architectural sophistication
Unverified assumptions discovered mid-execution are unrecoverable in a fixed-window migration; discovered in week one they cost a day
Avoidance of verification is driven by fear that answers will close doors — but the doors are already open or closed regardless

Committable now. (B) Full compliance-optimized migration — all 4 services extracted with audit and PCI QSA. Realistic at 9-12 months. Plannable but not committable on current information. (C) Original plan — not achievable given constraint set. The two-wave framing is the honest structure: Wave 1 commits to MVM, Wave 2 plans auth and payments extraction with information from Wave 1. Key accountability finding: seam analysis recommended cycle 2, schema inspection cycle 3, ORM coupling characterization cycle 5 — all remain unexecuted at cycle 9.
