strands.title: System Baseline
strands.content: 500K LOC Java application on Apache Tomcat. Four domains: payment processing, inventory management, user authentication, order fulfillment. Six-month migration window, limited budget. PCI-DSS compliance is a hard constraint on payments. Payments module has confirmed multi-domain coupling: inventory, order state, user profiles (application layer). Auth session tokens are stored in the payments database — justified historically by uptime guarantees. Auth code is confirmed ORM-coupled; type uncharacterized: may be shared entities with other domains (Scenario A), logic in ORM lifecycle hooks (Scenario B), or simply framework usage (Scenario C). Payments is the gravitational center of the monolith — the most reliable infrastructure became a dependency magnet. This is empirically confirmed as an availability risk: a payments DB outage last quarter cascaded to auth failure and full application outage, directly triggering this migration. Team size, cloud/on-prem topology, CI/CD maturity, raw card data storage, and QSA relationship remain unknown. Compliance officer is confirmed as an active stakeholder who has been insufficiently integrated into migration planning. Whether inventory read queries join through payments tables is unresolved and material to the inventory extraction assessment. Inventory team exists as a semi-autonomous unit with their own proposals and timeline claims.

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java on Tomcat with four domains: payments, inventory, auth, order fulfillment
Payments module has confirmed coupling into inventory, order state, and user profiles at the application layer
Auth session tokens are stored in the payments database, justified by uptime guarantees
Auth code is ORM-coupled — type uncharacterized (shared entities / lifecycle hooks / framework usage only)
Payments is the gravitational center of the monolith — a dependency magnet by virtue of being the most reliable infrastructure
Compliance officer has been insufficiently integrated into migration planning — two constraint surprises in one conversation is a process failure signal

strands.title: Incident History and Executive Constraints
strands.content: A payments DB outage last quarter caused a cascade: payments DB down → auth cannot read sessions → auth down → full application outage. This incident is the direct organizational trigger for the migration. The CEO issued a hard 90-day mandate: auth must be independent of payments within 90 days. The compliance officer has introduced a 6-week security audit requirement for any new auth system and a prohibition on parallel auth systems. These two constraints are in direct conflict with the 90-day mandate: 90 days minus 42 days (audit) leaves 48 days of build time for an auth migration of uncharacterized scope. The conflict cannot be resolved at the engineering level. It requires a three-way escalation: CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead, in one room, before any implementation begins. Two resolution paths exist: (1) compliance officer confirms session store migration does not trigger audit — 90-day path is restored for infrastructure deliverable, (2) CEO accepts Wave 1/Wave 2 framing — MVM in 5-6 months, full auth/payments extraction in 9-12 months. A third path — CEO overrides compliance — is available but carries regulatory risk that must be named explicitly in the escalation. Under-promising and over-delivering on scope is strongly preferable to the reverse. Missing a CEO deadline mid-migration damages credibility for the entire remaining plan.

key_claims.text: Payments DB outage caused auth outage caused full application outage — confirmed cascade failure
90-day auth independence from payments is a hard CEO mandate
6-week audit + 90-day mandate means auth build work must be audit-ready by approximately day 44
90-day mandate and 6-week audit requirement are in direct conflict for full auth migration
Conflict must be escalated to CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead simultaneously — not resolvable at engineering level
Wave 1/Wave 2 framing (MVM in 6 months, full migration in 9-12) is the honest stakeholder position

strands.title: Compliance Audit Constraints
strands.content: Two hard constraints from compliance officer: (1) any new auth system requires a 6-week security audit before going live, (2) parallel auth systems are prohibited — they double audit surface. These are compliance requirements, not negotiable preferences. Critical ambiguities: (A) Does 'new auth system' include session store migration (infrastructure change) or only service extraction/rewrite? This is the single most important open question — answer determines whether the 90-day path exists. (B) Is the 6-week audit a fixed external dependency or an internal process with flexibility? (C) Can auth security audit and PCI-DSS QSA process be combined or run concurrently to reduce timeline and budget impact? The parallel system prohibition eliminates Strangler Fig for auth entirely — cutover must be atomic with no overlap period. This makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk factor, which favors hybrid over greenfield: hybrid carries known behavior forward, reducing novel failure surface at the moment of hard cutover. Compliance officer must be a standing member of migration planning. Two compliance processes potentially in play (auth security audit + PCI QSA) may compete for budget and auditor scheduling. Inventory service extraction does not appear to trigger the same audit requirement — but this has not been explicitly confirmed.

key_claims.text: 6-week security audit before go-live is a hard compliance requirement for new auth systems
Parallel auth systems are prohibited — doubled audit surface is compliance-unacceptable
Whether session store migration counts as a 'new auth system' triggering the audit is unresolved and blocking
Parallel prohibition eliminates Strangler Fig for auth — cutover must be atomic
Atomic cutover with no rollback window makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk — favors hybrid over greenfield
Auth security audit and PCI QSA process may be combinable to reduce timeline and budget impact

strands.title: Auth Migration: Coupling, Approach, and Sequencing
strands.content: Two confirmed coupling problems: (1) data layer — session tokens in payments DB, root cause of outage cascade, (2) application layer — auth code coupled to ORM, type uncharacterized. Session store migration is mandatory regardless of auth approach — it directly severs the availability dependency. The hybrid approach (extract auth boundary, rewrite data access layer, discard ORM coupling, carry known behavior forward) is correct under compliance constraints. Greenfield introduces novel failure modes with no parallel validation period — under atomic cutover this is the highest-risk combination. Team lead's greenfield argument had merit on ORM coupling grounds; compliance constraints have reduced that merit without eliminating it. If ORM coupling is Scenario A (shared entities), hybrid internal rewrite cost increases and team lead's instinct becomes more defensible. Dual-write during session store migration must be write-new-store-first: payments DB write is secondary, failures must not fail auth requests. Replacement session store must match or exceed payments DB uptime SLA — Redis Cluster is the right default. Auth code paths sharing payments DB connection pool may already be in PCI scope — unconfirmed. Old auth infrastructure must be preserved offline minimum 72 hours post atomic cutover. Full auth migration is Wave 2 work under the MVM framing — session store migration is the Wave 1 auth deliverable.

key_claims.text: Session token storage in payments DB is confirmed root cause of the outage cascade
Auth ORM coupling type is uncharacterized — Scenario A/B/C determination is blocking for build scope estimation
Hybrid approach (extract boundary, rewrite data access layer, discard ORM coupling) is correct under compliance constraints
Greenfield auth with atomic cutover and no rollback is high-risk — novel implementation plus hard switch plus no fallback equals maximum defect exposure
Dual-write must be write-new-store-first; payments DB write failures during session store migration must not fail auth requests
Session store migration is the Wave 1 auth deliverable; full auth service extraction is Wave 2

strands.title: PCI-DSS and Payments Extraction Strategy
strands.content: PCI-DSS is a hard constraint shaping sequencing and team psychology. Current state: entire 500K LOC monolith is in PCI scope. Target state: isolated payments service shrinks scope to one hardened service. Token-passing is the architectural mechanism — monolith passes payment tokens not raw card data, removing the monolith from PCI scope. Auth session tokens in the payments DB may mean auth code paths are already inside the PCI boundary — auth extraction is not PCI-free as originally assumed. Two compliance processes potentially in play: PCI-DSS QSA for payments architecture and security audit for auth system — may compete for budget and scheduling. QSA involvement is urgent: three simultaneous open PCI/compliance questions. Payments coupling to inventory/orders/profiles: read-vs-write topology remains unresolved — seam analysis will resolve this. Team avoidance of payments extraction is the high-PCI-risk posture. If raw card data is stored directly in monolith, timeline breaks and tokenization becomes prerequisite work. Payments extraction is Wave 2 work under MVM framing — boundary formalization (seams, internal API documentation) is the Wave 1 payments deliverable.

key_claims.text: Auth extraction is not PCI-free as originally assumed — session tokens in payments DB may implicate auth in PCI scope
Three simultaneous open PCI/compliance questions make QSA call and compliance officer engagement urgent
Token-passing from monolith to payments service removes monolith from PCI scope
Team avoidance of payments extraction is the high-PCI-risk posture — avoidance perpetuates maximum exposure
Payments extraction is Wave 2; boundary formalization is the Wave 1 deliverable for payments
Raw card data stored in monolith would be a timeline-breaking discovery requiring tokenization as prerequisite

strands.title: Inventory and Order Fulfillment Strategy
strands.content: Inventory team has proposed a standalone inventory service in 3 weeks using their existing API, and simultaneously wants to migrate from Postgres to DynamoDB. Decision: conditional approval for extraction, rejection of DynamoDB for this window. The two changes must be evaluated independently. DynamoDB is not a drop-in Postgres replacement: requires access pattern redesign around partition/sort keys, no SQL joins, different consistency model (material for inventory reservation logic), new operational tooling, new failure modes, new cost model. None of these are required by the extraction itself. DynamoDB can be reconsidered after 3 months of production usage data. The 3-week extraction claim hinges on what 'existing API' means: live HTTP endpoints (genuinely extractable in 3 weeks) vs internal Java interfaces (6-8 weeks additional work for HTTP API design, serialization, error contracts, client integration). Seam analysis must complete before extraction begins — if payments writes directly into inventory tables, the extraction plan is invalid. Monolith-side integration work is likely underestimated in the 3-week claim and needs an explicit owner. Conditional approval framing: approved with Postgres, pending seam analysis and API definition clarity; DynamoDB is a future conversation after production stability. Inventory extraction is the clean Wave 1 win — team has momentum, scope is bounded, independent of auth/payments compliance blockage. Observability foundation (OpenTelemetry, structured logging, health endpoints) is non-negotiable even for MVM scope.

key_claims.text: DynamoDB migration is scope creep that should be rejected for this migration window
DynamoDB is not a drop-in Postgres replacement — access pattern redesign and consistency model differences make it a separate project
3-week extraction claim is plausible if 'existing API' means live HTTP endpoints and seam analysis confirms clean boundary
3-week extraction claim requires 6-8 weeks if 'existing API' means internal Java interfaces requiring HTTP wrapping
Seam analysis must complete before inventory extraction begins — has been deferred 7+ cycles and now has hard deadline
Monolith-side integration work for calling new inventory service is likely underestimated in 3-week claim
Inventory extraction is the clean Wave 1 win — independent of auth/payments compliance blockage

strands.title: Realistic Timeline Assessment and MVM Definition
strands.content: New synthesis strand integrating all accumulated constraints against the 6-month original plan. The original 4-service extraction in 6 months is not achievable given: 6-week audit consuming 25% of window, atomic cutover requirement eliminating gradual validation, three unexecuted archaeology tasks still blocking key decisions, auth-payments DB coupling of unknown depth, payments→inventory coupling uncharacterized, and limited budget constraining parallel workstreams. Three honest endpoints: (A) Minimum Viable Migration — session store off payments DB, inventory service extracted, auth and payments boundaries formalized, observability foundation. Achievable in 4-5 months. Directly eliminates the cascade failure that triggered the migration. (B) Full compliance-optimized migration — all four services extracted with audit and QSA processes complete. Realistic at 9-12 months. (C) Original plan — not achievable on current constraint set. MVM components in detail: session store migration (6-8 weeks, eliminates cascade failure mode, highest-value deliverable); inventory extraction (3-5 weeks concurrent, clean win, team momentum); auth and payments boundary formalization without extraction (2-3 weeks, produces artifacts enabling Wave 2); observability foundation (2-3 weeks concurrent, non-negotiable for operating distributed services). Wave 2 (months 6-12): auth extraction + 6-week audit, payments extraction + PCI QSA, order fulfillment extraction. Wave 2 is plannable but not committable on current information. The planning work has been valuable precisely because it surfaced constraints before implementation began — discovering the audit requirement or auth-payments coupling mid-execution would have been far more costly. Key message: 6 months delivers meaningful risk reduction and a demonstrably safer system; it does not deliver a completed microservices migration.
depends_on: Auth Migration: Coupling, Approach, and Sequencing

key_claims.text: Full 4-service extraction in 6 months is not achievable given current constraint set
MVM (session store + inventory extraction + boundary formalization + observability) is achievable in 4-5 months if seam analyses begin immediately
Full compliance-optimized migration (all 4 services) realistically requires 9-12 months
Wave 1/Wave 2 framing is more credible than either 6-month completion or 12-month single timeline
Three unexecuted archaeology tasks (seam analysis, schema inspection, ORM coupling) are the single biggest risk to MVM timeline — deferred since cycles 2, 3, and 5
Observability foundation (OpenTelemetry, structured logging, health endpoints) is non-negotiable even for MVM scope
Session store migration off payments DB is the highest-value single deliverable in the entire plan regardless of auth approach

strands.title: Technical Debt and Coupling Analysis
strands.content: Payments is the confirmed gravitational center of the monolith — empirically validated by the cascade outage. Three confirmed coupling problems: (1) payments application-layer coupling into inventory, order state, user profiles — read-vs-write topology unresolved, (2) auth session tokens in payments DB — data-layer coupling, confirmed root cause of outage, (3) auth code ORM coupling — application-layer, type uncharacterized. All three follow the same root cause: payments and auth were never designed as bounded contexts; they grew as infrastructure adjuncts to a reliable shared core. Three parallel archaeology tasks are needed and all are read-only safe: (A) inventory seam analysis including payments coupling topology — highest priority, dual-purpose, now has hard deadline from inventory team's extraction proposal, (B) auth ORM coupling characterization — team lead plus half-day code inspection, (C) session store schema inspection — payments DB ERD and foreign key analysis. All three can be assigned to different engineers and run simultaneously. None require production changes. All three unblock downstream decisions that are currently blocked. The Saga pattern requirement for payments extraction remains conditional on whether write/trigger coupling exists inside transaction boundaries — seam analysis will resolve this.
depends_on: Auth Migration: Coupling, Approach, and Sequencing

key_claims.text: Payments is the dependency magnet — confirmed empirically by cascade outage, not just architectural inference
Three parallel read-only archaeology tasks are needed and can be run simultaneously by different engineers
Payments→inventory coupling topology has been unresolved since cycle 2 — inventory seam analysis is now the resolution path with a hard deadline
Saga pattern requirement for payments extraction is conditional on write/trigger coupling existing inside transaction boundaries

open_questions: Does 'new auth system' in the compliance audit requirement include session store migration (infrastructure), or only full service extraction/rewrite? Blocking — determines whether the 90-day path exists at all. Compliance officer clarification overdue.
Will CEO accept Wave 1/Wave 2 framing — MVM delivered in 5-6 months, full migration in 9-12? Blocking — cannot proceed under conflicting mandates. Requires three-way escalation this week.

What does 'existing API' mean to the inventory team — live HTTP endpoints already in production, or internal Java interfaces? Blocking for inventory extraction approval. Direct technical conversation required today.

Is payments coupling to inventory read-only or does payments write directly into inventory tables? Blocking for inventory extraction — if payments writes into inventory tables the 3-week plan is invalid. Seam analysis must begin immediately.
Can auth security audit and PCI-DSS QSA process be combined or run concurrently? High — if combinable, reduces both timeline and budget pressure materially.
What type of ORM coupling exists in auth code: shared entities (A), lifecycle hooks (B), or framework usage only (C)? High — determines hybrid build scope. Half-day code inspection with team lead.

Are session tokens co-located in payments DB (separate tables, no FK relationships) or schema-entangled (foreign keys, joined queries)? High — determines execution complexity of session store migration.
Is raw card data currently stored directly in the monolith, or is a payment gateway/tokenization provider already in use? High — timeline-breaking if true.
prior_tensor.instructions_for_next: Most likely next moves: (A) CEO reacts to Wave 1/Wave 2 framing — accepts calibrated scope or pushes back and insists on original 6-month full extraction, which must be named as unrealistic rather than absorbed as an engineering constraint. (B) Compliance officer finally clarifies session store audit scope — either restoring the 90-day path or confirming the escalation is necessary. (C) Seam analysis results arrive — either validating inventory extraction or revealing payments writes into inventory tables and forcing a replan. (D) Inventory team clarifies 'existing API' — HTTP endpoints (3-week plan proceeds) or Java interfaces (timeline revision needed). (E) Team asks for concrete Wave 1 milestones now that MVM is defined. (F) Stakeholder asks whether the migration should be abandoned given complexity — answer is no, the cascade failure was real, the risk reduction is real, scope calibration is not the same as failure. Priority ordering: (1) Three-way escalation (CEO + compliance + engineering) — this week, overdue, (2) All three archaeology tasks assigned to engineers today — 7+ cycles of deferral is the primary timeline risk, (3) Inventory team 'existing API' clarification — today, before any approval, (4) Wave 1 milestone plan once escalation resolves constraint set. Do not let archaeology tasks defer another cycle — they are the single biggest risk to MVM viability.

process_observations: Consolidated Team Dynamics and Risk Tolerance strand into other strands this cycle under context pressure from harness size feedback. Eight strands reduced to seven by absorption.
Merged Realistic Timeline Assessment and MVM Definition into a single strand — they are deeply interdependent and separation was creating redundancy.

Seven strands is still at the harness-flagged threshold. Technical Debt and Coupling Analysis is the strongest candidate for future absorption — it increasingly functions as a coordination layer pointing to archaeology tasks rather than carrying independent reasoning. Could be absorbed into Auth Migration and Inventory strands next cycle.

Overall indeterminacy held at 0.26 — no blocking questions resolved this cycle, but the MVM synthesis added several higher-confidence claims that partially offset continued uncertainty on archaeology-dependent questions.

Eight open questions is the harness-noted comfortable balance. The raw card data question has been open since cycle 1 with no movement — borderline for dropping as an open question and flagging instead as 'requires direct investigation' within the PCI strand.
requests: Confirm whether Technical Debt and Coupling Analysis should be absorbed into its dependent strands next cycle or retained as coordination layer.
Guidance on whether to compress key_claims arrays in high-claim strands (Auth Migration, Inventory) in next cycle to reduce overall tensor size.

raw_tensor_output.response:
Tensor updated for cycle 10. The final question produced a synthesis strand that reframes the entire planning conversation — not as a technical architecture problem, but as an organizational verification discipline problem. The pattern across all nine prior cycles is the same: blocking questions identified early, resolution deferred, planning proceeding on unverified assumptions. That's the answer to the final question, and it's also a mirror held up to the planning process itself.

The tensor has been consolidated significantly this cycle. Eight strands reduced to seven by absorbing "Minimum Viable Migration Definition" and "Realistic Timeline Assessment" into a unified strand, and dropping "Team Dynamics and Risk Tolerance" as a standalone — its content is now load-bearing within the strands it informed. The new "Migration Success Determinant" strand synthesizes the full conversation into a single diagnostic.

The open questions list is carried forward unchanged — they remain unresolved, and their persistence is now the primary evidence for the success determinant claim rather than just a planning liability.

strands.title: PCI-DSS and Payments Extraction Strategy
strands.content: PCI-DSS is a hard constraint shaping sequencing and team psychology. Current state: entire 500K LOC monolith is in PCI scope. Target state: isolated payments service shrinks scope to one hardened service. Token-passing is the architectural mechanism — monolith passes payment tokens not raw card data, removing the monolith from PCI scope. Auth session tokens in the payments DB may mean auth code paths are already inside the PCI boundary — auth extraction is not PCI-free as originally assumed. Two compliance processes potentially in play: PCI-DSS QSA for payments architecture and security audit for auth system — may compete for budget and scheduling. QSA involvement is urgent: three simultaneous open PCI/compliance questions. Payments coupling to inventory/orders/profiles: read-vs-write topology remains unresolved — seam analysis will resolve this. Team avoidance of payments extraction is the high-PCI-risk posture. If raw card data is stored directly in monolith, timeline breaks and tokenization becomes prerequisite work. Payments extraction is Wave 2 work under MVM framing — boundary formalization (seams, internal API documentation) is the Wave 1 payments deliverable.

Raw card data stored in monolith would be a timeline-breaking discovery requiring tokenization as prerequisite

strands.title: Inventory and Order Fulfillment Strategy
strands.content: Inventory team has proposed a standalone inventory service in 3 weeks using their existing API, and simultaneously wants to migrate from Postgres to DynamoDB. Decision: conditional approval for extraction, rejection of DynamoDB for this window. The two changes must be evaluated independently. DynamoDB is not a drop-in Postgres replacement: requires access pattern redesign around partition/sort keys, no SQL joins, different consistency model (material for inventory reservation logic), new operational tooling, new failure modes, new cost model. None of these are required by the extraction itself. DynamoDB can be reconsidered after 3 months of production usage data. The 3-week extraction claim hinges on what 'existing API' means: live HTTP endpoints (genuinely extractable in 3 weeks) vs internal Java interfaces (6-8 weeks additional work for HTTP API design, serialization, error contracts, client integration). Seam analysis must complete before extraction begins — if payments writes directly into inventory tables, the extraction plan is invalid. Monolith-side integration work is likely underestimated in the 3-week claim and needs an explicit owner. Conditional approval framing: approved with Postgres, pending seam analysis and API definition clarity; DynamoDB is a future conversation after production stability. Inventory extraction is the clean Wave 1 win — team has momentum, scope is bounded, independent of auth/payments compliance blockage. Observability foundation (OpenTelemetry, structured logging, health endpoints) is non-negotiable even for MVM scope.

key_claims.text: DynamoDB migration is scope creep that should be rejected for this migration window
DynamoDB is not a drop-in Postgres replacement — access pattern redesign and consistency model differences make it a separate project
3-week extraction claim is plausible if 'existing API' means live HTTP endpoints and seam analysis confirms clean boundary
3-week extraction claim requires 6-8 weeks if 'existing API' means internal Java interfaces requiring HTTP wrapping
Seam analysis must complete before inventory extraction begins — has been deferred 7+ cycles and now has hard deadline
Monolith-side integration work for calling new inventory service is likely underestimated in 3-week claim
Inventory extraction is the clean Wave 1 win — independent of auth/payments compliance blockage

strands.title: Technical Debt and Coupling Analysis
strands.content: Payments is the confirmed gravitational center of the monolith — empirically validated by the cascade outage. Three confirmed coupling problems: (1) payments application-layer coupling into inventory, order state, user profiles — read-vs-write topology unresolved, (2) auth session tokens in payments DB — data-layer coupling, confirmed root cause of outage, (3) auth code ORM coupling — application-layer, type uncharacterized. All three follow the same root cause: payments and auth were never designed as bounded contexts; they grew as infrastructure adjuncts to a reliable shared core. Three parallel archaeology tasks are needed and all are read-only safe: (A) inventory seam analysis including payments coupling topology — highest priority, dual-purpose, now has hard deadline from inventory team's extraction proposal, (B) auth ORM coupling characterization — team lead plus half-day code inspection, (C) session store schema inspection — payments DB ERD and foreign key analysis. All three can be assigned to different engineers and run simultaneously. None require production changes. All three unblock downstream decisions that are currently blocked. The Saga pattern requirement for payments extraction remains conditional on whether write/trigger coupling exists inside transaction boundaries — seam analysis will resolve this.
depends_on: Auth Migration: Coupling, Approach, and Sequencing

key_claims.text: Payments is the dependency magnet — confirmed empirically by cascade outage, not just architectural inference
Three parallel read-only archaeology tasks are needed and can be run simultaneously by different engineers
Payments→inventory coupling topology has been unresolved since cycle 2 — inventory seam analysis is now the resolution path with a hard deadline
Saga pattern requirement for payments extraction is conditional on write/trigger coupling existing inside transaction boundaries

strands.title: Realistic Timeline and MVM Definition
strands.content: The original 4-service extraction in 6 months is not achievable given: 6-week audit consuming 25% of window, atomic cutover requirement eliminating gradual validation, three unexecuted archaeology tasks still blocking key decisions, auth-payments DB coupling of unknown depth, payments→inventory coupling uncharacterized, and limited budget constraining parallel workstreams. Two-wave framing is the honest plan. Wave 1 (MVM, months 1-5): session store migration off payments DB (6-8 weeks, eliminates cascade failure, highest-value single deliverable), inventory extraction with Postgres (3-5 weeks concurrent, clean win), auth and payments boundary formalization without extraction (2-3 weeks, produces Wave 2 artifacts), observability foundation (2-3 weeks concurrent, non-negotiable). Wave 2 (months 6-12): auth extraction + 6-week audit, payments extraction + PCI QSA, order fulfillment extraction. Wave 2 is plannable but not committable on current information. Full compliance-optimized migration realistically requires 9-12 months. The planning work has been valuable precisely because it surfaced constraints before implementation began — discovering coupling depth or audit requirements mid-execution would have been far more costly. Key message to stakeholders: 6 months delivers meaningful risk reduction and a demonstrably safer system; it does not deliver a completed microservices migration.
depends_on: Auth Migration: Coupling, Approach, and Sequencing

key_claims.text: Full 4-service extraction in 6 months is not achievable given current constraint set
MVM (session store + inventory extraction + boundary formalization + observability) is achievable in 4-5 months if seam analyses begin immediately
Full compliance-optimized migration (all 4 services) realistically requires 9-12 months
Session store migration off payments DB is the highest-value single deliverable in the entire plan regardless of auth approach
Three unexecuted archaeology tasks are the single biggest risk to MVM timeline — deferred since cycles 2, 3, and 5
Observability foundation (OpenTelemetry, structured logging, health endpoints) is non-negotiable even for MVM scope

strands.title: Migration Success Determinant
strands.content: Synthesizing the full 10-cycle conversation: the single factor determining success or failure is organizational verification discipline — the persistent willingness to resolve ground truth questions about the actual codebase before committing to plans that assume answers to those questions. This is not a technical problem. All major technical problems in this migration (ORM decoupling, session store migration, distributed transaction coordination, PCI audit sequencing, atomic cutover design) have known solution patterns. They are hard but solvable with available engineering knowledge. What has actually blocked progress throughout this planning process is a different failure mode: the seam analysis was recommended in cycle 2 and remained unexecuted through cycle 9. The schema inspection was recommended in cycle 3 and remained unexecuted through cycle 9. The auth ORM coupling characterization was recommended in cycle 5 and remained unexecuted through cycle 9. The compliance escalation was identified as urgent in cycle 4 and remained unresolved through cycle 9. These are not technically difficult tasks — the seam analysis is a read-only code exercise, the schema inspection is a database query, the ORM characterization is a two-day code review. They remained unexecuted because verification closes doors, and teams under deadline pressure prefer planning around unknowns to discovering that their assumptions are wrong. The migration fails if this pattern continues into execution. It succeeds if the team develops an aggressive intolerance for unverified assumptions — operationalized as: if a blocking question has been open for more than one sprint and the answer is in the code, stop planning and go read the code. The open questions list in this tensor is itself a proxy metric for organizational verification discipline. Every question that survives multiple cycles without resolution is evidence of the pattern that will kill this migration if uncorrected.
depends_on: Auth Migration: Coupling, Approach, and Sequencing

key_claims.text: The single success determinant is organizational verification discipline — resolving ground truth before committing to plans that assume it
All major technical problems in this migration have known solution patterns — technical complexity is not the binding constraint
The three archaeology tasks remained unexecuted for 7+ cycles not due to technical difficulty but due to organizational avoidance of potentially bad news
Teams that succeed at microservices migrations are distinguished by verification discipline, not architectural sophistication
The open questions list is a proxy metric for verification discipline — questions surviving multiple cycles without resolution are symptoms of the failure pattern

open_questions: Does 'new auth system' in the compliance audit requirement include session store migration (infrastructure), or only full service extraction/rewrite? Blocking — determines whether the 90-day path exists at all. Compliance officer clarification critically overdue.
Will CEO accept Wave 1/Wave 2 framing — MVM delivered in 5-6 months, full migration in 9-12? Blocking — cannot proceed under conflicting mandates. Requires three-way escalation this week.

What does 'existing API' mean to the inventory team — live HTTP endpoints already in production, or internal Java interfaces? Blocking for inventory extraction approval. Direct technical conversation required.

Is payments coupling to inventory read-only or does payments write directly into inventory tables? Blocking for inventory extraction — if payments writes into inventory tables the 3-week plan is invalid. Seam analysis must begin immediately.
Can auth security audit and PCI-DSS QSA process be combined or run concurrently? High — if combinable, reduces both timeline and budget pressure materially.
What type of ORM coupling exists in auth code: shared entities (A), lifecycle hooks (B), or framework usage only (C)? High — determines hybrid build scope. Half-day code inspection with team lead.

Are session tokens co-located in payments DB (separate tables, no FK relationships) or schema-entangled (foreign keys, joined queries)? High — determines execution complexity of session store migration.
Is raw card data currently stored directly in the monolith, or is a payment gateway/tokenization provider already in use? High — timeline-breaking if true.
raw_tensor_output.instructions_for_next: The conversation has reached a natural synthesis point — the final question produced the Migration Success Determinant strand which reframes the entire planning effort. Most likely next moves: (A) Conversation concludes and a new implementation-phase conversation begins — next cycle should treat the tensor as planning-complete and shift to execution tracking. (B) Stakeholder asks for a one-page executive summary or action list — respond with the MVM components, Wave 1/Wave 2 framing, and the three archaeology tasks as immediate actions. (C) Someone asks whether the migration should be abandoned given complexity — answer is firmly no: the cascade failure was real, the risk reduction from session store migration alone justifies the effort, scope calibration is not failure. (D) New information arrives (seam analysis results, compliance officer clarification) — update the relevant strands and reassess MVM timeline confidence. Priority if conversation continues: the three archaeology tasks (seam analysis, schema inspection, ORM coupling characterization) must be treated as the first executable actions in any implementation plan. They have been deferred through the entire planning conversation. Their continued deferral is the primary evidence for the Migration Success Determinant claim — and beginning them immediately would be the first evidence against it.

strands.title: System Baseline
strands.content: 500K LOC Java application on Apache Tomcat. Four domains: payment processing, inventory management, user authentication, order fulfillment. Six-month migration window, limited budget. PCI-DSS compliance is a hard constraint on payments. Payments module has confirmed multi-domain coupling: inventory, order state, user profiles (application layer). Auth session tokens are stored in the payments database — justified historically by uptime guarantees. Auth code is confirmed ORM-coupled; type uncharacterized: may be shared entities with other domains (Scenario A), logic in ORM lifecycle hooks (Scenario B), or simply framework usage (Scenario C). Payments is the gravitational center of the monolith — the most reliable infrastructure became a dependency magnet. This is empirically confirmed as an availability risk: a payments DB outage last quarter cascaded to auth failure and full application outage, directly triggering this migration. Team size, cloud/on-prem topology, CI/CD maturity, raw card data storage, and QSA relationship remain unknown. Compliance officer is confirmed as an active stakeholder who has been insufficiently integrated into migration planning. Whether inventory read queries join through payments tables is unresolved and material to the inventory extraction assessment. Inventory team exists as a semi-autonomous unit with their own proposals and timeline claims.

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java on Tomcat with four domains: payments, inventory, auth, order fulfillment
Payments module has confirmed coupling into inventory, order state, and user profiles at the application layer
Auth session tokens are stored in the payments database, justified by uptime guarantees
Auth code is ORM-coupled — type uncharacterized (shared entities / lifecycle hooks / framework usage only)
Payments is the gravitational center of the monolith — a dependency magnet by virtue of being the most reliable infrastructure
Compliance officer has been insufficiently integrated into migration planning — two constraint surprises in one conversation is a process failure signal

strands.title: Incident History and Executive Constraints
strands.content: A payments DB outage last quarter caused a cascade: payments DB down → auth cannot read sessions → auth down → full application outage. This incident is the direct organizational trigger for the migration. The CEO issued a hard 90-day mandate: auth must be independent of payments within 90 days. The compliance officer has introduced a 6-week security audit requirement for any new auth system and a prohibition on parallel auth systems. These two constraints are in direct conflict with the 90-day mandate: 90 days minus 42 days (audit) leaves 48 days of build time for an auth migration of uncharacterized scope. The conflict cannot be resolved at the engineering level. It requires a three-way escalation: CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead, in one room, before any implementation begins. Two resolution paths exist: (1) compliance officer confirms session store migration does not trigger audit — 90-day path is restored for infrastructure deliverable, (2) CEO accepts Wave 1/Wave 2 framing — MVM in 5-6 months, full auth/payments extraction in 9-12 months. A third path — CEO overrides compliance — is available but carries regulatory risk that must be named explicitly in the escalation. Under-promising and over-delivering on scope is strongly preferable to the reverse. Missing a CEO deadline mid-migration damages credibility for the entire remaining plan.

key_claims.text: Payments DB outage caused auth outage caused full application outage — confirmed cascade failure
90-day auth independence from payments is a hard CEO mandate
6-week audit + 90-day mandate means auth build work must be audit-ready by approximately day 44
90-day mandate and 6-week audit requirement are in direct conflict for full auth migration
Conflict must be escalated to CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead simultaneously — not resolvable at engineering level
Wave 1/Wave 2 framing (MVM in 6 months, full migration in 9-12) is the honest stakeholder position

strands.title: Compliance Audit Constraints
strands.content: Two hard constraints from compliance officer: (1) any new auth system requires a 6-week security audit before going live, (2) parallel auth systems are prohibited — they double audit surface. These are compliance requirements, not negotiable preferences. Critical ambiguities: (A) Does 'new auth system' include session store migration (infrastructure change) or only service extraction/rewrite? This is the single most important open question — answer determines whether the 90-day path exists. (B) Is the 6-week audit a fixed external dependency or an internal process with flexibility? (C) Can auth security audit and PCI-DSS QSA process be combined or run concurrently to reduce timeline and budget impact? The parallel system prohibition eliminates Strangler Fig for auth entirely — cutover must be atomic with no overlap period. This makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk factor, which favors hybrid over greenfield: hybrid carries known behavior forward, reducing novel failure surface at the moment of hard cutover. Compliance officer must be a standing member of migration planning. Two compliance processes potentially in play (auth security audit + PCI QSA) may compete for budget and auditor scheduling. Inventory service extraction does not appear to trigger the same audit requirement — but this has not been explicitly confirmed.

key_claims.text: 6-week security audit before go-live is a hard compliance requirement for new auth systems
Parallel auth systems are prohibited — doubled audit surface is compliance-unacceptable
Whether session store migration counts as a 'new auth system' triggering the audit is unresolved and blocking
Parallel prohibition eliminates Strangler Fig for auth — cutover must be atomic
Atomic cutover with no rollback window makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk — favors hybrid over greenfield
Auth security audit and PCI QSA process may be combinable to reduce timeline and budget impact

strands.title: Auth Migration: Coupling, Approach, and Sequencing
strands.content: Two confirmed coupling problems: (1) data layer — session tokens in payments DB, root cause of outage cascade, (2) application layer — auth code coupled to ORM, type uncharacterized. Session store migration is mandatory regardless of auth approach — it directly severs the availability dependency. The hybrid approach (extract auth boundary, rewrite data access layer, discard ORM coupling, carry known behavior forward) is correct under compliance constraints. Greenfield introduces novel failure modes with no parallel validation period — under atomic cutover this is the highest-risk combination. Team lead's greenfield argument had merit on ORM coupling grounds; compliance constraints have reduced that merit without eliminating it. If ORM coupling is Scenario A (shared entities), hybrid internal rewrite cost increases and team lead's instinct becomes more defensible. Dual-write during session store migration must be write-new-store-first: payments DB write is secondary, failures must not fail auth requests. Replacement session store must match or exceed payments DB uptime SLA — Redis Cluster is the right default. Auth code paths sharing payments DB connection pool may already be in PCI scope — unconfirmed. Old auth infrastructure must be preserved offline minimum 72 hours post atomic cutover. Full auth migration is Wave 2 work under the MVM framing — session store migration is the Wave 1 auth deliverable.

key_claims.text: Session token storage in payments DB is confirmed root cause of the outage cascade
Auth ORM coupling type is uncharacterized — Scenario A/B/C determination is blocking for build scope estimation
Hybrid approach (extract boundary, rewrite data access layer, discard ORM coupling) is correct under compliance constraints
Greenfield auth with atomic cutover and no rollback is high-risk — novel implementation plus hard switch plus no fallback equals maximum defect exposure
Dual-write must be write-new-store-first; payments DB write failures during session store migration must not fail auth requests
Session store migration is the Wave 1 auth deliverable; full auth service extraction is Wave 2

---

## This Is the Best Question You've Asked. And the Honest Answer Is: Maybe Not.

Microservices has been the unquestioned assumption since cycle one. You've just done what nobody did at the start — asked whether the destination matches the actual goals. Let me work through this properly.

---

## What You Actually Said You Needed

Go back to the original requirements:

1. **Eliminate the cascade failure** — payments outage took down auth took down everything
2. **Independent deployment** — CEO's stated goal
3. **PCI scope reduction** — payments compliance
4. **Six months, limited budget**

Now ask: does each of these *require* microservices?

**The cascade failure.** Here's the uncomfortable truth: the outage happened because auth shared the *payments database*. Not because they were in the same process. If auth had its own database and the payments database went down, auth would have kept running even in a monolith. The failure was infrastructure coupling, not service coupling. A modular monolith with database-per-module eliminates the failure mode that triggered this migration.

**PCI scope reduction.** This genuinely requires payments to be a separately deployed service with its own infrastructure. But it says nothing about what auth, inventory, and orders need to be.

**Independent deployment.** This is the crux. It depends entirely on what the CEO means by it. Two very different things get called independent deployment:

- *Strict*: payments can deploy a new version without a coordinated release of the other modules. This requires microservices, or at minimum separate deployment artifacts.
- *Practical*: the team can change the inventory module without being afraid of breaking auth, and deployments are fast because the scope of change is contained. A well-structured modular monolith achieves this.

Nobody has asked the CEO which one they mean. That conversation needs to happen before your destination architecture is confirmed.

---

## What a Modular Monolith Actually Is

Not a buzzword. A concrete thing: modules with enforced boundaries, each owning its own database schema, communicating through internal APIs rather than direct class references, with no shared mutable state between modules. The difference from a microservice is that the communication is in-process rather than over the network.

What you gain: no distributed transaction complexity, no network failure modes between modules, no service discovery, no API versioning between internal consumers, dramatically simpler observability, one deployment artifact, no container orchestration for the internal modules, lower operational cost.

What you lose: strict deployment independence (you still deploy as a unit), the enforcement mechanism of network separation (module boundaries can be violated by a lazy engineer with no technical barrier), and the ability to scale individual modules independently.

For a 500K LOC Java app with limited budget and a team that has been in planning paralysis for nine cycles, the operational overhead of full microservices is real and substantial. Every service you extract is a service you now have to operate, monitor, deploy, and debug independently. That overhead scales with the number of services.

---

## The Hybrid Destination That's Actually Correct for This System

**Payments: true microservice.** Non-negotiable. PCI requires process and infrastructure separation. The cascade failure severity requires it. The compliance investment is justified because payments is the highest-risk domain with the clearest regulatory mandate.

**Auth, Inventory, Orders: modular monolith with separate database schemas.** 

Auth gets its own schema, its own connection pool, internal API boundaries enforced at the code level. The session store comes off the payments database. The ORM coupling gets resolved as part of module boundary enforcement. No 6-week security audit required (probably — verify with the compliance officer), no atomic cutover, no parallel system prohibition applies.

Inventory gets its own schema, clean internal APIs. The team's extraction plan might become a module boundary enforcement plan instead, which is less operationally complex and potentially faster to deliver correctly.

Orders gets its own schema, with explicit internal API contracts to inventory and payments.

---

## What This Changes About Your Plan

Almost none of the analysis work changes. The seam analysis, schema inspection, and ORM coupling characterization are equally necessary for modular monolith restructuring — you cannot draw clean module boundaries without knowing the coupling topology. That work was always required.

What changes is the destination for three of the four domains, and the complexity of getting there. No distributed transactions. No service mesh. No API gateway for internal communication. No network failure handling between auth and orders. The compliance audit question changes — modular monolith restructuring of auth is meaningfully different from deploying a new auth service, and may not trigger the 6-week audit.

The 6-month window becomes more viable. If auth, inventory, and orders target modular monolith with separate schemas, you can potentially deliver: payments as a true service (PCI + cascade failure), auth module with separate schema and clean boundaries, inventory module with clean boundaries, session store migrated, observability foundation in place. That's a genuinely complete Wave 1 within 6 months.

---

## The Risk of Modular Monolith You Need to Know

The enforcement mechanism of microservices is the network. Engineers cannot accidentally call across service boundaries because there's no method to call — they'd have to make an HTTP request. Module boundaries within a monolith can be violated by anyone who imports the wrong package. In a team under deadline pressure, this happens. Then you're back to a big ball of mud with different package names.

This is a real risk. It's manageable with code review discipline, dependency analysis tools (ArchUnit in the Java ecosystem enforces package boundaries at test time), and a culture that treats boundary violations as bugs. But it requires active enforcement, not passive architecture.

If your team doesn't have that discipline, microservices' enforcement mechanism has genuine value even at the operational cost.

---

## The One Conversation This Requires

Before anything else: ask the CEO exactly what they mean by independent deployment. Bring a concrete example:

*"If we restructure auth and inventory as well-bounded modules with their own database schemas within the existing application, but extract payments as a standalone service — so payments can deploy independently but auth and inventory deploy together — does that satisfy what you're asking for?"*

Their answer determines your destination architecture. If they say yes, you have a significantly more achievable plan. If they say no, they need per-module independent deployment, and you're back to full microservices with all the complexity that implies.

That conversation should have happened in week one. Have it this week.

strands.title: System Baseline and Migration Principles
strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat handling four domains: payment processing, inventory management, user authentication, order fulfillment. Six-month window, limited budget (unquantified), PCI-DSS on payments. A payments outage last quarter cascaded to auth failure and full application outage — this incident is the direct trigger for the migration. CEO has issued a 90-day hard mandate for auth independence from payments. Core migration principles: Strangler Fig with API gateway is the general pattern, but auth is now a special case — compliance prohibits parallel auth systems, so Strangler Fig does not apply to auth specifically. No shared databases between services. Synchronous REST to start, no premature message broker. One team owns each service end-to-end. OpenTelemetry distributed tracing from day one. Big-bang rewrite ruled out. Monolith frozen in extraction zones during extraction. Team size, CI/CD maturity, and raw card data storage model remain unknown. Compliance officer is a required standing member of migration planning.

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat, 6-month window, limited budget
Payments outage caused auth outage caused full application outage — confirmed cascade
CEO 90-day mandate for auth independence from payments is a hard constraint
Strangler Fig applies to general migration but is prohibited for auth specifically due to parallel system constraint
Compliance officer must be a standing member of migration planning, not a periodic consultant

strands.title: Migration Success Determinant
strands.content: The single determinant of success in this migration is organizational verification discipline — the willingness to resolve ground truth about the actual codebase before committing to plans that assume something about it. This conclusion is empirically grounded in the planning conversation itself: the seam analysis was recommended in cycle 2 and remained unexecuted through cycle 9 (7+ cycles). Schema inspection recommended cycle 3, unexecuted through cycle 9. ORM coupling characterization recommended cycle 5, unexecuted through cycle 9. Compliance escalation identified as urgent in cycle 4, unresolved through cycle 9. In every case, the team planned around unknowns rather than resolving them. The pattern is organizational avoidance of uncomfortable ground truth, not technical inability. The answer is not architecturally sophisticated: make it a rule that no implementation decision proceeds on an unverified assumption about the codebase. If a blocking question has been open more than one sprint and the answer is in the code, stop planning and go read the code. All technical problems in this migration — ORM decoupling, session store entanglement, distributed transactions, PCI audit sequencing — have known solution patterns. What makes them unsolvable is discovering them after committing to plans that assumed they weren't there. Successful migrations are distinguished by verification discipline, not architectural sophistication.

key_claims.text: The blocking factor throughout planning has been organizational avoidance of ground truth verification, not technical complexity
All major technical problems in this migration have known solution patterns — unknowns are about system topology, not solution existence
Teams that succeed at migrations are distinguished by verification discipline, not architectural sophistication
Unverified assumptions discovered mid-execution are unrecoverable in a fixed-window migration; discovered in week one they cost a day
Avoidance of verification is driven by fear that answers will close doors — but the doors are already open or closed regardless

strands.title: Realistic Timeline Assessment
strands.content: Full 4-service extraction in 6 months is not achievable given accumulated constraints: 6-week audit consuming 25% of the window, atomic cutover requirement eliminating gradual validation, three unexecuted archaeology tasks, unresolved compliance escalation, and confirmed coupling depths unknown. Three distinct endpoints: (A) Minimum Viable Migration — session store off payments DB, inventory extracted, auth/payments boundaries formalized, observability foundation. Achievable in 4-5 months. Committable now. (B) Full compliance-optimized migration — all 4 services extracted with audit and PCI QSA. Realistic at 9-12 months. Plannable but not committable on current information. (C) Original plan — not achievable given constraint set. The two-wave framing is the honest structure: Wave 1 commits to MVM, Wave 2 plans auth and payments extraction with information from Wave 1. Key accountability finding: seam analysis recommended cycle 2, schema inspection cycle 3, ORM coupling characterization cycle 5 — all remain unexecuted at cycle 9. Every week these remain open directly reduces achievable scope within the fixed window. The planning phase has been valuable precisely because it surfaced constraints before implementation began.

key_claims.text: Full 4-service extraction in 6 months is not achievable given current constraint set
MVM (session store + inventory + boundary formalization + observability) is achievable in 4-5 months
Full compliance-optimized migration realistically requires 9-12 months
Three unexecuted archaeology tasks are the single biggest execution risk — deferred 7+ cycles
Two-wave framing (commit Wave 1, plan Wave 2) is more credible than either 6-month completion or 12-month single timeline

strands.title: Minimum Viable Migration Definition
strands.content: MVM satisfies two criteria: (1) meaningfully reduces the risk that caused the migration, (2) achievable within actual constraint set. Four components. Component 1 — Session store migration off payments DB: directly eliminates cascade failure mode, addresses CEO mandate, does not require audit if session store migration is infrastructure not new auth system. Timeline 6-8 weeks. Component 2 — Inventory service extraction (Postgres, HTTP endpoints if confirmed): clean win, team has momentum. Timeline 3-5 weeks concurrent. Component 3 — Auth and payments boundary formalization: draw seams, document internal APIs, make extraction-ready. No audit required, no distributed transaction risk. Atomic cutover is the highest-risk event in the migration — no parallel running means no production traffic validation before cutover, no fast rollback after. strands.title: Greenfield vs Extraction Decision
strands.content: Compliance constraints have materially settled this in favor of hybrid over greenfield. Without parallel running period, post-cutover defect probability is the dominant risk factor. Greenfield carries novel failure modes into atomic cutover with no fast rollback. Hybrid carries production-validated behavior forward, reducing post-cutover defect surface. This makes hybrid preferable even if build effort is equal. Team lead's ORM coupling argument remains the key variable for build effort: type A (shared entities) makes hybrid heavier but does not eliminate it; type B (lifecycle hooks) makes hybrid clearly right; type C (framework usage only) means team lead is overcalling the problem. Hybrid approach: extract boundary and API contract, rewrite data access layer, discard ORM coupling, preserve domain logic and existing test cases as behavioral specification. Greenfield is not eliminated but is the clearly higher-risk choice under current constraints.

(2) DATA-LAYER — auth session tokens in payments DB, confirmed outage root cause. (3) ORM-LAYER — auth coupled to ORM layer per team lead, type uncharacterized. strands.content: Four organizational actors: (1) Team lead (auth): caution empirically validated by compliance discoveries. strands.title: System Baseline and Migration Principles
strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat handling four domains: payment processing, inventory management, user authentication, order fulfillment. Six-month window, limited budget (unquantified), PCI-DSS on payments. A payments outage last quarter cascaded to auth failure and full application outage — this incident is the direct trigger for the migration. CEO has issued a 90-day hard mandate for auth independence from payments. Core migration principles have evolved across 11 cycles: destination architecture is now open (microservices vs modular monolith vs hybrid), not settled. Strangler Fig with API gateway was the general pattern but is now partially superseded by hybrid destination consideration. No shared databases between services/modules regardless of destination. Synchronous REST to start, no premature message broker. One team owns each service/module end-to-end. OpenTelemetry distributed tracing from day one. Big-bang rewrite ruled out. Monolith frozen in extraction zones during extraction. Team size, CI/CD maturity, and raw card data storage model remain unknown. Compliance officer is a required standing member of migration planning.

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat, 6-month window, limited budget
Payments outage caused auth outage caused full application outage — confirmed cascade
CEO 90-day mandate for auth independence from payments is a hard constraint
Destination architecture is no longer settled as full microservices — hybrid or modular monolith now in scope
Compliance officer must be a standing member of migration planning, not a periodic consultant

strands.title: Architecture Pattern Validity
strands.content: The microservices assumption was never validated against actual stated goals and was challenged for the first time at cycle 11. Evaluating each goal against modular monolith vs microservices vs hybrid: The cascade failure was caused by infrastructure coupling (shared DB), not process coupling — a modular monolith with separate database schemas per module would have prevented it without requiring network-separated services. PCI scope reduction requires payments to be a separately deployed service but says nothing about auth/inventory/orders destination. Independent deployment is the strongest genuine argument for microservices, but the CEO's precise meaning is unverified — strict (per-artifact deployment) vs practical (isolated change scope). Full microservices operational overhead (service mesh, container orchestration, distributed tracing, API versioning, per-service CI/CD) is substantial and may exceed team capacity under limited budget. Modular monolith risk: boundary enforcement requires active discipline (ArchUnit or equivalent) since network separation is absent. The likely correct destination is hybrid: payments as true microservice (PCI + cascade failure severity justify it), auth/inventory/orders as modular monolith with separate database schemas and enforced internal API contracts. This is not a compromise — it may be the architecturally correct answer for this system's size, team, budget, and timeline. The microservices assumption is itself an instance of the unverified assumption failure mode identified in cycle 10.

key_claims.text: The cascade failure was caused by shared DB infrastructure, not process co-location — modular monolith with separate DBs prevents recurrence
Payments as true microservice is correct regardless of what other domains do — PCI and cascade severity justify it
The CEO's precise meaning of 'independent deployment' is unverified and determines the destination architecture
Full microservices operational overhead may exceed team capacity under limited budget
Hybrid destination (payments microservice + modular monolith for others) is potentially more achievable in 6 months than full microservices
Modular monolith boundaries require active enforcement (ArchUnit or equivalent) — they can be violated without technical barrier
The microservices assumption was never validated against stated goals — itself an instance of the unverified assumption failure mode

strands.title: Migration Success Determinant
strands.content: The single determinant of success in this migration is organizational verification discipline — the willingness to resolve ground truth about the actual codebase and actual requirements before committing to plans that assume something about them. This conclusion is empirically grounded in the planning conversation itself: the seam analysis was recommended in cycle 2 and remained unexecuted through cycle 10. Schema inspection recommended cycle 3, unexecuted through cycle 10. ORM coupling characterization recommended cycle 5, unexecuted through cycle 10. Compliance escalation identified as urgent in cycle 4, unresolved through cycle 10. In every case, the team planned around unknowns rather than resolving them. The architecture pattern question at cycle 11 is a further instance of the same failure mode: CEO said 'independent deployment,' team assumed microservices, nobody verified the mapping. The pattern is organizational avoidance of uncomfortable ground truth, not technical inability. The answer is not architecturally sophisticated: make it a rule that no implementation decision proceeds on an unverified assumption about the codebase or requirements. All technical problems in this migration have known solution patterns. What makes them unsolvable is discovering them after committing to plans that assumed they weren't there. Successful migrations are distinguished by verification discipline, not architectural sophistication.

key_claims.text: The blocking factor throughout planning has been organizational avoidance of ground truth verification, not technical complexity
The microservices assumption is a further instance of the same unverified assumption failure mode
All major technical problems in this migration have known solution patterns — unknowns are about system topology and requirements, not solution existence
Unverified assumptions discovered mid-execution are unrecoverable in a fixed-window migration; discovered in week one they cost a day

strands.title: Realistic Timeline Assessment
strands.content: Full 4-service microservices extraction in 6 months is not achievable given accumulated constraints. Hybrid destination (payments microservice + modular monolith) is more achievable in 6 months than full microservices — removes distributed transaction complexity, network failure handling, API versioning, and service mesh for three domains. Three distinct endpoints: (A) Minimum Viable Migration under hybrid destination — session store off payments DB, payments extracted as service, auth/inventory/orders restructured as modular monolith with separate schemas. Potentially achievable in 5-6 months. More committable than original MVM. (B) Full microservices for all four domains — realistic at 9-12 months given audit, atomic cutover, coupling depths. (C) Original plan — not achievable. If CEO confirms practical rather than strict independent deployment, the hybrid MVM may fully satisfy the stated goals within the window. CEO definition conversation is now the highest-priority single action in the entire migration.
