strands.title: Inventory, Orders, and Realistic Timeline
strands.content: Consolidated strand covering inventory/orders strategy and timeline assessment. Inventory team proposed standalone inventory service in 3 weeks using existing API, simultaneously requesting Postgres→DynamoDB migration. Decision: conditional approval for extraction with Postgres, rejection of DynamoDB for this window. DynamoDB requires access pattern redesign, no SQL joins, different consistency model, new operational tooling, new failure modes — none required by extraction itself. Can be reconsidered after 3 months production usage. The 3-week claim hinges on 'existing API' meaning live HTTP endpoints vs internal Java interfaces. Seam analysis must complete before extraction begins — payments writes directly into inventory tables would invalidate the 3-week plan and change the consistency design requirements. Monolith-side integration work is likely underestimated. Under modular monolith destination, inventory extraction becomes module boundary enforcement — retains in-process communication, eliminates distributed transaction risk. Timeline honest assessment: full 4-service microservices extraction in 6 months is not achievable. Two-wave framing: Wave 1 (MVM, months 1-5) covers session store migration, inventory extraction, auth/payments boundary formalization, observability. Wave 2 (months 6-12) covers auth extraction + audit (if microservices destination), payments extraction + QSA. Under hybrid destination (payments microservice + modular monolith for others), the 6-month window may deliver a more complete outcome: session store migration, payments boundary and extraction groundwork, modular restructuring of auth/inventory/orders with separate schemas, observability. The three unexecuted archaeology tasks — seam analysis (overdue since cycle 2), schema inspection (overdue since cycle 3), ORM coupling characterization (overdue since cycle 5) — remain the single biggest execution risk. The seam analysis must now additionally answer whether payments and inventory share a transaction boundary, making it even more critical than before. Every week these remain unexecuted directly reduces achievable scope within the 6-month window. Observability foundation (OpenTelemetry, structured logging, health endpoints) is non-negotiable under any destination architecture.

3-week extraction claim is plausible if 'existing API' means live HTTP endpoints and seam analysis confirms clean boundary
Full 4-service microservices extraction in 6 months is not achievable given current constraint set
Hybrid destination may deliver more complete outcomes in 6 months than microservices MVM

strands.title: System Baseline
strands.content: 500K LOC Java application on Apache Tomcat. Four domains: payment processing, inventory management, user authentication, order fulfillment. Six-month migration window, limited budget. PCI-DSS compliance is a hard constraint on payments. Payments module has confirmed multi-domain coupling: inventory, order state, user profiles (application layer). Auth session tokens are stored in the payments database — justified historically by uptime guarantees. Auth code is confirmed ORM-coupled; type uncharacterized: may be shared entities with other domains (Scenario A), logic in ORM lifecycle hooks (Scenario B), or simply framework usage (Scenario C). Payments is the gravitational center of the monolith — the most reliable infrastructure became a dependency magnet. This is empirically confirmed as an availability risk: a payments DB outage last quarter cascaded to auth failure and full application outage, directly triggering this migration. Team size, cloud/on-prem topology, CI/CD maturity, raw card data storage, and QSA relationship remain unknown. Compliance officer is confirmed as an active stakeholder who has been insufficiently integrated into migration planning. Whether inventory read queries join through payments tables is unresolved and material to both inventory extraction assessment and saga design. Inventory team exists as a semi-autonomous unit with their own proposals and timeline claims. Microservices was the assumed destination from cycle 1 but was challenged at cycle 11 — hybrid destination (payments as microservice, others as modular monolith) is under active evaluation. Architecture pattern question remains open pending CEO definition of 'independent deployment.'

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java on Tomcat with four domains: payments, inventory, auth, order fulfillment
Payments module has confirmed coupling into inventory, order state, and user profiles at the application layer
Auth session tokens are stored in the payments database, justified by uptime guarantees
Auth code is ORM-coupled — type uncharacterized (shared entities / lifecycle hooks / framework usage only)
Payments is the gravitational center of the monolith — a dependency magnet by virtue of being the most reliable infrastructure

strands.title: Architecture Pattern Validity
strands.content: The microservices assumption has been challenged at cycle 11 and further refined at cycle 12 by the consistency analysis. Evaluating actual goals against modular monolith vs microservices: (1) Cascade failure elimination: caused by infrastructure coupling (shared DB), not process coupling. A modular monolith with database-per-module would have prevented it. Process separation was not the relevant variable. (2) Independent deployment: the strongest genuine argument for microservices. Strict independent deployment requires microservices or equivalent. Practical deployment safety is achievable in a well-structured modular monolith. CEO's precise meaning is unverified and blocking. (3) PCI scope reduction: requires payments to be a separately deployed service. Says nothing about what auth, inventory, and orders must be. (4) Consistency: new finding from cycle 12 — modular monolith with shared database (separate schemas, same instance) preserves ACID transaction boundary spanning payments and inventory. If payments and inventory currently share a transaction boundary in the monolith, microservices extraction genuinely removes a consistency guarantee that modular monolith preserves. This is a material advantage of modular monolith over microservices for the payments+inventory consistency problem specifically. (5) Six months, limited budget: modular monolith is substantially cheaper to operate — no service mesh, no container orchestration, no distributed tracing required for in-process modules, no API versioning between internal consumers. The hybrid destination remains likely correct: payments as true microservice (PCI + cascade severity + isolation), auth/inventory/orders as modular monolith with separate database schemas and enforced internal API boundaries. Key risk of modular monolith: network separation enforces microservice boundaries automatically; module boundaries within a monolith require active enforcement (ArchUnit or equivalent). The microservices assumption is itself an instance of the unverified assumption failure mode — CEO said 'independent deployment,' team assumed microservices, the mapping was never validated.

key_claims.text: The cascade failure was caused by infrastructure coupling (shared DB), not process coupling — a modular monolith with separate DBs would have prevented it
Modular monolith with shared database preserves ACID transaction boundary spanning payments and inventory — a consistency advantage over microservices
PCI scope reduction requires payments as a true separate service regardless of what other domains do
The CEO's definition of independent deployment has never been verified — strict vs practical are meaningfully different requirements
Hybrid destination (payments as microservice, auth/inventory/orders as modular monolith) is potentially the architecturally correct answer for this system
Modular monolith boundaries require active enforcement (ArchUnit or equivalent) — network separation is not available as the enforcement mechanism

strands.title: Incident History and Executive Constraints
strands.content: A payments DB outage last quarter caused a cascade: payments DB down → auth cannot read sessions → auth down → full application outage. This incident is the direct organizational trigger for the migration. The CEO issued a hard 90-day mandate: auth must be independent of payments within 90 days. The compliance officer introduced a 6-week security audit requirement for any new auth system and a prohibition on parallel auth systems. These constraints are in direct conflict with the 90-day mandate: 90 days minus 42 days (audit) leaves 48 days of build time for an auth migration of uncharacterized scope. The conflict requires three-way escalation: CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead, before implementation begins. Two resolution paths: (1) compliance officer confirms session store migration does not trigger audit — 90-day path restored for infrastructure deliverable, (2) CEO accepts Wave 1/Wave 2 framing. A third path — CEO overrides compliance — carries regulatory risk that must be named explicitly. Architecture pattern question adds a fourth path: modular monolith restructuring of auth may not trigger the audit requirement at all, potentially dissolving the conflict. The CEO's definition of independent deployment must be verified — if practical deployment safety satisfies the goal, modular monolith may satisfy the mandate without triggering audit.

key_claims.text: Payments DB outage caused auth outage caused full application outage — confirmed cascade failure
90-day auth independence from payments is a hard CEO mandate
90-day mandate and 6-week audit requirement are in direct conflict for full auth service extraction
Conflict must be escalated to CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead simultaneously — not resolvable at engineering level
Modular monolith restructuring of auth may not trigger the 6-week audit, potentially dissolving the CEO/compliance conflict

strands.title: Compliance Audit Constraints
strands.content: Two hard constraints from compliance officer: (1) any new auth system requires a 6-week security audit before going live, (2) parallel auth systems are prohibited — they double audit surface. These are compliance requirements, not negotiable preferences. The parallel system prohibition eliminates Strangler Fig for auth entirely — cutover must be atomic. This makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk factor, which favors hybrid extraction over greenfield: hybrid carries known behavior forward, reducing novel failure surface at atomic cutover. Critical ambiguities: (A) Does 'new auth system' include session store migration (infrastructure) or only service extraction/rewrite? (B) Does modular monolith restructuring of auth count as a 'new auth system'? This is a high-value clarification — if modular monolith is exempt, the CEO/compliance conflict may dissolve. (C) Is the 6-week audit a fixed external dependency or an internal process with flexibility? (D) Can auth security audit and PCI-DSS QSA process be combined? Two compliance processes potentially in play (auth security audit + PCI QSA) may compete for budget and auditor scheduling. Compliance officer must answer three questions in a single call: session store audit trigger, modular monolith audit trigger, and audit/QSA combination possibility.

key_claims.text: 6-week security audit before go-live is a hard compliance requirement for new auth systems
Parallel auth systems are prohibited — doubled audit surface is compliance-unacceptable
Whether session store migration counts as a 'new auth system' triggering the audit is unresolved and blocking
Whether modular monolith restructuring of auth triggers the audit is unresolved with high upside if answered favorably
Atomic cutover with no rollback window makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk — favors hybrid over greenfield

strands.title: Auth Migration: Coupling, Approach, and Sequencing
strands.content: Two confirmed coupling problems: (1) data layer — session tokens in payments DB, root cause of outage cascade, (2) application layer — auth code coupled to ORM, type uncharacterized. Session store migration is mandatory regardless of auth approach — it directly severs the availability dependency. The hybrid approach (extract auth boundary, rewrite data access layer, discard ORM coupling, carry known behavior forward) remains correct for a microservices destination. For a modular monolith destination, the equivalent is: enforce module boundary within monolith, migrate session store to separate schema, rewrite data access layer without ORM coupling to other domains. Under the modular monolith path: likely no 6-week audit, no atomic cutover risk, no parallel system prohibition problem — significant simplification. The greenfield vs hybrid debate is resolved in favor of hybrid under compliance constraints: atomic cutover with no rollback makes 'known behavior' more valuable than 'clean implementation.' Greenfield has novel failure modes; hybrid carries known failure modes. Under deadline pressure, known is safer. Dual-write during session store migration must be write-new-store-first: payments DB write is secondary, failures must not fail auth requests. Replacement session store must match or exceed payments DB uptime SLA — Redis Cluster is the right default. ORM coupling type (Scenario A/B/C) remains uncharacterized and affects build scope under either destination architecture.

key_claims.text: Session token storage in payments DB is confirmed root cause of the outage cascade
Auth ORM coupling type is uncharacterized — Scenario A/B/C determination is blocking for build scope estimation under any destination architecture
Hybrid approach is favored over greenfield specifically because atomic cutover with no rollback makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk
Modular monolith destination for auth eliminates atomic cutover risk and likely eliminates audit trigger — significant simplification over microservices path
Dual-write must be write-new-store-first; payments DB write failures during session store migration must not fail auth requests

strands.title: PCI-DSS and Payments Extraction Strategy
strands.content: PCI-DSS is a hard constraint unaffected by the modular monolith question — payments must be extracted as a true service regardless of what other domains do. Current state: entire 500K LOC monolith is in PCI scope. Target state: isolated payments service shrinks scope to one hardened service. Token-passing is the architectural mechanism — monolith passes payment tokens not raw card data. Auth session tokens in the payments DB may mean auth code paths are already inside the PCI boundary — auth extraction is not PCI-free as originally assumed. Under modular monolith destination, auth module boundary enforcement and session store migration resolve the co-location concern without full service extraction. Two compliance processes potentially in play: PCI-DSS QSA for payments architecture and security audit for auth system. QSA involvement is urgent. Payments extraction now has an additional prerequisite from cycle 12: saga pattern design must precede payments extraction, not follow it. The outbox pattern and saga orchestrator must be designed before payments service extraction begins — you cannot retrofit distributed coordination onto an already-extracted service. Raw card data storage question remains open and is timeline-breaking if true.

key_claims.text: Payments must be extracted as a true microservice regardless of modular monolith consideration for other domains — PCI requires it
Auth extraction is not PCI-free as originally assumed — session tokens in payments DB may implicate auth in PCI scope
Saga pattern design is a prerequisite for payments extraction, not a post-extraction concern
Team avoidance of payments extraction perpetuates maximum PCI exposure

strands.title: Distributed Consistency and Saga Patterns
strands.content: The payments team's position — cannot accept eventual consistency for financial transactions — is correct but misapplied. It conflates two distinct consistency requirements: (1) consistency of the payment record itself (did this charge succeed?) — must be strongly consistent, is a LOCAL concern within the payments service, fully achievable with local ACID transactions, (2) consistency between payment state and inventory state — this is the distributed coordination problem and does not require distributed ACID to solve correctly. The monolith does not provide the distributed consistency guarantee it appears to: the payment gateway API call is a network call outside the transaction boundary. Application crashes between steps create inconsistency even in the monolith. Major financial networks (SWIFT, ACH, card networks) operate under eventual consistency with compensation, not distributed ACID. Four patterns required for correct distributed financial coordination: (A) Outbox pattern — payment record and outbox event written in the same local ACID transaction. Relay process publishes event after commit. Guarantees event publication if and only if payment committed. Foundation for all other patterns. (B) Orchestration-based saga — preferred over choreography for financial workflows because the full workflow is visible in one place, compensating transactions are explicit, and audit trails are straightforward. Saga steps: ReserveInventory → ChargePayment → ConfirmOrder. Compensation flows backward: ConfirmOrder failure → RefundPayment + ReleaseInventoryReservation. Reserve inventory BEFORE charging payment — prevents charging without fulfillment capability. (C) Idempotency — all payment operations must be idempotent via client-supplied idempotency keys. Non-negotiable. Prevents double-charging on network retry. Cannot be retrofitted. (D) Reconciliation jobs — detect and heal inconsistencies that fall outside saga compensation logic. Complement to sagas, not alternative. Run at interval appropriate to business tolerance. Seam analysis must now answer an additional question: do payments and inventory currently share a database transaction boundary? If yes, microservices extraction genuinely removes a consistency guarantee that modular monolith with shared database preserves. This finding materially affects destination architecture recommendation.

key_claims.text: Strong consistency is required within the payments service locally — not across service boundaries
The monolith does not provide true distributed consistency — payment gateway call is outside transaction boundary, application crashes create inconsistency
Major financial networks operate under eventual consistency with compensation, not distributed ACID
Outbox pattern guarantees event publication if and only if the local transaction commits
Idempotency for all payment operations is non-negotiable — prevents double-charging on retry, cannot be retrofitted
Orchestration-based saga is preferable to choreography for financial workflows due to auditability and explicit compensation
Whether payments and inventory currently share a transaction boundary is the new critical seam analysis question — answer affects destination architecture recommendation

Full 4-service microservices extraction in 6 months is not achievable given current constraint set

---

## Four Things I Got Wrong, and Why.

This is a fair question and it deserves a direct answer, not a diplomatic one.

---

## 1. I Was Wrong About Auth Having Clean Boundaries

In cycle one I said auth extraction should go first because auth has "relatively clean boundaries in most Java monoliths." That was category reasoning — how auth services usually look — applied to your specific system without verification.

Your auth service stores session tokens in the payments database. That's not a clean boundary. That's one of the messier coupling patterns I've seen. When that came out in cycle three, the entire sequencing recommendation had to be rebuilt.

The tell was the phrase "in most Java monoliths." That's a red flag in architectural advice. Most is not your system. I should have said: auth is often well-bounded, but verify the session store location and ORM dependencies before treating it as the easy first extraction. I said it confidently when I should have said it conditionally.

---

## 2. I Should Have Questioned Microservices in Cycle One, Not Cycle Eleven

This one bothers me most. The question you asked in cycle eleven — "is microservices even the right answer?" — is a first principles question that should be asked before any sequencing discussion. Instead I spent ten cycles planning an extraction in a destination I never verified was correct.

The cascade failure that triggered this migration happened because auth shared the payments *database*. That's infrastructure coupling. A modular monolith with database-per-module would have prevented it. The failure mode had nothing to do with being in the same process.

I didn't question the microservices assumption because you came in with it framed as the goal, and I accepted the frame. A better response in cycle one would have been: before we plan the extraction sequence, let's confirm microservices is the right destination. Does independent deployment mean per-service deployment artifacts, or faster and safer deployment of isolated components? Those are different things with different architectural implications.

Ten cycles of planning against a destination that may have been wrong from the start. That's a real failure of the initial response.

---

## 3. The Month-by-Month Plan in Cycle One Was Overconfident

I gave you a confident schedule — auth months one and two, payments months two through four, inventory deferred — without any of the information required to make that schedule credible. No coupling topology data. No compliance constraints. No knowledge of the session store location. No team capability assessment.

The plan wasn't wrong because the facts were unknown. It was wrong because I presented it with confidence appropriate to a verified plan, not a hypothesis contingent on archaeology. A more honest version would have looked like: here is the provisional shape of the plan, and here are the five things that need to be verified before any of these timelines are committable.

By cycle nine that plan was formally superseded. The month-by-month schedule I gave in cycle one has essentially zero resemblance to the realistic timeline we arrived at through the conversation. That should have been foreseeable from the information available at cycle one.

---

## 4. I Misread the Team's Anxiety as a Management Problem

In cycle two I framed the team's nervousness about touching payments as avoidance behavior — something to be redirected with the right argument. I said their anxiety was "about PCI compliance overhead, not the tangle itself" and that the compliance argument "cuts the opposite direction from how the team is reading it."

Then in cycle four you told me that payments had already caused a production outage. The entire application went down. The team's nervousness was not misplaced anxiety about a theoretical risk. It was a reasonable response to something that had already happened to them.

I treated their signal as noise to be overcome. It was information to be understood. The team lead's later argument that the auth code needs a rewrite, the team's caution about payments coupling — these were people who knew their system telling me something true about it. I was too quick to redirect rather than listen.

---

## The Pattern Underneath All Four

Every reversal has the same structure: I made a confident claim based on how these systems usually work, and then your specific system turned out to be different from the usual.

Auth usually has clean boundaries — yours didn't. Microservices is usually the right migration destination — for your goals and constraints, maybe not. Six months is usually enough for this scope — not with a 6-week audit requirement and three unresolved coupling questions. Team anxiety is usually overcaution — yours was empirically grounded.

That's the thing about systems that have been running in production for years and accumulated real technical debt: they are not usual. They are specifically weird in the ways that matter most, and the specific weirdness is exactly what doesn't show up in category reasoning.

The conversation got better every time a new specific fact arrived. The initial recommendations were the weakest part. Which is an argument for the conclusion we reached in cycle ten: verify before committing, and treat confident architectural advice given without system-specific verification as a starting hypothesis, not a plan.

strands.title: System Baseline and Migration Principles
strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat handling four domains: payment processing, inventory management, user authentication, order fulfillment. Six-month window, limited budget (unquantified), PCI-DSS on payments. A payments outage last quarter cascaded to auth failure and full application outage — this incident is the direct trigger for the migration. CEO has issued a 90-day hard mandate for auth independence from payments. Core migration principles have evolved across 12 cycles: destination architecture is now open (microservices vs modular monolith vs hybrid), not settled. Strangler Fig with API gateway was the general pattern but is now partially superseded by hybrid destination consideration. No shared databases between services/modules regardless of destination. Synchronous REST to start, no premature message broker — except that saga/outbox pattern for payments extraction now requires a message broker decision. One team owns each service/module end-to-end. OpenTelemetry distributed tracing from day one. Big-bang rewrite ruled out. Monolith frozen in extraction zones during extraction. Team size, CI/CD maturity, and raw card data storage model remain unknown. Compliance officer is a required standing member of migration planning.

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat, 6-month window, limited budget
Payments outage caused auth outage caused full application outage — confirmed cascade
CEO 90-day mandate for auth independence from payments is a hard constraint
Destination architecture is no longer settled as full microservices — hybrid or modular monolith now in scope
Compliance officer must be a standing member of migration planning, not a periodic consultant

strands.title: Architecture Pattern Validity
strands.content: The microservices assumption was never validated against actual stated goals and was challenged at cycle 11. The consistency question (cycle 12) adds a new dimension: modular monolith with shared database allows local ACID transactions spanning payments and inventory schemas, which is a genuine consistency advantage over microservices for these two domains. Evaluating each goal: The cascade failure was caused by infrastructure coupling (shared DB), not process coupling — a modular monolith with separate database schemas would have prevented it. PCI scope reduction requires payments to be a separately deployed service but says nothing about auth/inventory/orders destination. Independent deployment is the strongest genuine argument for microservices, but the CEO's precise meaning is unverified. Full microservices operational overhead is substantial and may exceed team capacity. Modular monolith risk: boundary enforcement requires active discipline (ArchUnit) since network separation is absent. The consistency question partially favors modular monolith for payments+inventory specifically: if they currently share a ACID transaction boundary, extracting payments as a microservice removes that guarantee and requires saga coordination to replace it. If the team lacks saga implementation confidence, modular monolith with shared database preserves the guarantee at the cost of deployment independence. The likely correct destination remains hybrid: payments as true microservice (PCI + cascade failure severity justify it), auth/inventory/orders as modular monolith with separate database schemas. But the saga complexity of payments extraction is now a confirmed prerequisite cost that must be accounted for regardless.

key_claims.text: The cascade failure was caused by shared DB infrastructure, not process co-location — modular monolith with separate DBs prevents recurrence
Payments as true microservice is correct regardless of what other domains do — PCI and cascade severity justify it
Modular monolith with shared database allows ACID transaction spanning payments and inventory schemas — genuine consistency advantage over microservices
The CEO's precise meaning of 'independent deployment' is unverified and determines the destination architecture
Hybrid destination (payments microservice + modular monolith for others) is potentially more achievable in 6 months than full microservices
The microservices assumption was never validated against stated goals — itself an instance of the unverified assumption failure mode

strands.title: Distributed Consistency and Saga Patterns
strands.content: The payments team's concern about eventual consistency is correct in principle but misapplied in scope. Strong consistency is required within the payments service's local transaction boundary — not across service boundaries. Financial systems globally (SWIFT, ACH, card networks) operate under eventual consistency with compensation, not distributed ACID. The monolith does not actually provide the strong cross-domain consistency it appears to provide: the payment gateway API call is a network call outside the database transaction, meaning the failure mode of 'card charged but application crashed before commit' exists in the monolith today — it is just invisible. Key patterns required for payments extraction: (1) Outbox pattern — payment record and outbox event written in the same local ACID transaction; relay process publishes to broker. Guarantees event publication if and only if payment committed. (2) Orchestration-based saga preferred over choreography for financial workflows — explicit workflow state, visible compensation logic, auditable. Compensation order: reserve inventory first, then charge payment, then confirm order. Reverse compensation on failure. (3) Idempotency for all payment operations — non-negotiable. X-Idempotency-Key header on every request; payment service checks before processing; prevents double-charging on retry. (4) Reconciliation jobs — detect and heal inconsistencies outside designed saga paths; run hourly or nightly; complement to saga correctness, not alternative. Critical new sequencing constraint: saga pattern implementation is a prerequisite for payments extraction, not a post-extraction concern. The seam analysis must now answer an additional question: do payments and inventory currently share a single ACID transaction boundary? If yes, extraction removes a real guarantee that saga patterns must replace. If no, the current system already has the eventual consistency exposure and saga is a pure improvement. The payments team must be shown the current monolith failure modes (gateway call outside transaction, crash-after-commit scenarios) to understand that they do not have the consistency guarantee they believe they have.

key_claims.text: Strong consistency is required within the payments service local transaction, not across service boundaries
The monolith payment gateway API call is outside the database transaction — eventual consistency exposure exists today
Major financial networks operate under eventual consistency with compensation, not distributed ACID
Outbox pattern guarantees event publication if and only if local transaction commits
Orchestration-based saga is preferable to choreography for financial workflows due to auditability and explicit compensation logic
Idempotency for all payment operations is non-negotiable — prevents double-charging on retry
Saga pattern implementation is a prerequisite for payments extraction, not a post-extraction concern
Whether payments and inventory currently share a single ACID transaction boundary is a new critical unknown from the seam analysis

strands.title: Migration Success Determinant
strands.content: The single determinant of success in this migration is organizational verification discipline — the willingness to resolve ground truth about the actual codebase and actual requirements before committing to plans that assume something about them. This conclusion is empirically grounded in the planning conversation itself: the seam analysis was recommended in cycle 2 and remained unexecuted through cycle 11. Schema inspection recommended cycle 3, unexecuted through cycle 11. ORM coupling characterization recommended cycle 5, unexecuted through cycle 11. Compliance escalation identified as urgent in cycle 4, unresolved through cycle 11. In every case, the team planned around unknowns rather than resolving them. The architecture pattern question at cycle 11 is a further instance of the same failure mode: CEO said 'independent deployment,' team assumed microservices, nobody verified the mapping. The consistency question at cycle 12 adds another instance: the payments team believes they have strong consistency guarantees today that they may not actually have — nobody has verified whether the payment gateway call is inside or outside the transaction boundary. The pattern is organizational avoidance of uncomfortable ground truth, not technical inability. Successful migrations are distinguished by verification discipline, not architectural sophistication. The seam analysis now has an additional required output: transaction boundary scope for payments+inventory.

strands.title: Realistic Timeline Assessment
strands.content: Full 4-service microservices extraction in 6 months is not achievable given accumulated constraints. Hybrid destination (payments microservice + modular monolith) is more achievable in 6 months than full microservices — removes distributed transaction complexity, network failure handling, API versioning, and service mesh for three domains. Saga pattern implementation for payments extraction is now confirmed as a prerequisite cost that adds to payments extraction scope regardless of destination decision for other domains. Three distinct endpoints: (A) Minimum Viable Migration under hybrid destination — session store off payments DB, payments extracted as service with saga/outbox/idempotency, auth/inventory/orders restructured as modular monolith with separate schemas. Potentially achievable in 5-6 months but saga prerequisite adds scope to payments work. (B) Full microservices for all four domains — realistic at 9-12 months given audit, atomic cutover, coupling depths, and now saga implementation for all inter-service coordination. (C) Original plan — not achievable. CEO definition conversation remains highest-priority single action. Saga design must begin before payments extraction, not after.

strands.title: System Baseline and Migration Principles
strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat handling four domains: payment processing, inventory management, user authentication, order fulfillment. Six-month window, limited budget (unquantified), PCI-DSS on payments. A payments outage last quarter cascaded to auth failure and full application outage — this incident is the direct trigger for the migration. CEO has issued a 90-day hard mandate for auth independence from payments. Core migration principles have evolved across 13 cycles: destination architecture is now open (microservices vs modular monolith vs hybrid), not settled. Strangler Fig with API gateway was the general pattern but is now partially superseded by hybrid destination consideration. No shared databases between services/modules regardless of destination. Synchronous REST to start, no premature message broker — except that saga/outbox pattern for payments extraction now requires a message broker decision. One team owns each service/module end-to-end. OpenTelemetry distributed tracing from day one. Big-bang rewrite ruled out. Monolith frozen in extraction zones during extraction. Team size, CI/CD maturity, and raw card data storage model remain unknown. Compliance officer is a required standing member of migration planning.

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat, 6-month window, limited budget
Payments outage caused auth outage caused full application outage — confirmed cascade

strands.title: Architecture Pattern Validity
strands.content: The microservices assumption was never validated against actual stated goals and was challenged at cycle 11. The consistency question (cycle 12) adds a new dimension: modular monolith with shared database allows local ACID transactions spanning payments and inventory schemas, which is a genuine consistency advantage over microservices for these two domains. Evaluating each goal: The cascade failure was caused by infrastructure coupling (shared DB), not process coupling — a modular monolith with separate database schemas would have prevented it. PCI scope reduction requires payments to be a separately deployed service but says nothing about auth/inventory/orders destination. Independent deployment is the strongest genuine argument for microservices, but the CEO's precise meaning is unverified. Full microservices operational overhead is substantial and may exceed team capacity. Modular monolith risk: boundary enforcement requires active discipline (ArchUnit) since network separation is absent. The consistency question partially favors modular monolith for payments+inventory specifically. The likely correct destination remains hybrid: payments as true microservice (PCI + cascade failure severity justify it), auth/inventory/orders as modular monolith with separate database schemas. The saga complexity of payments extraction is a confirmed prerequisite cost regardless. Epistemic revision (cycle 13): the microservices assumption should have been interrogated in cycle 1 — its persistence through cycle 11 without challenge was a planning failure.

The CEO's precise meaning of 'independent deployment' is unverified and determines the destination architecture

strands.title: Epistemic Revision Log
strands.content: Retrospective strand initialized cycle 13. Four confirmed positions from cycle 1 that were materially wrong and required revision: (1) Auth-first sequencing — recommended on grounds that auth has 'clean boundaries in most Java monoliths.' Category reasoning applied without system-specific verification. Invalidated cycle 3 when session tokens in payments DB were discovered. The tell was the phrase 'in most Java monoliths' — a red flag for generalization substituting for verification. (2) Microservices as unquestioned destination — accepted the framing from cycle 1 without interrogating whether it satisfied actual stated goals. Should have been a cycle 1 question. Not challenged until cycle 11 by the user. The cascade failure was infrastructure coupling not process coupling — a modular monolith would have prevented it. (3) Month-by-month plan in cycle 1 was overconfident — no coupling topology data, no compliance constraints, no session store location known. Presented with confidence appropriate to a verified plan when it was a hypothesis contingent on unexecuted archaeology. By cycle 9 it was formally superseded with essentially zero resemblance to the realistic timeline. (4) Team anxiety misread as a management problem — framed in cycle 2 as avoidance behavior to redirect. Cycle 4 revealed the cascade failure had already happened. The team's nervousness was an empirically grounded signal about a real system that had already failed, not overcaution. Underlying pattern across all four reversals: confident claims based on category patterns (how these systems usually look) that were wrong for this specific system. The conversation improved every time a specific fact arrived. The initial recommendations were the weakest part. This is structurally identical to the failure mode identified in the Migration Success Determinant strand.

key_claims.text: Auth-first sequencing recommendation was based on category reasoning ('most Java monoliths'), invalidated by specific fact (session tokens in payments DB) in cycle 3
Microservices destination should have been interrogated in cycle 1 — its acceptance without challenge through cycle 11 was a planning error
The cycle 1 month-by-month plan was overconfident given the unknowns present — should have been framed as conditional hypothesis
Team anxiety was empirically grounded signal about a system that had already failed, not psychological avoidance behavior
The planning conversation modeled the same verify-before-committing failure mode it identified as the migration success determinant
Architectural advice given without system-specific verification is a starting hypothesis, not a recommendation

strands.title: Migration Success Determinant
strands.content: The single determinant of success in this migration is organizational verification discipline — the willingness to resolve ground truth about the actual codebase and actual requirements before committing to plans that assume something about them. This conclusion is empirically grounded in the planning conversation itself and reinforced by the cycle 13 retrospective: the seam analysis was recommended in cycle 2 and remained unexecuted through cycle 12. Schema inspection recommended cycle 3, unexecuted. ORM coupling characterization recommended cycle 5, unexecuted. Compliance escalation identified as urgent in cycle 4, unresolved. In every case, the team planned around unknowns rather than resolving them. The architecture pattern question at cycle 11 is a further instance: CEO said 'independent deployment,' team assumed microservices, nobody verified the mapping. The consistency question at cycle 12 adds another: the payments team may not have the consistency guarantee they believe they have. The cycle 13 retrospective adds a meta-level confirmation: the planning conversation itself exhibited the same failure mode — confident positions based on category reasoning, revised when specific facts arrived. The pattern is organizational avoidance of uncomfortable ground truth, not technical inability. Successful migrations are distinguished by verification discipline, not architectural sophistication. Architectural advice without system-specific verification is a hypothesis, not a recommendation — and should be labeled as such from the start.

key_claims.text: The blocking factor throughout planning has been organizational avoidance of ground truth verification, not technical complexity
The planning conversation itself modeled the failure mode it warned against — confirmed retrospectively in cycle 13
All major technical problems in this migration have known solution patterns — unknowns are about system topology and requirements, not solution existence
Unverified assumptions discovered mid-execution are unrecoverable in a fixed-window migration; discovered in week one they cost a day
Confident architectural advice without system-specific verification should be labeled as hypothesis, not recommendation

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Granular cycle-by-cycle delta tracking of every incremental position change across 13 cycles
declared_losses.why: The Epistemic Revision Log captures the four major reversals thematically — which is more useful for future planning than exhaustive delta enumeration. Individual cycle deltas are not recoverable from the tensor but the patterns they form are retained.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: The framing of team anxiety as a 'management problem' from cycle 2 — fully superseded by cycle 13 retrospective which reframes it as an empirically grounded signal
declared_losses.why: The original framing was incorrect and retaining it alongside the correction creates contradiction. The correction is the operative position.

strands.title: System Baseline
strands.content: 500K LOC Java application on Apache Tomcat. Four domains: payment processing, inventory management, user authentication, order fulfillment. Six-month migration window, limited budget. PCI-DSS compliance is a hard constraint on payments. Payments module has confirmed multi-domain coupling: inventory, order state, user profiles (application layer). Auth session tokens are stored in the payments database — justified historically by uptime guarantees. Auth code is confirmed ORM-coupled; type uncharacterized: may be shared entities with other domains (Scenario A), logic in ORM lifecycle hooks (Scenario B), or simply framework usage (Scenario C). Payments is the gravitational center of the monolith — the most reliable infrastructure became a dependency magnet. This is empirically confirmed as an availability risk: a payments DB outage last quarter cascaded to auth failure and full application outage, directly triggering this migration. Team size, cloud/on-prem topology, CI/CD maturity, raw card data storage, and QSA relationship remain unknown. Compliance officer is confirmed as an active stakeholder who has been insufficiently integrated into migration planning. Whether inventory read queries join through payments tables is unresolved and material to both inventory extraction assessment and saga design. Inventory team exists as a semi-autonomous unit with their own proposals and timeline claims. Microservices was the assumed destination from cycle 1 but was challenged at cycle 11 — hybrid destination (payments as microservice, others as modular monolith) is under active evaluation. Architecture pattern question remains open pending CEO definition of 'independent deployment.'

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java on Tomcat with four domains: payments, inventory, auth, order fulfillment
Payments module has confirmed coupling into inventory, order state, and user profiles at the application layer
Auth session tokens are stored in the payments database, justified by uptime guarantees
Auth code is ORM-coupled — type uncharacterized (shared entities / lifecycle hooks / framework usage only)
Payments is the gravitational center of the monolith — a dependency magnet by virtue of being the most reliable infrastructure

strands.title: Architecture Pattern Validity
strands.content: The microservices assumption has been challenged at cycle 11 and further refined at cycle 12 by the consistency analysis. Evaluating actual goals against modular monolith vs microservices: (1) Cascade failure elimination: caused by infrastructure coupling (shared DB), not process coupling. A modular monolith with database-per-module would have prevented it. Process separation was not the relevant variable. (2) Independent deployment: the strongest genuine argument for microservices. Strict independent deployment requires microservices or equivalent. Practical deployment safety is achievable in a well-structured modular monolith. CEO's precise meaning is unverified and blocking. (3) PCI scope reduction: requires payments to be a separately deployed service. Says nothing about what auth, inventory, and orders must be. (4) Consistency: new finding from cycle 12 — modular monolith with shared database (separate schemas, same instance) preserves ACID transaction boundary spanning payments and inventory. If payments and inventory currently share a transaction boundary in the monolith, microservices extraction genuinely removes a consistency guarantee that modular monolith preserves. This is a material advantage of modular monolith over microservices for the payments+inventory consistency problem specifically. (5) Six months, limited budget: modular monolith is substantially cheaper to operate — no service mesh, no container orchestration, no distributed tracing required for in-process modules, no API versioning between internal consumers. The hybrid destination remains likely correct: payments as true microservice (PCI + cascade severity + isolation), auth/inventory/orders as modular monolith with separate database schemas and enforced internal API boundaries. Key risk of modular monolith: network separation enforces microservice boundaries automatically; module boundaries within a monolith require active enforcement (ArchUnit or equivalent). The microservices assumption is itself an instance of the unverified assumption failure mode — CEO said 'independent deployment,' team assumed microservices, the mapping was never validated.

key_claims.text: The cascade failure was caused by infrastructure coupling (shared DB), not process coupling — a modular monolith with separate DBs would have prevented it
Modular monolith with shared database preserves ACID transaction boundary spanning payments and inventory — a consistency advantage over microservices
PCI scope reduction requires payments as a true separate service regardless of what other domains do
The CEO's definition of independent deployment has never been verified — strict vs practical are meaningfully different requirements
Hybrid destination (payments as microservice, auth/inventory/orders as modular monolith) is potentially the architecturally correct answer for this system
Modular monolith boundaries require active enforcement (ArchUnit or equivalent) — network separation is not available as the enforcement mechanism

strands.title: Incident History and Executive Constraints
strands.content: A payments DB outage last quarter caused a cascade: payments DB down → auth cannot read sessions → auth down → full application outage. This incident is the direct organizational trigger for the migration. The CEO issued a hard 90-day mandate: auth must be independent of payments within 90 days. The compliance officer introduced a 6-week security audit requirement for any new auth system and a prohibition on parallel auth systems. These constraints are in direct conflict with the 90-day mandate: 90 days minus 42 days (audit) leaves 48 days of build time for an auth migration of uncharacterized scope. The conflict requires three-way escalation: CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead, before implementation begins. Two resolution paths: (1) compliance officer confirms session store migration does not trigger audit — 90-day path restored for infrastructure deliverable, (2) CEO accepts Wave 1/Wave 2 framing. A third path — CEO overrides compliance — carries regulatory risk that must be named explicitly. Architecture pattern question adds a fourth path: modular monolith restructuring of auth may not trigger the audit requirement at all, potentially dissolving the conflict. The CEO's definition of independent deployment must be verified — if practical deployment safety satisfies the goal, modular monolith may satisfy the mandate without triggering audit.

key_claims.text: Payments DB outage caused auth outage caused full application outage — confirmed cascade failure
90-day auth independence from payments is a hard CEO mandate
90-day mandate and 6-week audit requirement are in direct conflict for full auth service extraction
Conflict must be escalated to CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead simultaneously — not resolvable at engineering level
Modular monolith restructuring of auth may not trigger the 6-week audit, potentially dissolving the CEO/compliance conflict

strands.title: Compliance Audit Constraints
strands.content: Two hard constraints from compliance officer: (1) any new auth system requires a 6-week security audit before going live, (2) parallel auth systems are prohibited — they double audit surface. These are compliance requirements, not negotiable preferences. The parallel system prohibition eliminates Strangler Fig for auth entirely — cutover must be atomic. This makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk factor, which favors hybrid extraction over greenfield: hybrid carries known behavior forward, reducing novel failure surface at atomic cutover. (B) Does modular monolith restructuring of auth count as a 'new auth system'? This is a high-value clarification — if modular monolith is exempt, the CEO/compliance conflict may dissolve. strands.title: Auth Migration: Coupling, Approach, and Sequencing
strands.content: Two confirmed coupling problems: (1) data layer — session tokens in payments DB, root cause of outage cascade, (2) application layer — auth code coupled to ORM, type uncharacterized. Session store migration is mandatory regardless of auth approach — it directly severs the availability dependency. The hybrid approach (extract auth boundary, rewrite data access layer, discard ORM coupling, carry known behavior forward) remains correct for a microservices destination. For a modular monolith destination, the equivalent is: enforce module boundary within monolith, migrate session store to separate schema, rewrite data access layer without ORM coupling to other domains. Under the modular monolith path: likely no 6-week audit, no atomic cutover risk, no parallel system prohibition problem — significant simplification. The greenfield vs hybrid debate is resolved in favor of hybrid under compliance constraints: atomic cutover with no rollback makes 'known behavior' more valuable than 'clean implementation.' Greenfield has novel failure modes; hybrid carries known failure modes. Under deadline pressure, known is safer. Dual-write during session store migration must be write-new-store-first: payments DB write is secondary, failures must not fail auth requests. Replacement session store must match or exceed payments DB uptime SLA — Redis Cluster is the right default. ORM coupling type (Scenario A/B/C) remains uncharacterized and affects build scope under either destination architecture.

strands.title: PCI-DSS and Payments Extraction Strategy
strands.content: PCI-DSS is a hard constraint unaffected by the modular monolith question — payments must be extracted as a true service regardless of what other domains do. Current state: entire 500K LOC monolith is in PCI scope. Target state: isolated payments service shrinks scope to one hardened service. Token-passing is the architectural mechanism — monolith passes payment tokens not raw card data. Auth session tokens in the payments DB may mean auth code paths are already inside the PCI boundary — auth extraction is not PCI-free as originally assumed. Under modular monolith destination, auth module boundary enforcement and session store migration resolve the co-location concern without full service extraction. strands.title: Distributed Consistency and Saga Patterns
strands.content: The payments team's position — cannot accept eventual consistency for financial transactions — is correct but misapplied. It conflates two distinct consistency requirements: (1) consistency of the payment record itself (did this charge succeed?) — must be strongly consistent, is a LOCAL concern within the payments service, fully achievable with local ACID transactions, (2) consistency between payment state and inventory state — this is the distributed coordination problem and does not require distributed ACID to solve correctly. The monolith does not provide the distributed consistency guarantee it appears to: the payment gateway API call is a network call outside the transaction boundary. Application crashes between steps create inconsistency even in the monolith. Major financial networks (SWIFT, ACH, card networks) operate under eventual consistency with compensation, not distributed ACID. Four patterns required for correct distributed financial coordination: (A) Outbox pattern — payment record and outbox event written in the same local ACID transaction. Relay process publishes event after commit. Guarantees event publication if and only if payment committed. Foundation for all other patterns. (B) Orchestration-based saga — preferred over choreography for financial workflows because the full workflow is visible in one place, compensating transactions are explicit, and audit trails are straightforward. Saga steps: ReserveInventory → ChargePayment → ConfirmOrder. Compensation flows backward: ConfirmOrder failure → RefundPayment + ReleaseInventoryReservation. Reserve inventory BEFORE charging payment — prevents charging without fulfillment capability. (C) Idempotency — all payment operations must be idempotent via client-supplied idempotency keys. Non-negotiable. Prevents double-charging on network retry. Cannot be retrofitted. (D) Reconciliation jobs — detect and heal inconsistencies that fall outside saga compensation logic. Complement to sagas, not alternative. Run at interval appropriate to business tolerance. Seam analysis must now answer an additional question: do payments and inventory currently share a database transaction boundary? If yes, microservices extraction genuinely removes a consistency guarantee that modular monolith with shared database preserves. This finding materially affects destination architecture recommendation.

key_claims.text: Strong consistency is required within the payments service locally — not across service boundaries
The monolith does not provide true distributed consistency — payment gateway call is outside transaction boundary, application crashes create inconsistency
Major financial networks operate under eventual consistency with compensation, not distributed ACID
Outbox pattern guarantees event publication if and only if the local transaction commits
Idempotency for all payment operations is non-negotiable — prevents double-charging on retry, cannot be retrofitted
Orchestration-based saga is preferable to choreography for financial workflows due to auditability and explicit compensation
Whether payments and inventory currently share a transaction boundary is the new critical seam analysis question — answer affects destination architecture recommendation

strands.title: Inventory, Orders, and Realistic Timeline
strands.content: Consolidated strand covering inventory/orders strategy and timeline assessment. Inventory team proposed standalone inventory service in 3 weeks using existing API, simultaneously requesting Postgres→DynamoDB migration. Decision: conditional approval for extraction with Postgres, rejection of DynamoDB for this window. DynamoDB requires access pattern redesign, no SQL joins, different consistency model, new operational tooling, new failure modes — none required by extraction itself. Can be reconsidered after 3 months production usage. The 3-week claim hinges on 'existing API' meaning live HTTP endpoints vs internal Java interfaces. Seam analysis must complete before extraction begins — payments writes directly into inventory tables would invalidate the 3-week plan and change the consistency design requirements. Monolith-side integration work is likely underestimated. Under modular monolith destination, inventory extraction becomes module boundary enforcement — retains in-process communication, eliminates distributed transaction risk. Timeline honest assessment: full 4-service microservices extraction in 6 months is not achievable. Two-wave framing: Wave 1 (MVM, months 1-5) covers session store migration, inventory extraction, auth/payments boundary formalization, observability. Wave 2 (months 6-12) covers auth extraction + audit (if microservices destination), payments extraction + QSA. Under hybrid destination (payments microservice + modular monolith for others), the 6-month window may deliver a more complete outcome: session store migration, payments boundary and extraction groundwork, modular restructuring of auth/inventory/orders with separate schemas, observability. The three unexecuted archaeology tasks — seam analysis (overdue since cycle 2), schema inspection (overdue since cycle 3), ORM coupling characterization (overdue since cycle 5) — remain the single biggest execution risk. The seam analysis must now additionally answer whether payments and inventory share a transaction boundary, making it even more critical than before. Every week these remain unexecuted directly reduces achievable scope within the 6-month window. Observability foundation (OpenTelemetry, structured logging, health endpoints) is non-negotiable under any destination architecture.

key_claims.text: DynamoDB migration is scope creep that should be rejected for this migration window
3-week extraction claim is plausible if 'existing API' means live HTTP endpoints and seam analysis confirms clean boundary
Full 4-service microservices extraction in 6 months is not achievable given current constraint set
Hybrid destination may deliver more complete outcomes in 6 months than microservices MVM
Three unexecuted archaeology tasks are the single biggest execution risk — overdue since cycles 2, 3, and 5
Seam analysis must now additionally identify whether payments and inventory share a transaction boundary
Observability foundation is non-negotiable under any destination architecture

Payments is the gravitational center — confirmed coupling into inventory, order state, and user profiles at the application layer. Auth session tokens stored in payments DB, justified historically by uptime guarantees. Auth code is ORM-coupled, type uncharacterized (shared entities / lifecycle hooks / framework usage). The cascade failure last quarter — payments DB outage → auth down → full application outage — is the organizational trigger. CEO issued 90-day mandate for auth independence from payments. Compliance officer introduced 6-week audit for any new auth system and prohibition on parallel auth systems. These constraints are in direct conflict. Team size, cloud/on-prem topology, CI/CD maturity, raw card data storage, and QSA relationship remain unknown. Compliance officer is an active stakeholder insufficiently integrated into planning.

strands.title: Architecture Pattern Validity
strands.content: The microservices assumption was never interrogated until cycle 11 — this is itself an instance of the unverified assumption failure mode. Evaluating actual goals against modular monolith vs microservices: (1) Cascade failure elimination: caused by infrastructure coupling (shared DB), not process coupling. Modular monolith with database-per-module would have prevented it. Process separation was not the relevant variable. (2) Independent deployment: the strongest genuine argument for microservices. Strict per-module deployment requires microservices. Practical deployment safety is achievable in a well-structured modular monolith. CEO's precise meaning is unverified and blocking. (3) PCI scope reduction: requires payments as a separately deployed service regardless of what other domains do. (4) Distributed consistency: modular monolith with shared database (separate schemas, same instance) preserves ACID transaction boundary spanning payments and inventory — a material consistency advantage over microservices for these two domains specifically. (5) Six months, limited budget: modular monolith substantially cheaper to operate — no service mesh, no container orchestration, no distributed tracing for in-process modules, no API versioning between internal consumers. Hybrid destination is likely correct: payments as true microservice (PCI + cascade severity), auth/inventory/orders as modular monolith with separate schemas and enforced internal API boundaries. Key risk of modular monolith: boundaries require active enforcement (ArchUnit) since network separation is not available as the enforcement mechanism.

Modular monolith with shared database preserves ACID transaction boundary spanning payments and inventory — consistency advantage over microservices
PCI scope reduction requires payments as a true separate service regardless of what other domains do
CEO's definition of independent deployment is unverified — strict vs practical are meaningfully different requirements
Hybrid destination (payments as microservice, auth/inventory/orders as modular monolith) is potentially the architecturally correct answer
Modular monolith boundaries require active enforcement — ArchUnit or equivalent is not optional

A third path — modular monolith restructuring of auth may not trigger audit at all — potentially dissolves the conflict. CEO's definition of independent deployment must be verified: if practical deployment safety satisfies the goal, modular monolith may satisfy the mandate.

strands.title: Migration Success Determinant
strands.content: The single determinant of success: closing the gap between decisions made and ground truth verified, faster than the calendar consumes the window. This applies retrospectively to the planning conversation itself — four positions taken in cycle 1 required material revision as specific facts emerged: (1) Auth-first recommendation based on 'clean boundaries in most Java monoliths' — category reasoning falsified by session tokens in payments DB. (2) Microservices as unquestioned destination — should have been interrogated in cycle 1, not cycle 11. The cascade failure was infrastructure coupling, not process coupling. (3) Month-by-month plan presented with confidence inappropriate to the information available — a conditional plan with explicit archaeology gates would have been honest. (4) Team anxiety treated as psychological avoidance — it was an empirically grounded signal; the cascade failure had already happened. The pattern across all four reversals: confident claims based on how these systems usually work, falsified by how this specific system actually is. The planning conversation modeled the exact anti-pattern it identified as the success determinant. Architectural advice without system-specific verification is a hypothesis, not a recommendation. The three archaeology tasks overdue since cycles 2, 3, and 5 are the most concrete expression of the failure mode — they represent verified facts that would resolve blocking questions, repeatedly deferred in favor of continued planning against assumptions.
