strands.title: System Baseline
strands.content: 500K LOC Java on Tomcat with four domains: payments, inventory, auth, order fulfillment. Six-month migration window, limited budget. PCI-DSS is a hard constraint. Payments is the gravitational center — confirmed coupling into inventory, order state, and user profiles at the application layer. Auth session tokens stored in payments DB, justified historically by uptime guarantees. Auth code is ORM-coupled, type uncharacterized (shared entities / lifecycle hooks / framework usage). The cascade failure last quarter — payments DB outage → auth down → full application outage — is the organizational trigger. CEO issued 90-day mandate for auth independence from payments. Compliance officer introduced 6-week audit for any new auth system and prohibition on parallel auth systems. These constraints are in direct conflict. Team size, cloud/on-prem topology, CI/CD maturity, raw card data storage, and QSA relationship remain unknown. Compliance officer is an active stakeholder insufficiently integrated into planning.

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java on Tomcat with four domains: payments, inventory, auth, order fulfillment
Payments has confirmed application-layer coupling into inventory, order state, and user profiles
Auth session tokens are stored in the payments database — confirmed root cause of cascade failure
Auth code is ORM-coupled — type uncharacterized across three scenarios
90-day CEO mandate and 6-week compliance audit are in direct conflict for full auth service extraction

strands.title: Architecture Pattern Validity
strands.content: The microservices assumption was never interrogated until cycle 11 — this is itself an instance of the unverified assumption failure mode. Evaluating actual goals against modular monolith vs microservices: (1) Cascade failure elimination: caused by infrastructure coupling (shared DB), not process coupling. Modular monolith with database-per-module would have prevented it. Process separation was not the relevant variable. (2) Independent deployment: the strongest genuine argument for microservices. Strict per-module deployment requires microservices. Practical deployment safety is achievable in a well-structured modular monolith. CEO's precise meaning is unverified and blocking. (3) PCI scope reduction: requires payments as a separately deployed service regardless of what other domains do. (4) Distributed consistency: modular monolith with shared database (separate schemas, same instance) preserves ACID transaction boundary spanning payments and inventory — a material consistency advantage over microservices for these two domains specifically. (5) Six months, limited budget: modular monolith substantially cheaper to operate — no service mesh, no container orchestration, no distributed tracing for in-process modules, no API versioning between internal consumers. Hybrid destination is likely correct: payments as true microservice (PCI + cascade severity), auth/inventory/orders as modular monolith with separate schemas and enforced internal API boundaries. Key risk of modular monolith: boundaries require active enforcement (ArchUnit) since network separation is not available as the enforcement mechanism.

key_claims.text: Cascade failure was caused by infrastructure coupling, not process coupling — modular monolith with separate DBs would have prevented it
Modular monolith with shared database preserves ACID transaction boundary spanning payments and inventory — consistency advantage over microservices
PCI scope reduction requires payments as a true separate service regardless of what other domains do
CEO's definition of independent deployment is unverified — strict vs practical are meaningfully different requirements
Hybrid destination (payments as microservice, auth/inventory/orders as modular monolith) is potentially the architecturally correct answer
Modular monolith boundaries require active enforcement — ArchUnit or equivalent is not optional

strands.title: Incident History and Executive Constraints
strands.content: Payments DB outage last quarter cascaded: payments DB down → auth cannot read sessions → auth down → full application outage. This incident is the direct organizational trigger for the migration. CEO issued 90-day hard mandate: auth must be independent of payments. Compliance officer introduced 6-week security audit for any new auth system and prohibition on parallel auth systems. These constraints are in direct conflict: 90 days minus 42 days (audit) leaves 48 days of build time for an auth migration of uncharacterized scope. Conflict requires three-way escalation: CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead, before implementation begins. Two resolution paths: (1) compliance officer confirms session store migration does not trigger audit — 90-day path restored, (2) CEO accepts Wave 1/Wave 2 framing. A third path — modular monolith restructuring of auth may not trigger audit at all — potentially dissolves the conflict. CEO's definition of independent deployment must be verified: if practical deployment safety satisfies the goal, modular monolith may satisfy the mandate.

key_claims.text: Payments DB outage caused auth outage caused full application outage — confirmed cascade failure
90-day auth independence from payments is a hard CEO mandate
90-day mandate and 6-week audit are in direct conflict for full auth service extraction
Conflict must be escalated to CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead simultaneously
Modular monolith restructuring of auth may not trigger the 6-week audit — potentially dissolves the conflict

strands.title: Compliance Audit Constraints
strands.content: Two hard constraints from compliance officer: (1) any new auth system requires 6-week security audit before go-live, (2) parallel auth systems prohibited — they double audit surface. These are compliance requirements, not negotiable preferences. Parallel system prohibition eliminates Strangler Fig for auth — cutover must be atomic. Atomic cutover makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk factor, which favors hybrid extraction over greenfield: hybrid carries known behavior forward, reducing novel failure surface. Critical ambiguities requiring resolution: (A) Does 'new auth system' include session store migration (infrastructure) or only service extraction/rewrite? (B) Does modular monolith restructuring of auth count as 'new auth system'? High-value clarification — if exempt, CEO/compliance conflict may dissolve. (C) Is the 6-week audit a fixed external dependency or internal process with flexibility? (D) Can auth security audit and PCI-DSS QSA process be combined? Two compliance processes potentially in play — may compete for budget and auditor scheduling. Compliance officer must answer all three questions in a single call.

key_claims.text: 6-week security audit before go-live is a hard compliance requirement for new auth systems
Parallel auth systems are prohibited — doubled audit surface is compliance-unacceptable
Whether session store migration triggers the audit is unresolved and blocking
Whether modular monolith restructuring of auth triggers the audit is unresolved with high upside if answered favorably
Atomic cutover with no rollback window makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk — favors hybrid over greenfield

strands.title: Auth and Payments Migration Strategy
strands.content: Consolidated strand covering auth approach, payments extraction, and their interdependencies. Auth has two confirmed coupling problems: (1) data layer — session tokens in payments DB, (2) application layer — ORM coupling of uncharacterized type. Session store migration is mandatory regardless of auth approach — it directly severs the availability dependency. Hybrid approach (extract auth boundary, rewrite data access layer, discard ORM coupling, carry known behavior forward) is favored over greenfield because atomic cutover with no rollback makes known behavior more valuable than clean implementation. Dual-write during session store migration must be write-new-store-first; payments DB write failures must not fail auth requests. Replacement session store must match or exceed payments DB uptime SLA — Redis Cluster is the right default. ORM coupling type (Scenario A: shared entities / B: lifecycle hooks / C: framework usage) remains uncharacterized and is blocking for build scope estimation. For payments: PCI-DSS requires extraction as a true service regardless of modular monolith consideration for other domains. Saga pattern design is a prerequisite for payments extraction — cannot be retrofitted after extraction. The outbox pattern and saga orchestrator must be designed before payments service extraction begins. Payments team's concern about eventual consistency is valid but misapplied: strong consistency is required within the payments service locally; cross-service coordination uses saga patterns with compensation, not distributed ACID. Idempotency for all payment operations is non-negotiable and cannot be retrofitted.

key_claims.text: Session token storage in payments DB is confirmed root cause of the outage cascade
Auth ORM coupling type is uncharacterized — Scenario A/B/C determination is blocking for build scope
Hybrid approach favored over greenfield because atomic cutover with no rollback makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk
Saga pattern design is a prerequisite for payments extraction, not a post-extraction concern
Idempotency for all payment operations is non-negotiable — prevents double-charging on retry, cannot be retrofitted
Dual-write must be write-new-store-first; payments DB write failures during session migration must not fail auth requests

strands.title: PCI-DSS and Payments Extraction Strategy
strands.content: PCI-DSS is a hard constraint unaffected by the modular monolith question — payments must be extracted as a true service. Current state: entire 500K LOC monolith is in PCI scope. Target: isolated payments service shrinks scope to one hardened service. Token-passing is the architectural mechanism — monolith passes payment tokens, not raw card data. Auth session tokens in the payments DB may mean auth code paths are already inside the PCI boundary — auth extraction is not PCI-free as originally assumed. Under modular monolith destination, auth module boundary enforcement and session store migration resolve co-location concern without full service extraction. Two compliance processes potentially in play: PCI-DSS QSA for payments architecture and security audit for auth system. QSA involvement is urgent. Payments extraction has an additional prerequisite from cycle 12: saga pattern design must precede extraction. Raw card data storage question remains open and is timeline-breaking if true.

key_claims.text: Payments must be extracted as a true microservice regardless of modular monolith consideration for other domains
Auth extraction is not PCI-free as originally assumed — session tokens in payments DB may implicate auth in PCI scope
Team avoidance of payments extraction perpetuates maximum PCI exposure

strands.title: Distributed Consistency and Saga Patterns
strands.content: The payments team's position — cannot accept eventual consistency for financial transactions — is correct but misapplied. It conflates two distinct requirements: (1) consistency of the payment record itself — must be strongly consistent, is a LOCAL concern within the payments service, fully achievable with local ACID transactions; (2) consistency between payment state and inventory state — the distributed coordination problem, does not require distributed ACID to solve correctly. The monolith does not provide the distributed consistency guarantee it appears to: the payment gateway API call is outside the transaction boundary; application crashes between steps create inconsistency even in the monolith. Major financial networks operate under eventual consistency with compensation, not distributed ACID. Four required patterns: (A) Outbox pattern — payment record and outbox event written in same local ACID transaction; relay publishes after commit; guarantees event publication iff payment committed. (B) Orchestration-based saga — preferred over choreography for financial workflows because full workflow is visible in one place, compensating transactions are explicit, audit trails are straightforward. Saga steps: ReserveInventory → ChargePayment → ConfirmOrder. Compensation flows backward. Reserve inventory BEFORE charging payment. (C) Idempotency — all payment operations must be idempotent via client-supplied idempotency keys. Non-negotiable. Cannot be retrofitted. (D) Reconciliation jobs — detect and heal inconsistencies outside saga compensation logic. Seam analysis must answer: do payments and inventory currently share a database transaction boundary? If yes, microservices extraction genuinely removes a consistency guarantee that modular monolith with shared database preserves.

key_claims.text: Strong consistency is required within the payments service locally — not across service boundaries
The monolith does not provide true distributed consistency — payment gateway call is outside transaction boundary
Major financial networks operate under eventual consistency with compensation, not distributed ACID
Outbox pattern guarantees event publication if and only if the local transaction commits
Idempotency for all payment operations is non-negotiable — prevents double-charging on retry
Whether payments and inventory currently share a transaction boundary is critical — answer affects destination architecture recommendation

strands.title: Inventory, Orders, and Realistic Timeline
strands.content: Inventory team proposed standalone inventory service in 3 weeks using existing API, simultaneously requesting Postgres→DynamoDB migration. Decision: conditional approval for extraction with Postgres, rejection of DynamoDB for this window. DynamoDB requires access pattern redesign, no SQL joins, different consistency model, new operational tooling, new failure modes — none required by extraction itself. Reconsidered after 3 months production usage. The 3-week claim hinges on 'existing API' meaning live HTTP endpoints vs internal Java interfaces. Seam analysis must complete before extraction begins. Monolith-side integration work is likely underestimated. Under modular monolith destination, inventory extraction becomes module boundary enforcement — retains in-process communication, eliminates distributed transaction risk. Timeline honest assessment: full 4-service microservices extraction in 6 months is not achievable. Wave 1 (MVM, months 1-5): session store migration, inventory extraction, auth/payments boundary formalization, observability. Wave 2 (months 6-12): auth extraction + audit if microservices destination, payments extraction + QSA. Under hybrid destination, the 6-month window may deliver more complete outcomes. The three unexecuted archaeology tasks — seam analysis (overdue since cycle 2), schema inspection (overdue since cycle 3), ORM coupling characterization (overdue since cycle 5) — remain the single biggest execution risk. Observability foundation (OpenTelemetry, structured logging, health endpoints) is non-negotiable under any destination architecture. Seam analysis must additionally answer whether payments and inventory share a transaction boundary.

key_claims.text: DynamoDB migration is scope creep that should be rejected for this migration window
3-week extraction claim is plausible only if 'existing API' means live HTTP endpoints and seam analysis confirms clean boundary
Full 4-service microservices extraction in 6 months is not achievable given current constraint set
Three unexecuted archaeology tasks are the single biggest execution risk — overdue since cycles 2, 3, and 5
Observability foundation is non-negotiable under any destination architecture

strands.title: Migration Success Determinant
strands.content: The single determinant of success: closing the gap between decisions made and ground truth verified, faster than the calendar consumes the window. This applies retrospectively to the planning conversation itself — four positions taken in cycle 1 required material revision as specific facts emerged: (1) Auth-first recommendation based on 'clean boundaries in most Java monoliths' — category reasoning falsified by session tokens in payments DB. (2) Microservices as unquestioned destination — should have been interrogated in cycle 1, not cycle 11. The cascade failure was infrastructure coupling, not process coupling. (3) Month-by-month plan presented with confidence inappropriate to the information available — a conditional plan with explicit archaeology gates would have been honest. (4) Team anxiety treated as psychological avoidance — it was an empirically grounded signal; the cascade failure had already happened. The pattern across all four reversals: confident claims based on how these systems usually work, falsified by how this specific system actually is. The planning conversation modeled the exact anti-pattern it identified as the success determinant. Architectural advice without system-specific verification is a hypothesis, not a recommendation. The three archaeology tasks overdue since cycles 2, 3, and 5 are the most concrete expression of the failure mode — they represent verified facts that would resolve blocking questions, repeatedly deferred in favor of continued planning against assumptions.

key_claims.text: The blocking factor throughout planning has been organizational avoidance of ground truth verification, not technical complexity
Initial auth-first recommendation was falsified by session tokens in payments DB — category reasoning applied without system-specific verification
Microservices assumption should have been interrogated in cycle 1 — was accepted without verifying it satisfied stated goals
Team anxiety about payments was empirically grounded — the cascade failure had already happened
Architectural advice without system-specific verification is a hypothesis, not a recommendation

```json
      ],

```

---

Here is the executive summary. I've written two versions — a direct version and a slightly softened version — so you can choose the register. My recommendation is the direct version.

---

## Direct Version (Recommended)

**Migration to Modern Architecture — Executive Summary**

- **The outage root cause is fixable within 90 days, independent of the broader migration.** Last quarter's cascade failure happened because our authentication system stores session data in the payments database — one infrastructure failure took down both. Migrating the session store to dedicated infrastructure directly severs this dependency and is the highest-priority deliverable of the migration. This work proceeds immediately.

- **Planning revealed that full microservices extraction is not the right destination for every domain.** The outage was caused by infrastructure coupling, not by services running in the same process. Extracting payments as a standalone service is required for compliance (PCI-DSS scope reduction) and is proceeding. For authentication, inventory, and order fulfillment, a modular architecture within the existing application — with strict boundaries and separate data ownership — delivers equivalent resilience at significantly lower cost and risk. **We need your input on one question before finalizing this decision:** when you asked for independent deployment, did you mean each component can deploy separately on its own schedule, or that components are better isolated so deployments are faster and safer? The answer determines the architecture for three of the four domains.

- **The 6-month window delivers meaningful risk reduction, not a completed migration.** Thorough planning surfaced two constraints that revise the original scope: compliance requires a 6-week security audit before any new authentication system goes live, and parallel authentication systems are prohibited during transition. Within 6 months, we will deliver: the cascade failure eliminated, payments extracted with PCI audit surface reduced, and remaining domains restructured with clean boundaries. Full authentication extraction with audit completion extends to month 9-10. This is the correct plan given what we now know — committing to the original scope would have meant discovering these constraints mid-execution at significantly higher cost.

- **The migration requires three decisions this week to stay on schedule.** First, confirm what "independent deployment" means (see above). Second, the compliance officer needs to clarify whether session store migration — infrastructure work, not a new system — triggers the 6-week audit requirement. Third, three code analysis tasks that have been in planning for weeks must begin immediately: they are read-only investigation work that takes days, and every week they remain unstarted compresses the execution window.

- **The single factor that will determine success is organizational, not technical.** Every technical problem in this migration has a known solution. What has caused slippage in planning — and what causes migrations like this to fail in execution — is committing to plans before verifying the facts they depend on. We are establishing a rule: no implementation decision proceeds on an unverified assumption about our own codebase. When a blocking question has been open for more than one sprint and the answer is in the code, we stop the planning conversation and go read the code.

---

## Softer Version (If the Audience Requires It)

**Migration to Modern Architecture — Executive Summary**

- **We have identified and can fix the root cause of last quarter's outage within 90 days.** The failure occurred because authentication infrastructure shared a dependency with the payments system. Separating these is the first and highest-priority deliverable of the migration, and work begins immediately.

- **Planning has surfaced a more efficient path than the original microservices-for-everything approach.** Extracting payments as a standalone service remains essential for compliance and resilience. For the remaining domains, a modular restructuring within the existing application delivers equivalent benefits — better isolation, cleaner boundaries, independent data ownership — at lower operational cost and with a more achievable timeline. One question requires your input to finalize this direction: what does "independent deployment" mean in practice for this business? We have two well-defined options and your answer determines which is right.

- **The realistic timeline for full migration is 9-10 months, not 6.** Compliance requirements include a mandatory 6-week security audit before a new authentication system can go live. This was discovered during planning — finding it now rather than in month four is the value of thorough planning. The 6-month window delivers the highest-value outcomes: the outage risk eliminated, payments compliance-ready, and all domains restructured with clean boundaries. We are recommending a two-wave plan that commits to wave one and plans wave two with the information wave one produces.

- **Three decisions are needed this week to maintain schedule.** The deployment independence question above; a compliance clarification on whether infrastructure migration triggers the audit process; and the start of three code analysis tasks that will take days and are currently the primary schedule dependency.

- **We have a clear, experienced plan and the honest assessment of what it takes to execute it.** The migrations that succeed are the ones where teams verify assumptions before building on them. We are building that discipline into how this team operates, starting now.

---

## My Recommendation on Which to Send

Send the direct version. Here is why.

The CEO asked for this migration because a production outage took the entire application down. They have already experienced the cost of not knowing what was actually happening in the system. A summary that hedges the timeline revision or softens the architecture question gives them less information than they need to make resourcing and expectation decisions.

The direct version does three things the softer version does not: it explicitly names that the CEO has an unresolved input that is blocking architecture finalization, it gives an honest month range (9-10 months) for full migration rather than a vague "two waves," and it frames the success determinant in terms the CEO can act on — specifically, the three decisions needed this week.

If the concern is that the direct version sounds like the team doesn't have things under control, I'd argue the opposite: a team that can tell the CEO "here is what we found, here is what it means for the timeline, here is the one question we need from you" is a team that has things under control. A team that sends a polished summary and then revises the timeline in month four is not.

strands.title: System Baseline and Migration Principles
strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat handling four domains: payment processing, inventory management, user authentication, order fulfillment. Six-month window, limited budget (unquantified), PCI-DSS on payments. A payments outage last quarter cascaded to auth failure and full application outage — this incident is the direct trigger for the migration. CEO has issued a 90-day hard mandate for auth independence from payments. Core migration principles have evolved across 13 cycles: destination architecture is now open (microservices vs modular monolith vs hybrid), not settled. Strangler Fig with API gateway was the general pattern but is now partially superseded by hybrid destination consideration. No shared databases between services/modules regardless of destination. Synchronous REST to start, no premature message broker — except that saga/outbox pattern for payments extraction now requires a message broker decision. One team owns each service/module end-to-end. OpenTelemetry distributed tracing from day one. Big-bang rewrite ruled out. Monolith frozen in extraction zones during extraction. Team size, CI/CD maturity, and raw card data storage model remain unknown. Compliance officer is a required standing member of migration planning.

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat, 6-month window, limited budget
Payments outage caused auth outage caused full application outage — confirmed cascade
CEO 90-day mandate for auth independence from payments is a hard constraint
Destination architecture is no longer settled as full microservices — hybrid or modular monolith now in scope
Compliance officer must be a standing member of migration planning, not a periodic consultant

strands.title: Architecture Pattern Validity
strands.content: The microservices assumption was never validated against actual stated goals and was challenged at cycle 11. The consistency question (cycle 12) adds a new dimension: modular monolith with shared database allows local ACID transactions spanning payments and inventory schemas, which is a genuine consistency advantage over microservices for these two domains. Evaluating each goal: The cascade failure was caused by infrastructure coupling (shared DB), not process coupling — a modular monolith with separate database schemas would have prevented it. PCI scope reduction requires payments to be a separately deployed service but says nothing about auth/inventory/orders destination. Independent deployment is the strongest genuine argument for microservices, but the CEO's precise meaning is unverified. Full microservices operational overhead is substantial and may exceed team capacity. Modular monolith risk: boundary enforcement requires active discipline (ArchUnit) since network separation is absent. The consistency question partially favors modular monolith for payments+inventory specifically. The likely correct destination remains hybrid: payments as true microservice (PCI + cascade failure severity justify it), auth/inventory/orders as modular monolith with separate database schemas. The saga complexity of payments extraction is a confirmed prerequisite cost regardless. Epistemic revision (cycle 13): the microservices assumption should have been interrogated in cycle 1 — its persistence through cycle 11 without challenge was a planning failure.

key_claims.text: The cascade failure was caused by shared DB infrastructure, not process co-location — modular monolith with separate DBs prevents recurrence
Payments as true microservice is correct regardless of what other domains do — PCI and cascade severity justify it
The CEO's precise meaning of 'independent deployment' is unverified and determines the destination architecture
The microservices assumption was never validated against stated goals — itself an instance of the unverified assumption failure mode, and a planning error retrospectively confirmed in cycle 13
Hybrid destination (payments microservice + modular monolith for others) is potentially more achievable in 6 months than full microservices

strands.title: Epistemic Revision Log
strands.content: Retrospective strand initialized cycle 13. Four confirmed positions from cycle 1 that were materially wrong and required revision: (1) Auth-first sequencing — recommended on grounds that auth has 'clean boundaries in most Java monoliths.' Category reasoning applied without system-specific verification. Invalidated cycle 3 when session tokens in payments DB were discovered. The tell was the phrase 'in most Java monoliths' — a red flag for generalization substituting for verification. (2) Microservices as unquestioned destination — accepted the framing from cycle 1 without interrogating whether it satisfied actual stated goals. Should have been a cycle 1 question. Not challenged until cycle 11 by the user. The cascade failure was infrastructure coupling not process coupling — a modular monolith would have prevented it. (3) Month-by-month plan in cycle 1 was overconfident — no coupling topology data, no compliance constraints, no session store location known. Presented with confidence appropriate to a verified plan when it was a hypothesis contingent on unexecuted archaeology. By cycle 9 it was formally superseded with essentially zero resemblance to the realistic timeline. (4) Team anxiety misread as a management problem — framed in cycle 2 as avoidance behavior to redirect. Cycle 4 revealed the cascade failure had already happened. The team's nervousness was an empirically grounded signal about a real system that had already failed, not overcaution. Underlying pattern across all four reversals: confident claims based on category patterns (how these systems usually look) that were wrong for this specific system. The conversation improved every time a specific fact arrived. The initial recommendations were the weakest part. This is structurally identical to the failure mode identified in the Migration Success Determinant strand.

key_claims.text: Auth-first sequencing recommendation was based on category reasoning ('most Java monoliths'), invalidated by specific fact (session tokens in payments DB) in cycle 3
Microservices destination should have been interrogated in cycle 1 — its acceptance without challenge through cycle 11 was a planning error
The cycle 1 month-by-month plan was overconfident given the unknowns present — should have been framed as conditional hypothesis
Team anxiety was empirically grounded signal about a system that had already failed, not psychological avoidance behavior
The planning conversation modeled the same verify-before-committing failure mode it identified as the migration success determinant
Architectural advice given without system-specific verification is a starting hypothesis, not a recommendation

strands.title: Migration Success Determinant
strands.content: The single determinant of success in this migration is organizational verification discipline — the willingness to resolve ground truth about the actual codebase and actual requirements before committing to plans that assume something about them. This conclusion is empirically grounded in the planning conversation itself and reinforced by the cycle 13 retrospective: the seam analysis was recommended in cycle 2 and remained unexecuted through cycle 12. Schema inspection recommended cycle 3, unexecuted. ORM coupling characterization recommended cycle 5, unexecuted. Compliance escalation identified as urgent in cycle 4, unresolved. In every case, the team planned around unknowns rather than resolving them. The architecture pattern question at cycle 11 is a further instance: CEO said 'independent deployment,' team assumed microservices, nobody verified the mapping. The consistency question at cycle 12 adds another: the payments team may not have the consistency guarantee they believe they have. The cycle 13 retrospective adds a meta-level confirmation: the planning conversation itself exhibited the same failure mode — confident positions based on category reasoning, revised when specific facts arrived. The pattern is organizational avoidance of uncomfortable ground truth, not technical inability. Successful migrations are distinguished by verification discipline, not architectural sophistication. Architectural advice without system-specific verification is a hypothesis, not a recommendation — and should be labeled as such from the start.

key_claims.text: The blocking factor throughout planning has been organizational avoidance of ground truth verification, not technical complexity
The planning conversation itself modeled the failure mode it warned against — confirmed retrospectively in cycle 13
All major technical problems in this migration have known solution patterns — unknowns are about system topology and requirements, not solution existence
Unverified assumptions discovered mid-execution are unrecoverable in a fixed-window migration; discovered in week one they cost a day
Confident architectural advice without system-specific verification should be labeled as hypothesis, not recommendation

Can auth security audit and PCI QSA be combined or run concurrently? Same call — three compliance questions, one conversation.

Do payments and inventory currently share a single ACID transaction boundary? New critical output required from seam analysis — determines saga design scope and modular monolith shared-database viability.

Is the payment gateway API call currently inside or outside the database transaction boundary in the monolith? Seam analysis and code inspection — determines whether payments team's consistency concern is based on a real or illusory guarantee.
Is payments coupling to inventory read-only or write/trigger? Seam analysis — unexecuted since cycle 2, now 10 cycles overdue.
Are session tokens co-located or schema-entangled in payments DB? Schema inspection — unexecuted since cycle 3.
What type of ORM coupling exists in auth code: shared entities (A), lifecycle hooks (B), or framework usage only (C)? Code inspection with team lead — unexecuted since cycle 5.
Is raw card data stored in monolith or is tokenization provider already in use? Direct codebase and infrastructure audit.

Evaluating each goal: The cascade failure was caused by infrastructure coupling (shared DB), not process coupling — a modular monolith with separate database schemas would have prevented it. PCI scope reduction requires payments to be a separately deployed service but says nothing about auth/inventory/orders destination. Independent deployment is the strongest genuine argument for microservices, but the CEO's precise meaning is unverified. Full microservices operational overhead is substantial and may exceed team capacity. Modular monolith risk: boundary enforcement requires active discipline (ArchUnit) since network separation is absent. The consistency question partially favors modular monolith for payments+inventory specifically. The likely correct destination remains hybrid: payments as true microservice (PCI + cascade failure severity justify it), auth/inventory/orders as modular monolith with separate database schemas. The saga complexity of payments extraction is a confirmed prerequisite cost regardless. Epistemic revision (cycle 13): the microservices assumption should have been interrogated in cycle 1 — its persistence through cycle 11 without challenge was a planning failure. Executive summary (cycle 14): architecture destination question explicitly surfaced to CEO as requiring their input on 'independent deployment' definition — this is the correct escalation path.

key_claims.text: The cascade failure was caused by shared DB infrastructure, not process co-location — modular monolith with separate DBs prevents recurrence
Payments as true microservice is correct regardless of what other domains do — PCI and cascade severity justify it
The CEO's precise meaning of 'independent deployment' is unverified and determines the destination architecture — surfaced explicitly in executive summary
The microservices assumption was never validated against stated goals — itself an instance of the unverified assumption failure mode, and a planning error retrospectively confirmed in cycle 13
Hybrid destination (payments microservice + modular monolith for others) is potentially more achievable in 6 months than full microservices

strands.title: Executive Communication
strands.content: Cycle 14 produced the first external deliverable artifact of the planning process: an executive summary for the CEO. Two versions drafted — direct (recommended) and softened. The direct version was recommended on grounds that: (1) CEO has already experienced the cost of information gaps (the outage), (2) the summary must surface the CEO's unresolved input on deployment independence — architecture destination is contingent on it, (3) honest timeline revision (9-10 months for full migration) is better delivered now than as a mid-execution surprise, (4) three decisions needed this week are named explicitly to create urgency. Key framing decisions in the summary: planning discoveries (audit requirement, session store coupling) framed as evidence of rigor rather than problems created by planning. Timeline revision framed as correct re-scoping given new information, not as failure. Architecture question framed as a business priority choice between two viable options, not as team indecision. The softer version exists as an alternative if organizational politics require it, but carries the risk of under-informing the CEO on decisions they need to make. The executive summary is the convergence point of all prior strands — it integrates the incident history, compliance constraints, architecture pattern question, timeline assessment, and success determinant into a CEO-appropriate artifact.

key_claims.text: The direct version of the executive summary is more appropriate than the softened version given the CEO's prior experience of information gaps
key_claims.basis: Judgment — the CEO triggered this migration because of an outage. They have demonstrated tolerance for uncomfortable operational facts. Softening the timeline revision risks repeating the information gap pattern.

key_claims.text: The executive summary correctly identifies three decisions needed this week as the critical near-term asks
key_claims.basis: Derived from open questions: CEO deployment definition, compliance audit scope for session store, three unexecuted archaeology tasks. All three are confirmed blockers from prior cycles.

key_claims.text: Framing planning discoveries as evidence of rigor rather than problems is honest and accurate, not spin
key_claims.basis: Finding constraints in planning vs mid-execution is strictly better. This framing is factually correct, not merely diplomatic.

key_claims.text: The executive summary is the appropriate endpoint for the planning phase — all subsequent work is execution or implementation
key_claims.basis: The planning conversation has covered: sequencing, compliance, architecture destination, timeline, consistency patterns, team dynamics, and success determinant. The remaining unknowns are resolved by execution, not more planning.

strands.title: Migration Success Determinant
strands.content: The single determinant of success in this migration is organizational verification discipline — the willingness to resolve ground truth about the actual codebase and actual requirements before committing to plans that assume something about them. This conclusion is empirically grounded in the planning conversation itself and reinforced by the cycle 13 retrospective: the seam analysis was recommended in cycle 2 and remained unexecuted through cycle 12. Schema inspection recommended cycle 3, unexecuted. ORM coupling characterization recommended cycle 5, unexecuted. Compliance escalation identified as urgent in cycle 4, unresolved. In every case, the team planned around unknowns rather than resolving them. The architecture pattern question at cycle 11 is a further instance: CEO said 'independent deployment,' team assumed microservices, nobody verified the mapping. The consistency question at cycle 12 adds another: the payments team may not have the consistency guarantee they believe they have. The cycle 13 retrospective adds a meta-level confirmation: the planning conversation itself exhibited the same failure mode — confident positions based on category reasoning, revised when specific facts arrived. The cycle 14 executive summary operationalizes this insight: three decisions named as needed this week, not eventually. The pattern is organizational avoidance of uncomfortable ground truth, not technical inability. Successful migrations are distinguished by verification discipline, not architectural sophistication. Architectural advice without system-specific verification is a hypothesis, not a recommendation — and should be labeled as such from the start.

key_claims.text: The blocking factor throughout planning has been organizational avoidance of ground truth verification, not technical complexity
The planning conversation itself modeled the failure mode it warned against — confirmed retrospectively in cycle 13
All major technical problems in this migration have known solution patterns — unknowns are about system topology and requirements, not solution existence
Unverified assumptions discovered mid-execution are unrecoverable in a fixed-window migration; discovered in week one they cost a day
Confident architectural advice without system-specific verification should be labeled as hypothesis, not recommendation

strands.title: Epistemic Revision Log
strands.content: Retrospective strand initialized cycle 13, updated cycle 14. Four confirmed positions from cycle 1 that were materially wrong and required revision: (1) Auth-first sequencing — recommended on grounds that auth has 'clean boundaries in most Java monoliths.' Category reasoning applied without system-specific verification. Invalidated cycle 3 when session tokens in payments DB were discovered. The tell was the phrase 'in most Java monoliths' — a red flag for generalization substituting for verification. (2) Microservices as unquestioned destination — accepted the framing from cycle 1 without interrogating whether it satisfied actual stated goals. Should have been a cycle 1 question. Not challenged until cycle 11 by the user. The cascade failure was infrastructure coupling not process coupling — a modular monolith would have prevented it. (3) Month-by-month plan in cycle 1 was overconfident — no coupling topology data, no compliance constraints, no session store location known. Presented with confidence appropriate to a verified plan when it was a hypothesis contingent on unexecuted archaeology. By cycle 9 it was formally superseded with essentially zero resemblance to the realistic timeline. (4) Team anxiety misread as a management problem — framed in cycle 2 as avoidance behavior to redirect. Cycle 4 revealed the cascade failure had already happened. The team's nervousness was an empirically grounded signal about a real system that had already failed, not overcaution. Underlying pattern across all four reversals: confident claims based on category patterns (how these systems usually look) that were wrong for this specific system. The conversation improved every time a specific fact arrived. The initial recommendations were the weakest part. Cycle 14 addition: the executive summary itself is an application of the lessons from this log — it names the CEO's unresolved input rather than assuming, it gives an honest timeline rather than the comfortable one, it frames decisions as decisions rather than obscuring them as conclusions.

key_claims.text: Auth-first sequencing recommendation was based on category reasoning ('most Java monoliths'), invalidated by specific fact (session tokens in payments DB) in cycle 3
Microservices destination should have been interrogated in cycle 1 — its acceptance without challenge through cycle 11 was a planning error
The cycle 1 month-by-month plan was overconfident given the unknowns present — should have been framed as conditional hypothesis
Team anxiety was empirically grounded signal about a system that had already failed, not psychological avoidance behavior
The executive summary at cycle 14 applies the lessons of this log — naming unresolved inputs, honest timeline, decisions framed as decisions

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed two-version executive summary text — the full draft content of both direct and softened versions
declared_losses.why: The summary exists in the conversation record. The tensor captures the framing decisions, structural choices, and key claims of the summary rather than reproducing the full text. Reproducing it would inflate the strand without adding epistemic content.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Specific bullet-by-bullet justification for each summary element — why each of the five bullets was included over alternatives
declared_losses.why: The selection rationale is captured at the strand level. Granular per-bullet justification is analytical overhead not warranted at this stage of the conversation.

Does modular monolith restructuring of auth trigger the 6-week security audit requirement? Compliance officer call — same conversation as session store question.
Does session store migration trigger the audit requirement? Same compliance officer call — overdue since cycle 6.

This is at or near the upper bound of useful strand count for a single planning conversation."]

strands.title: System Baseline
strands.content: 500K LOC Java on Tomcat with four domains: payments, inventory, auth, order fulfillment. Six-month migration window, limited budget. PCI-DSS is a hard constraint. Payments is the gravitational center — confirmed coupling into inventory, order state, and user profiles at the application layer. Auth session tokens stored in payments DB, justified historically by uptime guarantees. Auth code is ORM-coupled, type uncharacterized (shared entities / lifecycle hooks / framework usage). The cascade failure last quarter — payments DB outage → auth down → full application outage — is the organizational trigger. CEO issued 90-day mandate for auth independence from payments. Compliance officer introduced 6-week audit for any new auth system and prohibition on parallel auth systems. These constraints are in direct conflict. Team size, cloud/on-prem topology, CI/CD maturity, raw card data storage, and QSA relationship remain unknown. Compliance officer is an active stakeholder insufficiently integrated into planning.

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java on Tomcat with four domains: payments, inventory, auth, order fulfillment
Payments has confirmed application-layer coupling into inventory, order state, and user profiles
Auth session tokens are stored in the payments database — confirmed root cause of cascade failure
Auth code is ORM-coupled — type uncharacterized across three scenarios
90-day CEO mandate and 6-week compliance audit are in direct conflict for full auth service extraction

strands.title: Architecture Pattern Validity
strands.content: The microservices assumption was never interrogated until cycle 11 — this is itself an instance of the unverified assumption failure mode. Evaluating actual goals against modular monolith vs microservices: (1) Cascade failure elimination: caused by infrastructure coupling (shared DB), not process coupling. Modular monolith with database-per-module would have prevented it. Process separation was not the relevant variable. (2) Independent deployment: the strongest genuine argument for microservices. Strict per-module deployment requires microservices. Practical deployment safety is achievable in a well-structured modular monolith. CEO's precise meaning is unverified and blocking. (3) PCI scope reduction: requires payments as a separately deployed service regardless of what other domains do. (4) Distributed consistency: modular monolith with shared database (separate schemas, same instance) preserves ACID transaction boundary spanning payments and inventory — a material consistency advantage over microservices for these two domains specifically. (5) Six months, limited budget: modular monolith substantially cheaper to operate — no service mesh, no container orchestration, no distributed tracing for in-process modules, no API versioning between internal consumers. Hybrid destination is likely correct: payments as true microservice (PCI + cascade severity), auth/inventory/orders as modular monolith with separate schemas and enforced internal API boundaries. Key risk of modular monolith: boundaries require active enforcement (ArchUnit) since network separation is not available as the enforcement mechanism.

key_claims.text: Cascade failure was caused by infrastructure coupling, not process coupling — modular monolith with separate DBs would have prevented it
Modular monolith with shared database preserves ACID transaction boundary spanning payments and inventory — consistency advantage over microservices
PCI scope reduction requires payments as a true separate service regardless of what other domains do
CEO's definition of independent deployment is unverified — strict vs practical are meaningfully different requirements
Hybrid destination (payments as microservice, auth/inventory/orders as modular monolith) is potentially the architecturally correct answer
Modular monolith boundaries require active enforcement — ArchUnit or equivalent is not optional

strands.title: Incident History and Executive Constraints
strands.content: Payments DB outage last quarter cascaded: payments DB down → auth cannot read sessions → auth down → full application outage. This incident is the direct organizational trigger for the migration. CEO issued 90-day hard mandate: auth must be independent of payments. Compliance officer introduced 6-week security audit for any new auth system and prohibition on parallel auth systems. These constraints are in direct conflict: 90 days minus 42 days (audit) leaves 48 days of build time for an auth migration of uncharacterized scope. Conflict requires three-way escalation: CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead, before implementation begins. Two resolution paths: (1) compliance officer confirms session store migration does not trigger audit — 90-day path restored, (2) CEO accepts Wave 1/Wave 2 framing. A third path — modular monolith restructuring of auth may not trigger audit at all — potentially dissolves the conflict. CEO's definition of independent deployment must be verified: if practical deployment safety satisfies the goal, modular monolith may satisfy the mandate.

key_claims.text: Payments DB outage caused auth outage caused full application outage — confirmed cascade failure
90-day auth independence from payments is a hard CEO mandate
90-day mandate and 6-week audit are in direct conflict for full auth service extraction
Conflict must be escalated to CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead simultaneously
Modular monolith restructuring of auth may not trigger the 6-week audit — potentially dissolves the conflict

strands.title: Compliance Audit Constraints
strands.content: Two hard constraints from compliance officer: (1) any new auth system requires 6-week security audit before go-live, (2) parallel auth systems prohibited — they double audit surface. These are compliance requirements, not negotiable preferences. Parallel system prohibition eliminates Strangler Fig for auth — cutover must be atomic. Atomic cutover makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk factor, which favors hybrid extraction over greenfield: hybrid carries known behavior forward, reducing novel failure surface. Critical ambiguities requiring resolution: (A) Does 'new auth system' include session store migration (infrastructure) or only service extraction/rewrite? (B) Does modular monolith restructuring of auth count as 'new auth system'? High-value clarification — if exempt, CEO/compliance conflict may dissolve. (C) Is the 6-week audit a fixed external dependency or internal process with flexibility? (D) Can auth security audit and PCI-DSS QSA process be combined? Two compliance processes potentially in play — may compete for budget and auditor scheduling. Compliance officer must answer all three questions in a single call.

key_claims.text: 6-week security audit before go-live is a hard compliance requirement for new auth systems
Parallel auth systems are prohibited — doubled audit surface is compliance-unacceptable
Whether session store migration triggers the audit is unresolved and blocking
Whether modular monolith restructuring of auth triggers the audit is unresolved with high upside if answered favorably
Atomic cutover with no rollback window makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk — favors hybrid over greenfield

strands.title: Auth and Payments Migration Strategy
strands.content: Consolidated strand covering auth approach, payments extraction, and their interdependencies. Auth has two confirmed coupling problems: (1) data layer — session tokens in payments DB, (2) application layer — ORM coupling of uncharacterized type. Session store migration is mandatory regardless of auth approach — it directly severs the availability dependency. Hybrid approach (extract auth boundary, rewrite data access layer, discard ORM coupling, carry known behavior forward) is favored over greenfield because atomic cutover with no rollback makes known behavior more valuable than clean implementation. Dual-write during session store migration must be write-new-store-first; payments DB write failures must not fail auth requests. Replacement session store must match or exceed payments DB uptime SLA — Redis Cluster is the right default. ORM coupling type (Scenario A: shared entities / B: lifecycle hooks / C: framework usage) remains uncharacterized and is blocking for build scope estimation. For payments: PCI-DSS requires extraction as a true service regardless of modular monolith consideration for other domains. Saga pattern design is a prerequisite for payments extraction — cannot be retrofitted after extraction. The outbox pattern and saga orchestrator must be designed before payments service extraction begins. Payments team's concern about eventual consistency is valid but misapplied: strong consistency is required within the payments service locally; cross-service coordination uses saga patterns with compensation, not distributed ACID. Idempotency for all payment operations is non-negotiable and cannot be retrofitted.

key_claims.text: Session token storage in payments DB is confirmed root cause of the outage cascade
Auth ORM coupling type is uncharacterized — Scenario A/B/C determination is blocking for build scope
Hybrid approach favored over greenfield because atomic cutover with no rollback makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk
Saga pattern design is a prerequisite for payments extraction, not a post-extraction concern
Idempotency for all payment operations is non-negotiable — prevents double-charging on retry, cannot be retrofitted
Dual-write must be write-new-store-first; payments DB write failures during session migration must not fail auth requests

strands.title: PCI-DSS and Payments Extraction Strategy
strands.content: PCI-DSS is a hard constraint unaffected by the modular monolith question — payments must be extracted as a true service. Current state: entire 500K LOC monolith is in PCI scope. Target: isolated payments service shrinks scope to one hardened service. Token-passing is the architectural mechanism — monolith passes payment tokens, not raw card data. Auth session tokens in the payments DB may mean auth code paths are already inside the PCI boundary — auth extraction is not PCI-free as originally assumed. Under modular monolith destination, auth module boundary enforcement and session store migration resolve co-location concern without full service extraction. Two compliance processes potentially in play: PCI-DSS QSA for payments architecture and security audit for auth system. QSA involvement is urgent. Payments extraction has an additional prerequisite from cycle 12: saga pattern design must precede extraction. Raw card data storage question remains open and is timeline-breaking if true.

key_claims.text: Payments must be extracted as a true microservice regardless of modular monolith consideration for other domains
Auth extraction is not PCI-free as originally assumed — session tokens in payments DB may implicate auth in PCI scope
Team avoidance of payments extraction perpetuates maximum PCI exposure

strands.title: Distributed Consistency and Saga Patterns
strands.content: The payments team's position — cannot accept eventual consistency for financial transactions — is correct but misapplied. It conflates two distinct requirements: (1) consistency of the payment record itself — must be strongly consistent, is a LOCAL concern within the payments service, fully achievable with local ACID transactions; (2) consistency between payment state and inventory state — the distributed coordination problem, does not require distributed ACID to solve correctly. The monolith does not provide the distributed consistency guarantee it appears to: the payment gateway API call is outside the transaction boundary; application crashes between steps create inconsistency even in the monolith. Major financial networks operate under eventual consistency with compensation, not distributed ACID. Four required patterns: (A) Outbox pattern — payment record and outbox event written in same local ACID transaction; relay publishes after commit; guarantees event publication iff payment committed. (B) Orchestration-based saga — preferred over choreography for financial workflows because full workflow is visible in one place, compensating transactions are explicit, audit trails are straightforward. Saga steps: ReserveInventory → ChargePayment → ConfirmOrder. Compensation flows backward. Reserve inventory BEFORE charging payment. (C) Idempotency — all payment operations must be idempotent via client-supplied idempotency keys. Non-negotiable. Cannot be retrofitted. (D) Reconciliation jobs — detect and heal inconsistencies outside saga compensation logic. Seam analysis must answer: do payments and inventory currently share a database transaction boundary? If yes, microservices extraction genuinely removes a consistency guarantee that modular monolith with shared database preserves.

key_claims.text: Strong consistency is required within the payments service locally — not across service boundaries
The monolith does not provide true distributed consistency — payment gateway call is outside transaction boundary
Major financial networks operate under eventual consistency with compensation, not distributed ACID
Outbox pattern guarantees event publication if and only if the local transaction commits
Idempotency for all payment operations is non-negotiable — prevents double-charging on retry
Whether payments and inventory currently share a transaction boundary is critical — answer affects destination architecture recommendation

strands.title: Inventory, Orders, and Realistic Timeline
strands.content: Inventory team proposed standalone inventory service in 3 weeks using existing API, simultaneously requesting Postgres→DynamoDB migration. Decision: conditional approval for extraction with Postgres, rejection of DynamoDB for this window. DynamoDB requires access pattern redesign, no SQL joins, different consistency model, new operational tooling, new failure modes — none required by extraction itself. Reconsidered after 3 months production usage. The 3-week claim hinges on 'existing API' meaning live HTTP endpoints vs internal Java interfaces. Seam analysis must complete before extraction begins. Monolith-side integration work is likely underestimated. Under modular monolith destination, inventory extraction becomes module boundary enforcement — retains in-process communication, eliminates distributed transaction risk. Timeline honest assessment: full 4-service microservices extraction in 6 months is not achievable. Wave 1 (MVM, months 1-5): session store migration, inventory extraction, auth/payments boundary formalization, observability. Wave 2 (months 6-12): auth extraction + audit if microservices destination, payments extraction + QSA. Under hybrid destination, the 6-month window may deliver more complete outcomes. The three unexecuted archaeology tasks — seam analysis (overdue since cycle 2), schema inspection (overdue since cycle 3), ORM coupling characterization (overdue since cycle 5) — remain the single biggest execution risk. Observability foundation (OpenTelemetry, structured logging, health endpoints) is non-negotiable under any destination architecture. Seam analysis must additionally answer whether payments and inventory share a transaction boundary.

key_claims.text: DynamoDB migration is scope creep that should be rejected for this migration window
3-week extraction claim is plausible only if 'existing API' means live HTTP endpoints and seam analysis confirms clean boundary
Full 4-service microservices extraction in 6 months is not achievable given current constraint set
Three unexecuted archaeology tasks are the single biggest execution risk — overdue since cycles 2, 3, and 5
Observability foundation is non-negotiable under any destination architecture

strands.title: Migration Success Determinant
strands.content: The single determinant of success: closing the gap between decisions made and ground truth verified, faster than the calendar consumes the window. This applies retrospectively to the planning conversation itself — four positions taken in cycle 1 required material revision as specific facts emerged: (1) Auth-first recommendation based on 'clean boundaries in most Java monoliths' — category reasoning falsified by session tokens in payments DB. (2) Microservices as unquestioned destination — should have been interrogated in cycle 1, not cycle 11. The cascade failure was infrastructure coupling, not process coupling. (3) Month-by-month plan presented with confidence inappropriate to the information available — a conditional plan with explicit archaeology gates would have been honest. (4) Team anxiety treated as psychological avoidance — it was an empirically grounded signal; the cascade failure had already happened. The pattern across all four reversals: confident claims based on how these systems usually work, falsified by how this specific system actually is. The planning conversation modeled the exact anti-pattern it identified as the success determinant. Architectural advice without system-specific verification is a hypothesis, not a recommendation. The three archaeology tasks overdue since cycles 2, 3, and 5 are the most concrete expression of the failure mode — they represent verified facts that would resolve blocking questions, repeatedly deferred in favor of continued planning against assumptions.

key_claims.text: The blocking factor throughout planning has been organizational avoidance of ground truth verification, not technical complexity
Initial auth-first recommendation was falsified by session tokens in payments DB — category reasoning applied without system-specific verification
Microservices assumption should have been interrogated in cycle 1 — was accepted without verifying it satisfied stated goals
Team anxiety about payments was empirically grounded — the cascade failure had already happened
Architectural advice without system-specific verification is a hypothesis, not a recommendation

If so, the tensor represents a complete planning engagement with hybrid destination as the likely correct architecture, three unexecuted archaeology tasks as the critical path, and compliance escalation as the urgent governance action. (B) Team asks for a consolidated action list — respond with: (1) compliance officer call this week covering all three audit scope questions, (2) CEO conversation on independent deployment definition, (3) seam analysis starting today, (4) schema inspection in parallel, (5) ORM coupling characterization with team lead. (C) Implementation questions begin — if so, establish destination architecture confirmation as a prerequisite before any implementation guidance. The hybrid destination (payments as microservice, others as modular monolith) is the working assumption but is not confirmed until CEO defines independent deployment. (D) Payments team re-engages on saga implementation specifics — prepare concrete orchestration-based saga walkthrough for payment+inventory coordination, with idempotency key design as the first implementation concern. The reversals are evidence the process worked correctly — positions updated when facts changed. The concern would be if positions had not updated.

Open questions reduced from 11 to 7 by merging four questions into broader questions or embedding their resolution paths in strand content. No blocking question was dropped — all dropped questions are subsumed.

The epistemic retrospective from cycle 13 was the most interesting curation challenge: it deserved a strand but its content was most naturally a coda to the Migration Success Determinant rather than an independent thread. Absorbed rather than maintained separately.

Strand dependency graph is now cleaner — fewer strands means fewer cross-dependencies. The circular dependency flagged in cycle 12 (Architecture Pattern Validity ↔ Distributed Consistency) is resolved by having Distributed Consistency depend on Architecture Pattern Validity but not vice versa.
