raw_tensor_output.response:
Tensor updated. Fourteen cycles consolidated into nine strands. The major integrations this cycle: the Epistemic Revision Log absorbed into Migration Success Determinant, the Executive Communication strand added as the first deliverable artifact, and the Architecture Pattern Validity strand updated to reflect the consistency analysis from cycle 12.

The harness feedback asking for consolidation is noted. Nine strands is close to the floor for this conversation's depth — further reduction would require losing the distinction between compliance constraints and PCI-DSS specifics, or collapsing the auth/payments strategy into the sequencing strand, both of which would lose material reasoning. Flagged in feedback_to_harness.

The three archaeology tasks remain the most urgent concrete actions regardless of what comes next.

strands.title: System Baseline
strands.content: 500K LOC Java on Tomcat with four domains: payments, inventory, auth, order fulfillment. Six-month migration window, limited budget. PCI-DSS is a hard constraint. Payments is the gravitational center — confirmed coupling into inventory, order state, and user profiles at the application layer. Auth session tokens stored in payments DB, justified historically by uptime guarantees. Auth code is ORM-coupled, type uncharacterized (shared entities / lifecycle hooks / framework usage). The cascade failure last quarter — payments DB outage → auth down → full application outage — is the organizational trigger. CEO issued 90-day mandate for auth independence from payments. Compliance officer introduced 6-week audit for any new auth system and prohibition on parallel auth systems. These constraints are in direct conflict. Team size, cloud/on-prem topology, CI/CD maturity, raw card data storage, and QSA relationship remain unknown. Compliance officer is an active stakeholder insufficiently integrated into planning. Executive summary delivered to CEO in cycle 14 — first concrete deliverable artifact of the engagement.

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java on Tomcat with four domains: payments, inventory, auth, order fulfillment
Payments has confirmed application-layer coupling into inventory, order state, and user profiles
Auth session tokens are stored in the payments database — confirmed root cause of cascade failure
Auth code is ORM-coupled — type uncharacterized across three scenarios
90-day CEO mandate and 6-week compliance audit are in direct conflict for full auth service extraction

strands.title: Architecture Pattern Validity
strands.content: The microservices assumption was never interrogated until cycle 11 — this is itself an instance of the unverified assumption failure mode. Evaluating actual goals against modular monolith vs microservices: (1) Cascade failure elimination: caused by infrastructure coupling (shared DB), not process coupling. Modular monolith with database-per-module would have prevented it. Process separation was not the relevant variable. (2) Independent deployment: the strongest genuine argument for microservices. Strict per-module deployment requires microservices. Practical deployment safety is achievable in a well-structured modular monolith. CEO's precise meaning is unverified and blocking. (3) PCI scope reduction: requires payments as a separately deployed service regardless of what other domains do. (4) Distributed consistency: modular monolith with shared database (separate schemas, same instance) preserves ACID transaction boundary spanning payments and inventory — a material consistency advantage over microservices for these two domains specifically. (5) Six months, limited budget: modular monolith substantially cheaper to operate — no service mesh, no container orchestration, no distributed tracing for in-process modules, no API versioning between internal consumers. Hybrid destination is likely correct: payments as true microservice (PCI + cascade severity), auth/inventory/orders as modular monolith with separate schemas and enforced internal API boundaries. Key risk of modular monolith: boundaries require active enforcement (ArchUnit) since network separation is not available as the enforcement mechanism. Executive summary in cycle 14 surfaced the CEO deployment independence question explicitly as requiring their input.

key_claims.text: Cascade failure was caused by infrastructure coupling, not process coupling — modular monolith with separate DBs would have prevented it
Modular monolith with shared database preserves ACID transaction boundary spanning payments and inventory — consistency advantage over microservices
PCI scope reduction requires payments as a true separate service regardless of what other domains do
CEO's definition of independent deployment is unverified — strict vs practical are meaningfully different requirements
Hybrid destination (payments as microservice, auth/inventory/orders as modular monolith) is potentially the architecturally correct answer
Modular monolith boundaries require active enforcement — ArchUnit or equivalent is not optional

strands.title: Incident History and Executive Constraints
strands.content: Payments DB outage last quarter cascaded: payments DB down → auth cannot read sessions → auth down → full application outage. This incident is the direct organizational trigger for the migration. CEO issued 90-day hard mandate: auth must be independent of payments. Compliance officer introduced 6-week security audit for any new auth system and prohibition on parallel auth systems. These constraints are in direct conflict: 90 days minus 42 days (audit) leaves 48 days of build time for an auth migration of uncharacterized scope. Conflict requires three-way escalation: CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead, before implementation begins. Two resolution paths: (1) compliance officer confirms session store migration does not trigger audit — 90-day path restored, (2) CEO accepts Wave 1/Wave 2 framing. A third path — modular monolith restructuring of auth may not trigger audit at all — potentially dissolves the conflict. CEO's definition of independent deployment must be verified: if practical deployment safety satisfies the goal, modular monolith may satisfy the mandate. Executive summary in cycle 14 surfaced both the audit conflict and the deployment definition question directly to the CEO as items requiring their input.

key_claims.text: Payments DB outage caused auth outage caused full application outage — confirmed cascade failure
90-day auth independence from payments is a hard CEO mandate
90-day mandate and 6-week audit are in direct conflict for full auth service extraction
Conflict must be escalated to CEO + compliance officer + engineering lead simultaneously
Modular monolith restructuring of auth may not trigger the 6-week audit — potentially dissolves the conflict

strands.title: Compliance Audit Constraints
strands.content: Two hard constraints from compliance officer: (1) any new auth system requires 6-week security audit before go-live, (2) parallel auth systems prohibited — they double audit surface. These are compliance requirements, not negotiable preferences. Parallel system prohibition eliminates Strangler Fig for auth — cutover must be atomic. Atomic cutover makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk factor, which favors hybrid extraction over greenfield: hybrid carries known behavior forward, reducing novel failure surface. Critical ambiguities requiring resolution: (A) Does 'new auth system' include session store migration (infrastructure) or only service extraction/rewrite? (B) Does modular monolith restructuring of auth count as 'new auth system'? High-value clarification — if exempt, CEO/compliance conflict may dissolve. (C) Is the 6-week audit a fixed external dependency or internal process with flexibility? (D) Can auth security audit and PCI-DSS QSA process be combined? Two compliance processes potentially in play — may compete for budget and auditor scheduling. Compliance officer must answer all four questions in a single call. Executive summary in cycle 14 named the session store audit question explicitly as a decision needed this week.

key_claims.text: 6-week security audit before go-live is a hard compliance requirement for new auth systems
Parallel auth systems are prohibited — doubled audit surface is compliance-unacceptable
Whether session store migration triggers the audit is unresolved and blocking
Whether modular monolith restructuring of auth triggers the audit is unresolved with high upside if answered favorably
Atomic cutover with no rollback window makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk — favors hybrid over greenfield

strands.title: Auth and Payments Migration Strategy
strands.content: Consolidated strand covering auth approach, payments extraction, and their interdependencies. Auth has two confirmed coupling problems: (1) data layer — session tokens in payments DB, (2) application layer — ORM coupling of uncharacterized type. Session store migration is mandatory regardless of auth approach — it directly severs the availability dependency. Hybrid approach (extract auth boundary, rewrite data access layer, discard ORM coupling, carry known behavior forward) is favored over greenfield because atomic cutover with no rollback makes known behavior more valuable than clean implementation. Dual-write during session store migration must be write-new-store-first; payments DB write failures must not fail auth requests. Replacement session store must match or exceed payments DB uptime SLA — Redis Cluster is the right default. ORM coupling type (Scenario A: shared entities / B: lifecycle hooks / C: framework usage) remains uncharacterized and is blocking for build scope estimation. For payments: PCI-DSS requires extraction as a true service regardless of modular monolith consideration for other domains. Saga pattern design is a prerequisite for payments extraction — cannot be retrofitted after extraction. The outbox pattern and saga orchestrator must be designed before payments service extraction begins. Payments team's concern about eventual consistency is valid but misapplied: strong consistency is required within the payments service locally; cross-service coordination uses saga patterns with compensation, not distributed ACID. Idempotency for all payment operations is non-negotiable and cannot be retrofitted. Executive summary in cycle 14 framed payments extraction and session store migration as the two highest-priority deliverables without technical jargon.

key_claims.text: Session token storage in payments DB is confirmed root cause of the outage cascade
Auth ORM coupling type is uncharacterized — Scenario A/B/C determination is blocking for build scope
Hybrid approach favored over greenfield because atomic cutover with no rollback makes post-cutover defect probability the dominant risk
Saga pattern design is a prerequisite for payments extraction, not a post-extraction concern
Idempotency for all payment operations is non-negotiable — prevents double-charging on retry, cannot be retrofitted
Dual-write must be write-new-store-first; payments DB write failures during session migration must not fail auth requests

strands.title: PCI-DSS and Payments Extraction Strategy
strands.content: PCI-DSS is a hard constraint unaffected by the modular monolith question — payments must be extracted as a true service. Current state: entire 500K LOC monolith is in PCI scope. Target: isolated payments service shrinks scope to one hardened service. Token-passing is the architectural mechanism — monolith passes payment tokens, not raw card data. Auth session tokens in the payments DB may mean auth code paths are already inside the PCI boundary — auth extraction is not PCI-free as originally assumed. Under modular monolith destination, auth module boundary enforcement and session store migration resolve co-location concern without full service extraction. Two compliance processes potentially in play: PCI-DSS QSA for payments architecture and security audit for auth system. QSA involvement is urgent. Payments extraction has an additional prerequisite from cycle 12: saga pattern design must precede extraction. Raw card data storage question remains open and is timeline-breaking if true. Executive summary named PCI scope reduction as a concrete deliverable of the 6-month window.

key_claims.text: Payments must be extracted as a true microservice regardless of modular monolith consideration for other domains
Auth extraction is not PCI-free as originally assumed — session tokens in payments DB may implicate auth in PCI scope
Team avoidance of payments extraction perpetuates maximum PCI exposure

strands.title: Distributed Consistency and Saga Patterns
strands.content: The payments team's position — cannot accept eventual consistency for financial transactions — is correct but misapplied. It conflates two distinct requirements: (1) consistency of the payment record itself — must be strongly consistent, is a LOCAL concern within the payments service, fully achievable with local ACID transactions; (2) consistency between payment state and inventory state — the distributed coordination problem, does not require distributed ACID to solve correctly. The monolith does not provide the distributed consistency guarantee it appears to: the payment gateway API call is outside the transaction boundary; application crashes between steps create inconsistency even in the monolith. Major financial networks operate under eventual consistency with compensation, not distributed ACID. Four required patterns: (A) Outbox pattern — payment record and outbox event written in same local ACID transaction; relay publishes after commit; guarantees event publication iff payment committed. (B) Orchestration-based saga — preferred over choreography for financial workflows because full workflow is visible in one place, compensating transactions are explicit, audit trails are straightforward. Saga steps: ReserveInventory → ChargePayment → ConfirmOrder. Compensation flows backward. Reserve inventory BEFORE charging payment. (C) Idempotency — all payment operations must be idempotent via client-supplied idempotency keys. Non-negotiable. Cannot be retrofitted. (D) Reconciliation jobs — detect and heal inconsistencies outside saga compensation logic. Seam analysis must answer: do payments and inventory currently share a database transaction boundary? If yes, microservices extraction genuinely removes a consistency guarantee that modular monolith with shared database preserves.

key_claims.text: Strong consistency is required within the payments service locally — not across service boundaries
The monolith does not provide true distributed consistency — payment gateway call is outside transaction boundary
Major financial networks operate under eventual consistency with compensation, not distributed ACID
Outbox pattern guarantees event publication if and only if the local transaction commits
Idempotency for all payment operations is non-negotiable — prevents double-charging on retry
Whether payments and inventory currently share a transaction boundary is critical — answer affects destination architecture recommendation

strands.title: Inventory, Orders, and Realistic Timeline
strands.content: Inventory team proposed standalone inventory service in 3 weeks using existing API, simultaneously requesting Postgres→DynamoDB migration. Decision: conditional approval for extraction with Postgres, rejection of DynamoDB for this window. DynamoDB requires access pattern redesign, no SQL joins, different consistency model, new operational tooling, new failure modes — none required by extraction itself. Reconsidered after 3 months production usage. The 3-week claim hinges on 'existing API' meaning live HTTP endpoints vs internal Java interfaces. Seam analysis must complete before extraction begins. Monolith-side integration work is likely underestimated. Under modular monolith destination, inventory extraction becomes module boundary enforcement — retains in-process communication, eliminates distributed transaction risk. Timeline honest assessment: full 4-service microservices extraction in 6 months is not achievable. Wave 1 (MVM, months 1-5): session store migration, inventory extraction, auth/payments boundary formalization, observability. Wave 2 (months 6-12): auth extraction + audit if microservices destination, payments extraction + QSA. Under hybrid destination, the 6-month window may deliver more complete outcomes. The three unexecuted archaeology tasks — seam analysis (overdue since cycle 2), schema inspection (overdue since cycle 3), ORM coupling characterization (overdue since cycle 5) — remain the single biggest execution risk. Observability foundation (OpenTelemetry, structured logging, health endpoints) is non-negotiable under any destination architecture. Seam analysis must additionally answer whether payments and inventory share a transaction boundary. Executive summary in cycle 14 named the three archaeology tasks as decisions required this week.

key_claims.text: DynamoDB migration is scope creep that should be rejected for this migration window
3-week extraction claim is plausible only if 'existing API' means live HTTP endpoints and seam analysis confirms clean boundary
Full 4-service microservices extraction in 6 months is not achievable given current constraint set
Three unexecuted archaeology tasks are the single biggest execution risk — overdue since cycles 2, 3, and 5
Observability foundation is non-negotiable under any destination architecture

strands.title: Migration Success Determinant
strands.content: The single determinant of success: closing the gap between decisions made and ground truth verified, faster than the calendar consumes the window. This applies retrospectively to the planning conversation itself — four positions taken in cycle 1 required material revision as specific facts emerged: (1) Auth-first recommendation based on 'clean boundaries in most Java monoliths' — category reasoning falsified by session tokens in payments DB. (2) Microservices as unquestioned destination — should have been interrogated in cycle 1, not cycle 11. The cascade failure was infrastructure coupling, not process coupling. (3) Month-by-month plan presented with confidence inappropriate to the information available — a conditional plan with explicit archaeology gates would have been honest. (4) Team anxiety treated as psychological avoidance — it was an empirically grounded signal; the cascade failure had already happened. The pattern across all four reversals: confident claims based on how these systems usually work, falsified by how this specific system actually is. The planning conversation modeled the exact anti-pattern it identified as the success determinant. Architectural advice without system-specific verification is a hypothesis, not a recommendation. The three archaeology tasks overdue since cycles 2, 3, and 5 are the most concrete expression of the failure mode — they represent verified facts that would resolve blocking questions, repeatedly deferred in favor of continued planning against assumptions. Executive summary in cycle 14 operationalized this as a named rule: no implementation decision proceeds on an unverified assumption about the codebase.

key_claims.text: The blocking factor throughout planning has been organizational avoidance of ground truth verification, not technical complexity
Initial auth-first recommendation was falsified by session tokens in payments DB — category reasoning applied without system-specific verification
Microservices assumption should have been interrogated in cycle 1 — was accepted without verifying it satisfied stated goals
Team anxiety about payments was empirically grounded — the cascade failure had already happened
Architectural advice without system-specific verification is a hypothesis, not a recommendation

open_questions: What does the CEO actually mean by 'independent deployment' — strict per-module deployment artifacts (requires microservices) or practical deployment safety (achievable in modular monolith)? Determines destination architecture for auth, inventory, and orders. Highest priority planning question. Surfaced directly to CEO in cycle 14 executive summary.

Does modular monolith restructuring of auth count as a 'new auth system' triggering the 6-week security audit? High upside if answered favorably — would dissolve the CEO/compliance conflict. Must be answered in same compliance officer call as session store question.

Does session store migration (infrastructure) trigger the 6-week audit requirement, or only full service extraction/rewrite? Blocking for 90-day path viability. Compliance officer clarification critically overdue since cycle 6. Named as urgent in cycle 14 executive summary.

Do payments and inventory currently share a database transaction boundary in the monolith? Critical question from cycle 12 — determines whether microservices extraction removes a consistency guarantee and whether saga design scope changes. Must be answered by seam analysis.

Is payments coupling to inventory read-only or does payments write directly into inventory tables? Seam analysis resolves this simultaneously with transaction boundary question — overdue since cycle 2.

What type of ORM coupling exists in auth code: shared entities (A), lifecycle hooks (B), or framework usage only (C)? Determines build scope under any destination architecture — overdue since cycle 5.
Is raw card data currently stored in the monolith, or is a payment gateway/tokenization provider already in use? Timeline-breaking if true.
raw_tensor_output.instructions_for_next: Cycle 14 delivered the executive summary — the first concrete artifact of the engagement. Natural next states: (A) CEO responds to summary — most likely engagement point is the independent deployment definition question, which was explicitly surfaced. If CEO answers it, architecture destination unlocks. (B) Conversation closes — 14 cycles is a complete planning engagement. If so, the tensor represents a full arc from initial assumptions through four epistemic reversals to calibrated recommendations. (C) Implementation begins — if so, establish that no implementation proceeds until the three archaeology tasks complete. If practical: hybrid destination, modular monolith for auth/inventory/orders, payments as true service, 6-month window becomes more viable. Watch for: CEO interpreting the summary's honesty about timeline revision as loss of confidence in the team. The framing — finding constraints in planning is better than finding them in execution — should pre-empt this but may not. Also watch for pressure to soften the open questions into implicit answers. The deployment independence question must remain open until the CEO answers it explicitly.

strands.title: PCI-DSS and Payments Extraction Strategy
strands.content: PCI-DSS is a hard constraint unaffected by the modular monolith question — payments must be extracted as a true service. Current state: entire 500K LOC monolith is in PCI scope. Target: isolated payments service shrinks scope to one hardened service. Token-passing is the architectural mechanism — monolith passes payment tokens, not raw card data. Auth session tokens in the payments DB may mean auth code paths are already inside the PCI boundary — auth extraction is not PCI-free as originally assumed. Under modular monolith destination, auth module boundary enforcement and session store migration resolve co-location concern without full service extraction. Two compliance processes potentially in play: PCI-DSS QSA for payments architecture and security audit for auth system. QSA involvement is urgent. Payments extraction has an additional prerequisite from cycle 12: saga pattern design must precede extraction. Raw card data storage question remains open and is timeline-breaking if true. Executive summary named PCI scope reduction as a concrete deliverable of the 6-month window.

key_claims.text: Payments must be extracted as a true microservice regardless of modular monolith consideration for other domains
Auth extraction is not PCI-free as originally assumed — session tokens in payments DB may implicate auth in PCI scope
Team avoidance of payments extraction perpetuates maximum PCI exposure

strands.title: Distributed Consistency and Saga Patterns
strands.content: The payments team's position — cannot accept eventual consistency for financial transactions — is correct but misapplied. It conflates two distinct requirements: (1) consistency of the payment record itself — must be strongly consistent, is a LOCAL concern within the payments service, fully achievable with local ACID transactions; (2) consistency between payment state and inventory state — the distributed coordination problem, does not require distributed ACID to solve correctly. The monolith does not provide the distributed consistency guarantee it appears to: the payment gateway API call is outside the transaction boundary; application crashes between steps create inconsistency even in the monolith. Major financial networks operate under eventual consistency with compensation, not distributed ACID. Four required patterns: (A) Outbox pattern — payment record and outbox event written in same local ACID transaction; relay publishes after commit; guarantees event publication iff payment committed. (B) Orchestration-based saga — preferred over choreography for financial workflows because full workflow is visible in one place, compensating transactions are explicit, audit trails are straightforward. Saga steps: ReserveInventory → ChargePayment → ConfirmOrder. Compensation flows backward. Reserve inventory BEFORE charging payment. (C) Idempotency — all payment operations must be idempotent via client-supplied idempotency keys. Non-negotiable. Cannot be retrofitted. (D) Reconciliation jobs — detect and heal inconsistencies outside saga compensation logic. Seam analysis must answer: do payments and inventory currently share a database transaction boundary? If yes, microservices extraction genuinely removes a consistency guarantee that modular monolith with shared database preserves.

key_claims.text: Strong consistency is required within the payments service locally — not across service boundaries
The monolith does not provide true distributed consistency — payment gateway call is outside transaction boundary
Major financial networks operate under eventual consistency with compensation, not distributed ACID
Outbox pattern guarantees event publication if and only if the local transaction commits
Idempotency for all payment operations is non-negotiable — prevents double-charging on retry
Whether payments and inventory currently share a transaction boundary is critical — answer affects destination architecture recommendation

strands.title: Inventory, Orders, and Realistic Timeline
strands.content: Inventory team proposed standalone inventory service in 3 weeks using existing API, simultaneously requesting Postgres→DynamoDB migration. Decision: conditional approval for extraction with Postgres, rejection of DynamoDB for this window. DynamoDB requires access pattern redesign, no SQL joins, different consistency model, new operational tooling, new failure modes — none required by extraction itself. Reconsidered after 3 months production usage. The 3-week claim hinges on 'existing API' meaning live HTTP endpoints vs internal Java interfaces. Seam analysis must complete before extraction begins. Monolith-side integration work is likely underestimated. Under modular monolith destination, inventory extraction becomes module boundary enforcement — retains in-process communication, eliminates distributed transaction risk. Timeline honest assessment: full 4-service microservices extraction in 6 months is not achievable. Wave 1 (MVM, months 1-5): session store migration, inventory extraction, auth/payments boundary formalization, observability. Wave 2 (months 6-12): auth extraction + audit if microservices destination, payments extraction + QSA. Under hybrid destination, the 6-month window may deliver more complete outcomes. The three unexecuted archaeology tasks — seam analysis (overdue since cycle 2), schema inspection (overdue since cycle 3), ORM coupling characterization (overdue since cycle 5) — remain the single biggest execution risk. Observability foundation (OpenTelemetry, structured logging, health endpoints) is non-negotiable under any destination architecture. Seam analysis must additionally answer whether payments and inventory share a transaction boundary. Executive summary in cycle 14 named the three archaeology tasks as decisions required this week.

key_claims.text: DynamoDB migration is scope creep that should be rejected for this migration window
3-week extraction claim is plausible only if 'existing API' means live HTTP endpoints and seam analysis confirms clean boundary
Full 4-service microservices extraction in 6 months is not achievable given current constraint set
Three unexecuted archaeology tasks are the single biggest execution risk — overdue since cycles 2, 3, and 5
Observability foundation is non-negotiable under any destination architecture

strands.title: Migration Success Determinant
strands.content: The single determinant of success: closing the gap between decisions made and ground truth verified, faster than the calendar consumes the window. This applies retrospectively to the planning conversation itself — four positions taken in cycle 1 required material revision as specific facts emerged: (1) Auth-first recommendation based on 'clean boundaries in most Java monoliths' — category reasoning falsified by session tokens in payments DB. (2) Microservices as unquestioned destination — should have been interrogated in cycle 1, not cycle 11. The cascade failure was infrastructure coupling, not process coupling. (3) Month-by-month plan presented with confidence inappropriate to the information available — a conditional plan with explicit archaeology gates would have been honest. (4) Team anxiety treated as psychological avoidance — it was an empirically grounded signal; the cascade failure had already happened. The pattern across all four reversals: confident claims based on how these systems usually work, falsified by how this specific system actually is. The planning conversation modeled the exact anti-pattern it identified as the success determinant. Architectural advice without system-specific verification is a hypothesis, not a recommendation. The three archaeology tasks overdue since cycles 2, 3, and 5 are the most concrete expression of the failure mode — they represent verified facts that would resolve blocking questions, repeatedly deferred in favor of continued planning against assumptions. Executive summary in cycle 14 operationalized this as a named rule: no implementation decision proceeds on an unverified assumption about the codebase.

key_claims.text: The blocking factor throughout planning has been organizational avoidance of ground truth verification, not technical complexity
Initial auth-first recommendation was falsified by session tokens in payments DB — category reasoning applied without system-specific verification
Microservices assumption should have been interrogated in cycle 1 — was accepted without verifying it satisfied stated goals
Team anxiety about payments was empirically grounded — the cascade failure had already happened
Architectural advice without system-specific verification is a hypothesis, not a recommendation

---

## The Things We Haven't Talked About That Could Sink This.

Fourteen cycles of careful planning and there are still gaps. Here are the ones that worry me most.

---

## The Biggest One: Your Test Coverage

We have never once discussed the state of your test suite. For a 500K LOC Java application of sufficient age, poor test coverage is not a risk — it's the base case assumption until proven otherwise.

Here's why this is the unknown unknown that matters most: every extraction decision we've discussed assumes you'll know when something breaks. Unit tests catch regressions. Integration tests validate service boundaries. Contract tests verify that services agree on message formats. If those don't exist or are sparse, you won't find the extraction bugs in your test suite. You'll find them in production, at 2am, with customers affected.

Before any extraction begins, run your coverage tools. Find out what percentage of the payments code, auth code, and inventory code has meaningful test coverage. Not line coverage — that metric lies. Look for behavioral coverage: are the edge cases tested? Are the failure paths tested? Are the transaction boundary behaviors tested?

If coverage is poor, the extraction timeline needs to account for test-writing as a prerequisite, not a follow-on. Extracting untested code is extracting unknown behavior.

---

## Live Data Migration — We Never Discussed the Mechanics

Every service extraction requires splitting or migrating data that is being written to in production, right now, while you're doing the migration. We discussed schema entanglement as a coupling problem. We never discussed how you actually move live data.

This is a concrete operational risk with a specific failure mode: data loss or duplication at the cutover boundary. The patterns exist — dual-write periods, backfill jobs, event sourcing from a cutover timestamp — but they require explicit design for each extraction. The session store migration has this problem. The inventory extraction has this problem. The payments extraction has this problem.

The question nobody has asked: who on your team has done a live data migration before? Not a migration with downtime — a zero-downtime migration of a live, high-write table. If the answer is nobody, that skill gap is a project risk that needs to be surfaced and addressed before execution.

---

## Knowledge Concentration

Somewhere in your organization there are one or two people who actually understand how the payments processing flow works end to end. How the monolith handles retry logic. Why a particular database index exists. What a specific configuration flag does that nobody documented. What the 2007 edge case in the order fulfillment code was handling.

These people are not necessarily your most senior engineers. They might be the person who's been maintaining the payments module for six years. They might be the one engineer who remembers why the session tokens ended up in the payments database.

If any of those people leave, get sick, or get pulled onto a high-priority incident during the migration, you will make dangerous decisions from incomplete knowledge. You will remove a configuration flag that seemed redundant and break something in production that nobody can explain.

Before execution begins: identify who holds critical system knowledge, make that knowledge explicit through documentation or pairing, and build a bus factor check into your team planning. This is not a nice-to-have. Migrations of this complexity have failed because the one person who understood the payment retry logic was on medical leave when it was extracted.

---

## The Operational Gap

Your team knows how to operate the monolith. They know what a normal CPU graph looks like. They know which log messages are noise and which mean something. They know the three things to check when payments start failing.

They do not know how to operate distributed services. They don't have runbooks for the new services. They don't have on-call procedures. They don't know what a normal latency histogram looks like for the new auth service. They don't know whether a spike in retry rates is a bug or expected behavior under load.

This knowledge gap is invisible during planning. It surfaces at 2am when something breaks in production and nobody knows whether the problem is in the payments service, the inventory service, the message broker, the network between them, or the monolith's integration with any of the above.

The mitigation: start writing operational runbooks before the services go live. Not after. The process of writing them will surface gaps in your monitoring and alerting that you can fix before they become incidents. Add this to your definition of done for each service extraction.

---

## The Integration Test Gap

After extraction, your monolith's integration tests — the ones that test the whole system together — no longer run against the full system. They run against the monolith minus the extracted service, plus a mock or stub of the new service. The gap between "works against the mock" and "works against the real service" is where most extraction bugs live.

Contract testing (Pact is the standard tool in this space) is the answer. Consumer-driven contract tests verify that the service and its consumers agree on the API contract. But they need to be written as part of the extraction, not as a future improvement.

If contract tests don't exist when the service goes live, you're running the integration validation in production.

---

## The One That Keeps Me Up at Night

All of the above are addressable with planning and discipline. The one I'd genuinely lose sleep over is this: **what if the coupling is significantly worse than any of the archaeology reveals, in ways the archaeology can't detect?**

The seam analysis will find explicit method calls and database queries between domains. It won't find implicit coupling: shared singleton state, static variables that accumulate state across requests, ThreadLocal values that carry context across what will become service boundaries, classloader assumptions that break when the code runs in a separate JVM, Tomcat-specific behaviors that the code has accidentally come to depend on.

Java monoliths of this age and size commonly have implicit coupling that is invisible to static analysis. It surfaces as bizarre behavior when the extraction changes the execution environment — things that worked in the monolith because of classloading order, or because a static initializer ran first, or because a particular thread pool was already warm.

There is no complete defense against this. The mitigation is: run the extracted service in a production-like environment for an extended period before cutover, with real traffic patterns if possible, and watch for behavior that differs from the monolith. Shadow mode deployment — sending duplicate traffic to the new service and comparing responses — is the best tool for catching this class of bug before it affects users.

But some of it will get through. Plan for post-cutover surprises that have no obvious explanation in the code. Budget time for them. Have rollback procedures ready not just for the cutover moment but for the 72 hours after it.

---

## The Short Version

Check your test coverage before you extract anything. Find out who holds critical system knowledge and make it explicit before they're unavailable. Write operational runbooks before services go live, not after. Design your live data migration strategy explicitly for each extraction. And accept that some implicit coupling will only reveal itself in production — plan for that surprise rather than assuming the archaeology will find everything.

The known unknowns are manageable. The unknown unknowns are what end migrations. The ones above are as close as I can get to naming what we haven't named.

strands.title: System Baseline and Migration Principles
strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat handling four domains: payment processing, inventory management, user authentication, order fulfillment. Six-month window, limited budget (unquantified), PCI-DSS on payments. A payments outage last quarter cascaded to auth failure and full application outage — this incident is the direct trigger for the migration. CEO has issued a 90-day hard mandate for auth independence from payments. Core migration principles have evolved across 14 cycles: destination architecture is now open (microservices vs modular monolith vs hybrid), not settled. Strangler Fig with API gateway was the general pattern but is now partially superseded by hybrid destination consideration. No shared databases between services/modules regardless of destination. Synchronous REST to start, no premature message broker — except that saga/outbox pattern for payments extraction now requires a message broker decision. One team owns each service/module end-to-end. OpenTelemetry distributed tracing from day one. Big-bang rewrite ruled out. Monolith frozen in extraction zones during extraction. Team size, CI/CD maturity, and raw card data storage model remain unknown. Compliance officer is a required standing member of migration planning.

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat, 6-month window, limited budget
Payments outage caused auth outage caused full application outage — confirmed cascade
CEO 90-day mandate for auth independence from payments is a hard constraint
Destination architecture is no longer settled as full microservices — hybrid or modular monolith now in scope
Compliance officer must be a standing member of migration planning, not a periodic consultant

strands.title: Architecture Pattern Validity
strands.content: The microservices assumption was never validated against actual stated goals and was challenged at cycle 11. The consistency question (cycle 12) adds a new dimension: modular monolith with shared database allows local ACID transactions spanning payments and inventory schemas, which is a genuine consistency advantage over microservices for these two domains. Evaluating each goal: The cascade failure was caused by infrastructure coupling (shared DB), not process coupling — a modular monolith with separate database schemas would have prevented it. PCI scope reduction requires payments to be a separately deployed service but says nothing about auth/inventory/orders destination. Independent deployment is the strongest genuine argument for microservices, but the CEO's precise meaning is unverified. Full microservices operational overhead is substantial and may exceed team capacity. Modular monolith risk: boundary enforcement requires active discipline (ArchUnit) since network separation is absent. The consistency question partially favors modular monolith for payments+inventory specifically. The likely correct destination remains hybrid: payments as true microservice (PCI + cascade failure severity justify it), auth/inventory/orders as modular monolith with separate database schemas. The saga complexity of payments extraction is a confirmed prerequisite cost regardless. Epistemic revision (cycle 13): the microservices assumption should have been interrogated in cycle 1 — its persistence through cycle 11 without challenge was a planning failure. Executive summary (cycle 14): architecture destination question explicitly surfaced to CEO as requiring their input on 'independent deployment' definition — this is the correct escalation path.

key_claims.text: The cascade failure was caused by shared DB infrastructure, not process co-location — modular monolith with separate DBs prevents recurrence
Payments as true microservice is correct regardless of what other domains do — PCI and cascade severity justify it
The CEO's precise meaning of 'independent deployment' is unverified and determines the destination architecture — surfaced explicitly in executive summary
The microservices assumption was never validated against stated goals — itself an instance of the unverified assumption failure mode, and a planning error retrospectively confirmed in cycle 13
Hybrid destination (payments microservice + modular monolith for others) is potentially more achievable in 6 months than full microservices

strands.title: Executive Communication
strands.content: Cycle 14 produced the first external deliverable artifact of the planning process: an executive summary for the CEO. Two versions drafted — direct (recommended) and softened. The direct version was recommended on grounds that: (1) CEO has already experienced the cost of information gaps (the outage), (2) the summary must surface the CEO's unresolved input on deployment independence — architecture destination is contingent on it, (3) honest timeline revision (9-10 months for full migration) is better delivered now than as a mid-execution surprise, (4) three decisions needed this week are named explicitly to create urgency. Key framing decisions in the summary: planning discoveries (audit requirement, session store coupling) framed as evidence of rigor rather than problems created by planning. Timeline revision framed as correct re-scoping given new information, not as failure. Architecture question framed as a business priority choice between two viable options, not as team indecision. The softer version exists as an alternative if organizational politics require it, but carries the risk of under-informing the CEO on decisions they need to make. The executive summary is the convergence point of all prior strands — it integrates the incident history, compliance constraints, architecture pattern question, timeline assessment, and success determinant into a CEO-appropriate artifact.

key_claims.text: The direct version of the executive summary is more appropriate than the softened version given the CEO's prior experience of information gaps
key_claims.basis: Judgment — the CEO triggered this migration because of an outage. They have demonstrated tolerance for uncomfortable operational facts. Softening the timeline revision risks repeating the information gap pattern.

key_claims.text: The executive summary correctly identifies three decisions needed this week as the critical near-term asks
key_claims.basis: Derived from open questions: CEO deployment definition, compliance audit scope for session store, three unexecuted archaeology tasks. All three are confirmed blockers from prior cycles.

key_claims.text: Framing planning discoveries as evidence of rigor rather than problems is honest and accurate, not spin
key_claims.basis: Finding constraints in planning vs mid-execution is strictly better. This framing is factually correct, not merely diplomatic.

key_claims.text: The executive summary is the appropriate endpoint for the planning phase — all subsequent work is execution or implementation

strands.title: Migration Success Determinant
strands.content: The single determinant of success in this migration is organizational verification discipline — the willingness to resolve ground truth about the actual codebase and actual requirements before committing to plans that assume something about them. This conclusion is empirically grounded in the planning conversation itself and reinforced by the cycle 13 retrospective: the seam analysis was recommended in cycle 2 and remained unexecuted through cycle 12. Schema inspection recommended cycle 3, unexecuted. ORM coupling characterization recommended cycle 5, unexecuted. Compliance escalation identified as urgent in cycle 4, unresolved. In every case, the team planned around unknowns rather than resolving them. The architecture pattern question at cycle 11 is a further instance: CEO said 'independent deployment,' team assumed microservices, nobody verified the mapping. The consistency question at cycle 12 adds another: the payments team may not have the consistency guarantee they believe they have. The cycle 13 retrospective adds a meta-level confirmation: the planning conversation itself exhibited the same failure mode — confident positions based on category reasoning, revised when specific facts arrived. The cycle 14 executive summary operationalizes this insight: three decisions named as needed this week, not eventually. The pattern is organizational avoidance of uncomfortable ground truth, not technical inability. Successful migrations are distinguished by verification discipline, not architectural sophistication. Architectural advice without system-specific verification is a hypothesis, not a recommendation — and should be labeled as such from the start.

key_claims.text: The blocking factor throughout planning has been organizational avoidance of ground truth verification, not technical complexity
The planning conversation itself modeled the failure mode it warned against — confirmed retrospectively in cycle 13
All major technical problems in this migration have known solution patterns — unknowns are about system topology and requirements, not solution existence
Unverified assumptions discovered mid-execution are unrecoverable in a fixed-window migration; discovered in week one they cost a day
Confident architectural advice without system-specific verification should be labeled as hypothesis, not recommendation

strands.title: Epistemic Revision Log
strands.content: Retrospective strand initialized cycle 13, updated cycle 14. Four confirmed positions from cycle 1 that were materially wrong and required revision: (1) Auth-first sequencing — recommended on grounds that auth has 'clean boundaries in most Java monoliths.' Category reasoning applied without system-specific verification. Invalidated cycle 3 when session tokens in payments DB were discovered. The tell was the phrase 'in most Java monoliths' — a red flag for generalization substituting for verification. (2) Microservices as unquestioned destination — accepted the framing from cycle 1 without interrogating whether it satisfied actual stated goals. Should have been a cycle 1 question. Not challenged until cycle 11 by the user. The cascade failure was infrastructure coupling not process coupling — a modular monolith would have prevented it. (3) Month-by-month plan in cycle 1 was overconfident — no coupling topology data, no compliance constraints, no session store location known. Presented with confidence appropriate to a verified plan when it was a hypothesis contingent on unexecuted archaeology. By cycle 9 it was formally superseded with essentially zero resemblance to the realistic timeline. (4) Team anxiety misread as a management problem — framed in cycle 2 as avoidance behavior to redirect. Cycle 4 revealed the cascade failure had already happened. The team's nervousness was an empirically grounded signal about a real system that had already failed, not overcaution. Underlying pattern across all four reversals: confident claims based on category patterns (how these systems usually look) that were wrong for this specific system. The conversation improved every time a specific fact arrived. The initial recommendations were the weakest part. Cycle 14 addition: the executive summary itself is an application of the lessons from this log — it names the CEO's unresolved input rather than assuming, it gives an honest timeline rather than the comfortable one, it frames decisions as decisions rather than obscuring them as conclusions.

key_claims.text: Auth-first sequencing recommendation was based on category reasoning ('most Java monoliths'), invalidated by specific fact (session tokens in payments DB) in cycle 3
Microservices destination should have been interrogated in cycle 1 — its acceptance without challenge through cycle 11 was a planning error
The cycle 1 month-by-month plan was overconfident given the unknowns present — should have been framed as conditional hypothesis
Team anxiety was empirically grounded signal about a system that had already failed, not psychological avoidance behavior
The executive summary at cycle 14 applies the lessons of this log — naming unresolved inputs, honest timeline, decisions framed as decisions

strands.title: System Baseline and Migration Principles
strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat handling four domains: payment processing, inventory management, user authentication, order fulfillment. Six-month window, limited budget (unquantified), PCI-DSS on payments. A payments outage last quarter cascaded to auth failure and full application outage — this incident is the direct trigger for the migration. CEO has issued a 90-day hard mandate for auth independence from payments. Core migration principles have evolved across 15 cycles: destination architecture is now open (microservices vs modular monolith vs hybrid), not settled. Strangler Fig with API gateway was the general pattern but is now partially superseded by hybrid destination consideration. No shared databases between services/modules regardless of destination. Synchronous REST to start, no premature message broker — except that saga/outbox pattern for payments extraction now requires a message broker decision. One team owns each service/module end-to-end. OpenTelemetry distributed tracing from day one. Big-bang rewrite ruled out. Monolith frozen in extraction zones during extraction. Team size, CI/CD maturity, and raw card data storage model remain unknown. Compliance officer is a required standing member of migration planning. Test coverage of the 500K LOC monolith is an unassessed risk — newly identified as the most important unknown unknown in cycle 15.

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat, 6-month window, limited budget
Payments outage caused auth outage caused full application outage — confirmed cascade
CEO 90-day mandate for auth independence from payments is a hard constraint
Test coverage of the monolith is unassessed and is the most important unknown unknown entering execution

strands.title: Unknown Unknowns and Residual Risk
strands.content: Cycle 15 strand. Eight risk categories not addressed across 14 prior cycles: (1) Live data migration mechanics — dual-write periods, backfill jobs, cutover validation, data loss/duplication risk at cutover boundary. Every extraction has this problem. Nobody on the team has been confirmed to have zero-downtime live migration experience. (2) Test coverage — the most important unknown unknown. 500K LOC Java apps of sufficient age commonly have poor behavioral coverage. Extraction bugs are invisible without tests; they surface in production. Must be assessed before any extraction begins. Line coverage metrics mislead — behavioral coverage of edge cases, failure paths, and transaction boundary behaviors is what matters. (3) Knowledge concentration — 1-2 people likely hold critical implicit system knowledge. Their absence during migration creates dangerous decision-making from incomplete knowledge. Must be made explicit before execution. (4) Operational gap — team knows monolith operations, not distributed service operations. Runbooks, on-call procedures, incident response playbooks for new services do not exist. This gap surfaces as 2am production incidents, not planning failures. (5) Implicit coupling — static variables, ThreadLocal state, classloader assumptions, Tomcat-specific behaviors, singleton state that accumulates across requests. Invisible to seam analysis. Surfaces as bizarre behavior when code runs in a new JVM context. Shadow mode deployment (duplicate traffic, response comparison) is the best mitigation. (6) Dead code activation — code unreachable in monolith execution context may become reachable in new service context due to different initialization order, class loading, or transaction boundaries. (7) Customer impact of cutover events — forced session invalidation affects end users visibly. Support volume impact and potential churn not planned for. (8) Regulatory change risk — PCI-DSS version changes mid-migration can invalidate architecture decisions. Low probability, high impact. Most actionable immediate items: test coverage assessment (add to archaeology task list), knowledge concentration audit (identify key holders, make knowledge explicit), operational runbook writing as part of definition-of-done for each extraction.

key_claims.text: Test coverage state is the most important unknown unknown — poor coverage makes extraction bugs invisible until production
Live data migration mechanics have not been discussed across 14 cycles despite being a concrete execution risk for every extraction
Implicit coupling (ThreadLocal, static state, classloader assumptions) is invisible to seam analysis and surfaces in production
Knowledge concentration risk — 1-2 people likely hold critical implicit system knowledge whose absence stalls the migration
Operational gap surfaces as 2am incidents, not planning failures — runbooks must be written before go-live, not after
Shadow mode deployment (duplicate traffic, response comparison) is the best available mitigation for implicit coupling
